 45:   to split the linker into two in such a way that the latter half of
 46:   the process finishes as quickly as possible by speculatively parsing
 47:   and preprocessing input files in the first half of the process.
 48: 
 49: - One of the most computationally-intensive stage among linker stages
 50:   is symbol resolution. To resolve symbols, we basically have to throw
 51:   all symbol strings into a hash table to match undefined symbols with
 52:   defined symbols. But this can be done in the preloading stage using
 53:   [string interning](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/String_interning).
 54: 
 55: - Object files may contain a special section called a mergeable string
 56:   section. The section contains lots of null-terminated strings, and
 57:   the linker is expected to gather all mergeable string sections and
 58:   merge their contents. So, if two object files contain the same
 59:   string literal, for example, the resulting output will contain a
 60:   single merged string. This step is computationally intensive, but string
 61:   merging can be done in the preloading stage using string interning.
 62: 
 63: - Static archives (.a files) contain object files, but the static
 64:   archive's string table contains only defined symbols of member
 65:   object files and lacks other types of symbols. That makes static
 66:   archives unsuitable for speculative parsing. Therefore, the linker
 67:   should ignore the symbol table of static archive and directly read
 68:   static archive members.
 69: 
 70: - If there's a relocation that uses a GOT of a symbol, then we have to
 71:   create a GOT entry for that symbol. Otherwise, we shouldn't. That
 72:   means we need to scan all relocation tables to fix the length and
 73:   the contents of a .got section. This is computationally intensive,
 74:   but this step is parallelizable.
 75: 
 76: ### Linker Script
 77: 
 78: Linker script is an embedded language for the linker. It is mainly
 79: used to control how input sections are mapped to output sections and
 80: the layout of the output, but it can also do a lot of tricky stuff.
 81: Its feature is useful especially for embedded programming, but it's
 82: also an awfully underdocumented and complex language.
 83: 
 84: We have to implement a subset of the linker script language anwyay,
 85: because on Linux, /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so is (despite its
 86: name) not a shared object file but actually an ASCII file containing
 87: linker script code to load the _actual_ libc.so file. But the feature
 88: set for this purpose is very limited, and it is okay to implement them
 89: to mold.
 90: 
 91: Besides that, we really don't want to implement the linker script
 92: language. But at the same time, we want to satisfy the user needs that
 93: are currently satisfied with the linker script language. So, what
 94: should we do? Here is my observation:
 95: 
 96: - Linker script allows doing a lot of tricky stuff, such as specifying
 97:   the exact layout of a file, inserting arbitrary bytes between
 98:   sections, etc. But most of them can be done with a post-link binary
 99:   editing tool (such as `objcopy`).
100: 
101: - It looks like there are two things that truly cannot be done by a
102:   post-link editing tool: (a) mapping input sections to output
103:   sections, and (b) applying relocations.
104: 
105: From the above observation, I believe we need to provide only the
106: following features instead of the entire linker script language:
107: 
108: - A method to specify how input sections are mapped to output
109:   sections, and
110: 
111: - a method to set addresses to output sections, so that relocations
112:   are applied based on desired addresses.
113: 
114: I believe everything else can be done with a post-link binary editing
115: tool.
116: 
117: ### Details
118: 
119: - As we aim to the 1-second goal for Chromium, every millisecond
120:   counts. We can't ignore the latency of process exit. If we mmap a
121:   lot of files, \_exit(2) is not instantaneous but takes a few hundred
122:   milliseconds because the kernel has to clean up a lot of
123:   resources. As a workaround, we should organize the linker command as
124:   two processes; the first process forks the second process, and the
125:   second process does the actual work. As soon as the second process
126:   writes a result file to a filesystem, it notifies the first process,
127:   and the first process exits. The second process can take time to
128:   exit, because it is not an interactive process.
129: 
130: - At least on Linux, it looks like the filesystem's performance to
131:   allocate new blocks to a new file is the limiting factor when
132:   creating a new large file and filling its contents using mmap.
133:   If you already have a large file in the buffer cache, writing to it is
134:   much faster than creating a new fresh file and writing to it.
135:   Based on this observation, mold overwrites to an existing
136:   executable file if exists. My quick benchmark showed that I could
137:   save 300 milliseconds when creating a 2 GiB output file.
138:   Linux doesn't allow to open an executable for writing if it is
139:   running (you'll get a "text busy" error if you attempt). mold
140:   falls back to the usual way if it fails to open an output file.
141: 
142: - The output from the linker should be deterministic for the sake of
143:   [build reproducibility](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reproducible_builds)
144:   and ease of debugging. This might add a little bit of overhead to
145:   the linker, but that shouldn't be too much.
146: 
147: - A .build-id, a unique ID embedded to an output file, is usually
148:   computed by applying a cryptographic hash function (e.g. SHA-1) to
149:   an output file. This is a slow step, but we can speed it up by
150:   splitting a file into small chunks, computing SHA-1 for each chunk,
151:   and then computing SHA-1 of the concatenated SHA-1 hashes
152:   (i.e. constructing a [Merkle
153:   Tree](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Merkle_tree) of height 2).
154:   Modern x86 processors have purpose-built instructions for SHA-1 and
155:   can compute SHA-1 pretty quickly at about 2 GiB/s. Using 16
156:   cores, a build-id for a 2 GiB executable can be computed in 60 to 70
157:   milliseconds.
158: 
159: - BFD, gold, and lld support section garbage collection. That is, a
160:   linker runs a mark-sweep garbage collection on an input graph, where
161:   sections are vertices and relocations are edges, to discard all
162:   sections that are not reachable from the entry point symbol
163:   (i.e. `_start`) or a few other root sections. In mold, we are using
164:   multiple threads to mark sections concurrently.
165: 
166: - Similarly, BFD, gold an lld support Identical Comdat Folding (ICF)
167:   as yet another size optimization. ICF merges two or more read-only
168:   sections that happen to have the same contents and relocations.
169:   To do that, we have to find isomorphic subgraphs from larger graphs.
170:   I implemented a new algorithm for mold, which is 5x faster than lld
171:   to do ICF for Chromium (from 5 seconds to 1 second).
172: 
173: - [Intel Threading Building
174:   Blocks](https://github.com/oneapi-src/oneTBB) (TBB) is a good
175:   library for parallel execution and has several concurrent
176:   containers. We are particularly interested in using
177:   `parallel_for_each` and `concurrent_hash_map`.
178: 
179: - TBB provides `tbbmalloc` which works better for multi-threaded
180:   applications than the glib'c malloc, but it looks like
181:   [jemalloc](https://github.com/jemalloc/jemalloc) and
182:   [mimalloc](https://github.com/microsoft/mimalloc) are a little bit
183:   more scalable than `tbbmalloc`.
184: 
185: ### Size of the problem
186: 
187: When linking Chrome, a linker reads 3,430,966,844 bytes of data in
188: total. The data contains the following items:
189: 
190: | Data item                | Number
191: | ------------------------ | ------
192: | Object files             | 30,723
193: | Public undefined symbols | 1,428,149
194: | Mergeable strings        | 1,579,996
195: | Comdat groups            | 9,914,510
196: | Regular sections¹        | 10,345,314
197: | Public defined symbols   | 10,512,135
198: | Symbols                  | 23,953,607
199: | Sections                 | 27,543,225
200: | Relocations against SHF_ALLOC sections | 39,496,375
201: | Relocations              | 62,024,719
202: 
203: ¹ Sections that have to be copied from input object files to an
204: output file. Sections that contain relocations or symbols are for
205: example excluded.
206: 
207: ### Internals
208: 
209: In this section, I'll explain the internals of mold linker.
210: 
211: #### A brief history of Unix and the Unix linker
212: 
213: Conceptually, what a linker does is pretty simple. A compiler compiles
214: a fragment of a program (a single source file) into a fragment of
215: machine code and data (an object file, which typically has the .o
216: extension), and a linker stitches them together into a single
217: executable or a shared library image.
218: 
219: In reality, modern linkers for Unix-like systems are much more
220: complicated than the naive understanding because they have gradually
221: gained one feature at a time over the 50 years history of Unix, and
222: they are now something like a bag of lots of miscellaneous features in
223: which none of the features is more important than the others. It is
224: very easy to miss the forest for the trees, since for those who don't
225: know the details of the Unix linker, it is not clear which feature is
226: essential and which is not.
227: 
228: That being said, one thing is clear that at any point of Unix history,
229: a Unix linker has a coherent feature set for the Unix of that age. So,
230: let me entangle the history to see how the operating system, runtime,
231: and linker have gained features that we see today. That should give
232: you an idea of why a particular feature has been added to a linker in the
233: first place.
234: 
235: 1. Original Unix didn't support shared libraries, and a program was
236:    always loaded to a fixed address. An executable was something like
237:    a memory dump that was just loaded to a particular address by the
238:    kernel. After loading, the kernel started executing the program by
239:    setting the instruction pointer to a particular address.
240: 
241:    The most essential feature for any linker is relocation processing.
242:    The original Unix linker of course supported that. Let me explain
243:    what that is.
244: 
245:    Individual object files are inevitably incomplete as a program,
246:    because when a compiler created them, it only see a part of an
247:    entire program. For example, if an object file contains a function
248:    call that refers to another object file, the `call` instruction in the
249:    object cannot be complete, as the compiler has no idea as to what
250:    is the called function's address. To deal with this, the compiler
251:    emits a placeholder value (typically just zero) instead of a real
252:    address and leaves metadata in an object file saying "fix offset X
253:    of this file with an address of Y". That metadata is called
254:    "relocation". Relocations are typically processed by the linker.
255: 
256:    It is easy for a linker to apply relocations for the original Unix
257:    because a program is always loaded to a fixed address. It exactly
258:    knows the addresses of all functions and data when linking a
259:    program.
260: 
261:    Static library support, which is still an important feature of Unix
262:    linker, also dates back to this early period of Unix history.
263:    To understand what it is, imagine that you are trying to compile
264:    a program for the early Unix. You don't want to waste time to
265:    compile libc functions every time you compile your program (the
266:    computers of the era were incredibly slow), so you have already
267:    placed each libc function into a separate source file and compiled
268:    them individually. That means you have object files for each libc
269:    function, e.g., printf.o, scanf.o, atoi.o, write.o, etc.
270: 
271:    Given this configuration, all you have to do to link your program
272:    against libc functions is to pick up the right set of libc object
273:    files and give them to the linker along with the object files of your
274:    program. But, keeping the linker command line in sync with the
275:    libc functions you are using in your program is bothersome. You can
276:    be conservative; you can specify all libc object files to the
277:    command line, but that leads to program bloat because the linker
278:    unconditionally link all object files given to it no matter whether
279:    they are used or not. So, a new feature was added to the linker to
280:    fix the problem. That is the static library, which is also called
281:    the archive file.
282: 
283:    An archive file is just a bundle of object files, just like zip
284:    file but in an uncompressed form. An archive file typically has the
285:    .a file extension and named after its contents. For example, the
286:    archive file containing all libc objects is named `libc.a`.
287: 
288:    If you pass an archive file along with other object files to the
289:    linker, the linker pulls out an object file from the archive _only
290:    when_ it is referenced by other object files. In other words,
291:    unlike object files directly given to a linker, object files
292:    wrapped in an archive are not linked to the output by default.
293:    An archive works as a supplement to complete your program.
294: 
295:    Even today, you can still find a libc archive file. Run `ar t
296:    /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.a` on Linux should give you a list
297:    of object files in the libc archive.
298: 
299: 2. In the '80s, Sun Microsystems, a leading commercial Unix vendor at the
300:    time, added shared library support to their Unix variant, SunOS.
301: 
302: (This section is incomplete.)
303: 
304: ### Concurrency strategy
305: 
306: In this section, I'll explain the high-level concurrency strategy of
307: mold.
308: 
309: In most places, mold adopts data parallelism. That is, we have a huge
310: number of pieces of data of the same kind, and we process each of them
311: individually using parallel for-loop. For example, after identifying
312: the exact set of input object files, we need to scan all relocation
313: tables to determine the sizes of .got and .plt sections. We do that
314: using a parallel for-loop. The granularity of parallel processing in
315: this case is the relocation table.
316: 
317: Data parallelism is very efficient and scalable because there's no
318: need for threads to communicate with each other while working on each
319: element of data. In addition to that, data parallelism is easy to
320: understand, as it is just a for-loop in which multiple iterations may
321: be executed in parallel. We don't use high-level communication or
322: synchronization mechanisms such as channels, futures, promises,
323: latches or something like that in mold.
324: 
325: In some cases, we need to share a little bit of data between threads
326: while executing a parallel for-loop. For example, the loop to scan
327: relocations turns on "requires GOT" or "requires PLT" flags in a
328: symbol. Symbol is a shared resource, and writing to them from multiple
329: threads without synchronization is unsafe. To deal with it, we made
330: the flag an atomic variable.
331: 
332: The other common pattern you can find in mold which is build on top of
333: the parallel for-loop is the map-reduce pattern. That is, we run a
334: parallel for-loop on a large data set to produce a small data set and
335: process the small data set with a single thread. Let me take a
336: build-id computation as an example. Build-id is typically computed by
337: applying a cryptographic hash function such as SHA-1 on a linker's
338: output file. To compute it, we first consider an output as a sequence
339: of 1 MiB blocks and compute a SHA-1 hash for each block in parallel.
340: Then, we concatenate the SHA-1 hashes and compute a SHA-1 hash on the
341: hashes to get a final build-id.
342: 
343: Finally, we use concurrent hashmap at a few places in mold. Concurrent
344: hashmap is a hashmap to which multiple threads can safely insert items
345: in parallel. We use it in the symbol resolution stage, for example.
346: To resolve symbols, we basically have to throw in all defined symbols
347: into a hash table, so that we can find a matching defined symbol for
348: an undefined symbol by name. We do the hash table insertion from a
349: parallel for-loop which iterates over a list of input files.
350: 
351: Overall, even though mold is highly scalable, it succeeded to avoid
352: complexties you often find in complex parallel programs. From high
353: level, mold just serially executes the linker's internal passes one by
354: one. Each pass is parallelized using parallel for-loops.
355: 
356: ### Rejected ideas
357: 
358: In this section, I'll explain the alternative designs I currently do
359: not plan to implement and why I turned them down.
360: 
361: - Placing variable-length sections at end of an output file and start
362:   copying file contents before fixing the output file layout
363: 
364:   Idea: Fixing the layout of regular sections seems easy, and if we
365:   place them at beginning of a file, we can start copying their
366:   contents from their input files to an output file. While copying
367:   file contents, we can compute the sizes of variable-length sections
368:   such as .got or .plt and place them at end of the file.
369: 
370:   Reason for rejection: I did not choose this design because I doubt
371:   if it could actually shorten link time and I think I don't need it
372:   anyway.
373: 
374:   The linker has to de-duplicate comdat sections (i.e. inline
375:   functions that are included in multiple object files), so we
376:   cannot compute the layout of regular sections until we resolve all
377:   symbols and de-duplicate comdats. That takes a few hundred
378:   milliseconds. After that, we can compute the sizes of
379:   variable-length sections in less than 100 milliseconds. It's quite
380:   fast, so it doesn't seem to make much sense to proceed without
381:   fixing the final file layout.
382: 
383:   The other reason to reject this idea is because there's good a
384:   chance for this idea to have a negative impact on linker's overall
385:   performance. If we copy file contents before fixing the layout, we
386:   can't apply relocations to them while copying because symbol
387:   addresses are not available yet. If we fix the file layout first, we
388:   can apply relocations while copying, which is effectively zero-cost
389:   due to a very good data locality. On the other hand, if we apply
390:   relocations long after we copy file contents, it's pretty expensive
391:   because section contents are very likely to have been evicted from
392:   CPU cache.
393: 
394: - Incremental linking
395: 
396:   Idea: Incremental linking is a technique to patch a previous linker's
397:   output file so that only functions or data that are updated from the
398:   previous build are written to it. It is expected to significantly
399:   reduce the amount of data copied from input files to an output file
400:   and thus speed up linking. GNU BFD and gold linkers support it.
401: 
402:   Reason for rejection: I turned it down because it (1) is
403:   complicated, (2) doesn't seem to speed it up that much and (3) has
404:   several practical issues. Let me explain each of them.
405: 
406:   First, incremental linking for real C/C++ programs is not as easy as
407:   one might think. Let me take malloc as an example. malloc is usually
408:   defined by libc, but you can implement it in your program, and if
409:   that's the case, the symbol `malloc` will be resolved to your
410:   function instead of the one in libc. If you include a library that
411:   defines malloc (such as libjemalloc or libtbbmallc) before libc,
412:   their malloc will override libc's malloc.
413: 
414:   Assume that you are using a nonstandard malloc. What if you remove
415:   your malloc from your code, or remove `-ljemalloc` from your
416:   Makefile? The linker has to include a malloc from libc, which may
417:   include more object files to satisfy its dependencies. Such code
418:   change can affect the entire program rather than just replacing one
419:   function. The same is true for adding malloc to your program. Making
420:   a local change doesn't necessarily result in a local change in the
421:   binary level.  It can easily have cascading effects.
422: 
423:   Some ELF fancy features make incremental linking even harder to
424:   implement. Take the weak symbol as an example. If you define `atoi`
425:   as a weak symbol in your program, and if you are not using `atoi`
426:   at all in your program, that symbol will be resolved to address
427:   0. But if you start using some libc function that indirectly calls
428:   `atoi`, then `atoi` will be included in your program, and your weak
429:   symbol will be resolved to that function. I don't know how to
430:   efficiently fix up a binary for this case.
431: 
432:   This is a hard problem, so existing linkers don't try too hard to
433:   solve it. For example, IIRC, gold falls back to full link if any
434:   function is removed from a previous build. If you want to not annoy
435:   users in the fallback case, you need to make full link fast anyway.
436: 
437:   Second, incremental linking itself has an overhead. It has to detect
438:   updated files, patch an existing output file and write additional
439:   data to an output file for future incremental linking. GNU gold, for
440:   instance, takes almost 30 seconds on my machine to do a null
441:   incremental link (i.e. no object files are updated from a previous
442:   build) for chrome. It's just too slow.
443: 
444:   Third, there are other practical issues in incremental linking. It's
445:   not reproducible, so your binary isn't going to be the same as other
446:   binaries even if you are compiling the same source tree using the
447:   same compiler toolchain. Or, it is complex and there might be a bug
448:   in it. If something doesn't work correctly, "remove --incremental
449:   from your Makefile and try again" could be a piece of advice, but
450:   that isn't ideal.
451: 
452:   So, all in all, incremental linking is tricky. I wanted to make full
453:   link as fast as possible, so that we don't have to think about how
454:   to work around the slowness of full link.
455: 
456: - Defining a completely new file format and use it
457: 
458:   Idea: Sometimes, the ELF file format itself seems to be a limiting
459:   factor in improving the linker's performance. We might be able to make a
460:   far better one if we create a new file format.
461: 
462:   Reason for rejection: I rejected the idea because it apparently has
463:   a practical issue (backward compatibility issue) and also doesn't
464:   seem to improve the performance of linkers that much. As clearly
465:   demonstrated by mold, we can create a fast linker for ELF. I believe
466:   ELF isn't that bad, after all. The semantics of the existing Unix
467:   linkers, such as the name resolution algorithm or the linker script,
468:   have slowed the linkers down, but that's not a problem of the file
469:   format itself.
470: 
471: - Watching object files using inotify(2)
472: 
473:   Idea: When mold is running as a daemon for preloading, use
474:   inotify(2) to watch file system updates so that it can reload files
475:   as soon as they are updated.
476: 
477:   Reason for rejection: Just like the maximum number of files you can
478:   simultaneously open, the maximum number of files you can watch using
479:   inotify(2) isn't that large. Maybe just a single instance of mold is
480:   fine with inotify(2), but it may fail if you run multiple of it.
481: 
482:   The other reason for not doing it is because mold is quite fast
483:   without it anyway. Invoking stat(2) on each file for file update
484:   check takes less than 100 milliseconds for Chrome, and if most of
485:   the input files are not updated, parsing updated files takes almost
486:   no time.

================
File: docs/execstack.md
================
 1: This page explains the following warning message and how to fix it.
 2: mold emits this message when it sees an object file that may not be
 3: compatible with mold.
 4: 
 5: 
 6: ```
 7: mold: warning: foo.o: this file may cause a segmentation fault because it requires an executable stack. See https://github.com/rui314/mold/tree/main/docs/execstack.md for more info.
 8: ```
 9: 
10: # Background
11: 
12: On modern computers, the stack area (to which local variables are
13: stored) cannot contain executable code. If the control reaches the
14: stack area, the CPU refuses to execute any code there and the program
15: is usually terminated due to segmentation fault.
16: 
17: This is a security measure. The stack area used to be executable (old
18: CPUs generally execute any code as long as it is in a readable memory
19: region), but that provided an easy attack vector to a malicious user.
20: They wrote executable code to the stack area using some buffer
21: overflow bug and jumped there to run arbitrary code in a remote server
22: process.
23: 
24: To prevent this type of attack, the stack area is no longer executable
25: since the early 2000s. On Linux, the stack's executable-ness is
26: controlled by a bit in an executable, and the loader respects that
27: bit. The bit is set by the linker.
28: 
29: GCC had (and still has) a feature that depends on the executable
30: stack, so they invented a way to tell the linker to mark the stack
31: executable. Specifically, if an object file contains a
32: `.note.GNU-stack` section with the `SHF_EXECSTACK` bit, GNU linker
33: silently makes the stack of an output file executable.
34: 
35: But the GNU linker's behavior is dangerous. If you accidentally link
36: an object file that has that marker section, the entire stack area
37: silently becomes executable, disabling the security mechanism.
38: 
39: Therefore, mold simply ignores that marker section. If you are using
40: mold, you need to explicitly pass `-z execstack` to the linker to make
41: the stack executable.
42: 
43: # What caused this issue?
44: 
45: You are likely to use GCC's [Nested
46: Functions](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Nested-Functions.html)
47: feature which still depends on the executable stack.
48: 
49: # How to fix it?
50: 
51: If you know what you are doing, pass `-z execstack` to mold. Beware
52: that this will significantly weaken your program's security.
53: 
54: If you don't want to pass `-z execstack`, rewrite your code so that
55: your code does not depend on the executable stack.

================
File: docs/glossary.md
================
  1: The very concept of linking is simple: a compiler compiles a piece of
  2: source code into an object file (a file containing machine code), and
  3: a linker combines object files into a single executable or a shared
  4: library file. However, the actual implementation of the linker for
  5: modern systems is much more complicated because hardware, operating
  6: system, compiler and linker all have many more features.
  7: 
  8: In this file, I'll explain random topics in the glossary format that
  9: you need to understand to read mold code.
 10: 
 11: ## DSO
 12: 
 13: A .so file. Short for Dynamic Shared Object. Often called as a
 14: shared library, a dynamic libray or a shared object as well.
 15: 
 16: An DSO contains common functions and data that are used by multiple
 17: executables and/or other DSOs. At runtime, a DSO is loaded to a
 18: contiguous region in the virtual address.
 19: 
 20: ## Object file
 21: 
 22: A .o file. An object file contains machine code and data, but it
 23: cannot be executed because it's not self-contained. For example,
 24: if you compile a C source file containing a call of `printf`,
 25: the actual function code of `printf` is not included in the resulting
 26: object file. You include `stdio.h`, but that teaches the compiler
 27: only about `printf`'s type, and the compiler still don't know what
 28: `printf` actually does. Therefore, it cannot emit code for `printf`.
 29: 
 30: You need to link an object file with other object file or a shared
 31: library to make it exectuable.
 32: 
 33: ## Virtual address space
 34: 
 35: A pointer has a value like 0x803020 which is an address of the
 36: pointee. But it doesn't mean that the pointee resides at the
 37: physical memory address 0x803020 on the computer. Modern CPUs
 38: contains so-called Mmeory Management Unit (MMU), and all access to
 39: the memory are first translated by MMU to the physical address.
 40: The address before translation is called the "virtual address".
 41: Unless you are doing the kernel programming, all addresses you
 42: handle are virtual addresses.
 43: 
 44: The OS kernel controls the MMU so that each process owns the entire
 45: virtual address space. So, even if two process uses the same virtual
 46: address, they don't conflict. They are mapped to different physical
 47: addresses.
 48: 
 49: The existence of MMU has several implications to the linker. First,
 50: we can link the main executable to a specific address. On process
 51: startup, there's no code or data in the virtual address space, so
 52: the mapping of the main executable always succeed. However, it's not
 53: true to DSOs because they are loaded after the main executable and
 54: possibly other DSOs. Therefore, shared libraries must be linked in a
 55: way that they can be loaded to any address in the virtual address
 56: space.
 57: 
 58: ## Relocation
 59: 
 60: A piece of information for the linker as to how to link object files
 61: or a dynamic objects.
 62: 
 63: Object files can refer functions or data in other object files. For
 64: example, if you compile a function which calls a non-local function
 65: `foo`, the resulting code contains something like this:
 66: 
 67: ```
 68:   26:   e8 00 00 00 00          callq  2b <bar+0xb>
 69:                         27: R_X86_64_PLT32      foo-0x4
 70: ```
 71: 
 72: The above `callq` is the instruction to call a function at the
 73: machine code level. It's opcode is `0xe8` in x86-64, so the
 74: instruction begins with `0xe8`. The following four bytes are
 75: displacement; that is, the address of the branch target relative to
 76: the end of this `callq` instruction. Notice that the displacement is
 77: 0. The compiler couldn't fill the displacement because it has no
 78: idea as to where `foo` will be at runtime. So, the compiler write 0
 79: as a placeholder and instead write a relocation `R_X86_64_PLT32`
 80: with `foo` as its associated symbol. The linker reads this
 81: relocation, computes the offsets between this call instruction and
 82: function `foo` and overwrite the placeholder value 0 with an actual
 83: displacement.
 84: 
 85: There are many different types of relocations. For example, if you
 86: want to fix up not with a displacement but with an absolute address
 87: of a symbol, you need to use `R_X86_64_ABS64` instead.
 88: 
 89: ## Static library
 90: 
 91: A .a file. Often called as an archive file or just archive as well.
 92: 
 93: A static library is a container just like tar or zip. Actually,
 94: there's no technical reason to not use tar or (uncompressed) zip,
 95: but traditionally the .a file format is used by the linker.
 96: 
 97: A static library contains object files and can be passed to the
 98: linker along with other object files and/or archives.
 99: 
100: A linker pulls out object files from an archive only if it is needed
101: to resolve undefined symbols. In other words, object files in an
102: archive are not linked by default and used as a complement to supply
103: missing definitions. This is ideal for a library because you don't
104: want to link library functions unless you are actually using them.
105: 
106: Contrary to archive files, object files directly given to a linker
107: are always linked to the output.
108: 
109: To maximize the benefit of archive files, a library often used as a
110: static library is broken down to small files to separate each
111: function individually (for example, look at
112: https://git.musl-libc.org/cgit/musl/tree/src/stdio). By doing this,
113: you import only used functions.
114: 
115: A static file is created by `ar`, whose command line arguments are
116: similar to `tar`. A static library contains the symbol table which
117: offers a quick way to look up an object file for a defined symbol,
118: but mold does not use the static library's symbol table. mold
119: doesdn't need a symbol table to exist in an archive, and if exists,
120: mold just ignores it.
121: 
122: See also: DSO (dynamic library)
123: 
124: ## Symbol
125: 
126: A symbol is a label assigned to a specific location in an input file
127: or an output file. For example, if you define function `foo` and
128: compile it, the resulting object file contains a symbol `foo`
129: pointing to the beginning of the machine code for `foo`.
130: 
131: Usually, a symbol name is a function or a variable name. If an
132: object is anonymous (such the one for a string literal), a compiler
133: generated a unique symbol, which often starts with `.` to avoid
134: conflict with user-defined symbols.
135: 
136: For C++, symbol name is a complex "mangled" name. We need to mangle
137: identifiers because a simple name such as `foo` cannot be uniquely
138: identify a function or a data in C++, because for example `foo` may
139: be in a namespace or defined as a static member in some class. If
140: `foo` is an overloaded function, we need to distinguish different
141: `foo`s by its type. Therefore, C++ compiler mangles an identifier by
142: appending nmaepsace names, type information and such so that
143: different things get different names.
144: 
145: For example, a function `int foo(int)` in a namespace `bar` is
146: mangled as `_ZN3bar3fooEi`.
147: 
148: A symbol can be either defined or undefined. A defined symbol points
149: to some location in a file which may contain the function's machine
150: code or the variable's initial value. An undefined symbol does not
151: point to anywhere. It needs to be merged with a defined symbol with
152: the same name at link-time. This merging process is called "name
153: resolution".
154: 
155: For example, if your program is using `printf`, it usually contains
156: `printf` as an undefined symbol. You need to link it with `libc.a`
157: or `libc.so`, which contain a defined symbol of `printf`, to make a
158: complete program.

================
File: docs/mold.1
================
  1: .\" generated with Ronn-NG/v0.9.1
  2: .\" http://github.com/apjanke/ronn-ng/tree/0.9.1
  3: .TH "MOLD" "1" "February 2023" ""
  4: .SH "NAME"
  5: \fBmold\fR \- a modern linker
  6: .SH "SYNOPSIS"
  7: \fBmold\fR [\fIoption\fR\|\.\|\.\|\.] \fIfile\fR\|\.\|\.\|\.
  8: .SH "DESCRIPTION"
  9: \fBmold\fR is a faster drop\-in replacement for the default GNU ld(1)\.
 10: .SS "How to use"
 11: See \fIhttps://github\.com/rui314/mold#how\-to\-use\fR\.
 12: .SS "Compatibility"
 13: \fBMold\fR is designed to be a drop\-in replacement for the GNU linkers for linking user\-land programs\. If your user\-land program cannot be built due to missing command\-line options, please file a bug at \fIhttps://github\.com/rui314/mold/issues\fR\.
 14: .P
 15: Mold supports a very limited set of linker script features, which is just sufficient to read \fB/usr/lib/x86_64\-linux\-gnu/libc\.so\fR on Linux systems (on Linux, that file is despite its name not a shared library but an ASCII linker script that loads a real \fBlibc\.so\fR file\.)
 16: .P
 17: Beyond that, we have no plan to support any linker script features\. The linker script is an ad\-hoc, over\-designed, complex language which we believe needs to be disrupted by a simpler mechanism\. We have a plan to add a replacement for the linker script to \fBmold\fR instead\.
 18: .SS "Archive symbol resolution"
 19: Traditionally, Unix linkers are sensitive to the order in which input files appear on command line\. They process input files from the first (leftmost) file to the last (rightmost) file one\-by\-one\. While reading input files, they maintain sets of defined and undefined symbols\. When visiting an archive file (\fB\.a\fR files), they pull out object files to resolve as many undefined symbols as possible and go on to the next input file\. Object files that weren't pulled out will never have a chance for a second look\.
 20: .P
 21: Due to this semantics, you usually have to add archive files at the end of a command line, so that when a linker reaches archive files, it knows what symbols remain as undefined\.
 22: .P
 23: If you put archive files at the beginning of a command line, a linker doesn't have any undefined symbol, and thus no object files will be pulled out from archives\. You can change the processing order by \fB\-\-start\-group\fR and \fB\-\-end\-group\fR options, though they make a linker slower\.
 24: .P
 25: \fBmold\fR as well as LLVM lld(1) linker take a different approach\. They memorize what symbols can be resolved from archive files instead of forgetting it after processing each archive\. Therefore, \fBmold\fR and lld(1) can "go back" in a command line to pull out object files from archives if they are needed to resolve remaining undefined symbols\. They are not sensitive to the input file order\.
 26: .P
 27: \fB\-\-start\-group\fR and \fB\-\-end\-group\fR are still accepted by \fBmold\fR and lld(1) for compatibility with traditional linkers, but they are silently ignored\.
 28: .SS "Dynamic symbol resolution"
 29: Some Unix linker features are unable to be understood without understanding the semantics of dynamic symbol resolution\. Therefore, even though that's not specific to \fBmold\fR, we'll explain it here\.
 30: .P
 31: We use "ELF module" or just "module" as a collective term to refer to an executable or a shared library file in the ELF format\.
 32: .P
 33: An ELF module may have lists of imported symbols and exported symbols, as well as a list of shared library names from which imported symbols should be imported\. The point is that imported symbols are not bound to any specific shared library until runtime\.
 34: .P
 35: Here is how the Unix dynamic linker resolves dynamic symbols\. Upon the start of an ELF program, the dynamic linker construct a list of ELF modules which as a whole consist of a complete program\. The executable file is always at the beginning of the list followed by its depending shared libraries\. An imported symbol is searched from the beginning of the list to the end\. If two or more modules define the same symbol, the one that appears first in the list takes precedence over the others\.
 36: .P
 37: This Unix semantics are contrary to systems such as Windows that have the two\-level namespace for dynamic symbols\. On Windows, for example, dynamic symbols are represented as a tuple of (\fBsymbol\-name\fR, \fBshared\-library\-name\fR), so that each dynamic symbol is guaranteed to be resolved from some specific library\.
 38: .P
 39: Typically, an ELF module that exports a symbol also imports the same symbol\. Such a symbol is usually resolved to itself, but that's not the case if a module that appears before in the symbol search list provides another definition of the same symbol\.
 40: .P
 41: Let me take \fBmalloc\fR as an example\. Assume that you define your version of \fBmalloc\fR in your main executable file\. Then, all \fBmalloc\fR calls from any module are resolved to your function instead of that in libc, because the executable is always at the beginning of the dynamic symbol search list\. Note that even \fBmalloc\fR calls within libc are resolved to your definition since libc exports and imports \fBmalloc\fR\. Therefore, by defining \fBmalloc\fR yourself, you can overwrite a library function, and the \fBmalloc\fR in libc becomes dead code\.
 42: .P
 43: These Unix semantics are tricky and sometimes considered harmful\. For example, assume that you accidentally define \fBatoi\fR as a global function in your executable that behaves completely differently from the one in the C standard\. Then, all \fBatoi\fR function calls from any modules (even function calls within libc) are redirected to your function instead of the one in libc which will very likely to cause a problem\.
 44: .P
 45: That is a somewhat surprising consequence for an accidental name conflict\. On the other hand, this semantic is sometimes useful because it allows users to override library functions without rebuilding modules containing them\.
 46: .P
 47: Whether good or bad, you should keep this semantics in mind to understand the Unix linkers behaviors\.
 48: .SS "Build reproducibility"
 49: \fBmold\fR's output is deterministic\. That is, if you pass the same object files and the same command\-line options to the same version of \fBmold\fR, it is guaranteed that \fBmold\fR produces the bit\-by\-bit identical same output\. The linker's internal randomness, such as the timing of thread scheduling or iteration orders of hash tables, doesn't affect the output\.
 50: .P
 51: \fBmold\fR does not have any host\-specific default settings\. This is contrary to the GNU linkers to which some configurable values, such as system\-dependent library search paths, are hard\-coded\. \fBmold\fR depends only on its command\-line arguments\.
 52: .SH "MOLD\-SPECIFIC OPTIONS"
 53: .TP
 54: \fB\-\-chroot\fR=\fIdir\fR
 55: Set \fIdir\fR to root directory\.
 56: .TP
 57: \fB\-\-color\-diagnostics\fR=[ \fIauto\fR | \fIalways\fR | \fInever\fR ]
 58: Show diagnostics messages in color using ANSI escape sequences\. \fBauto\fR means that \fBmold\fR prints out messages in color only if the standard output is connected to a TTY\. Default is \fBauto\fR\.
 59: .TP
 60: \fB\-\-color\-diagnostics\fR
 61: Synonym for \fB\-\-color\-diagnostics=auto\fR\.
 62: .TP
 63: \fB\-\-no\-color\-diagnostics\fR
 64: Synonym for \fB\-\-color\-diagnostics=never\fR\.
 65: .TP
 66: \fB\-\-fork\fR, \fB\-\-no\-fork\fR
 67: Spawn a child process and let it do the actual linking\. When linking a large program, the OS kernel can take a few hundred milliseconds to terminate a \fBmold\fR process\. \fB\-\-fork\fR hides that latency\. By default, it does fork\.
 68: .TP
 69: \fB\-\-perf\fR
 70: Print performance statistics\.
 71: .TP
 72: \fB\-\-print\-dependencies\fR
 73: Print out dependency information for input files\.
 74: .IP
 75: Each line of the output for this option shows that which file depends on which file to use which symbol\. This option is useful to debug why some object file in a static archive got linked or why some shared library is kept in an output file's dependency list even with \fB\-\-as\-needed\fR\.
 76: .TP
 77: \fB\-\-repro\fR
 78: Archive input files as well as a text file containing command line options as a tar file so that you can run \fBmold\fR with the exact same inputs again\. This is useful to report a bug with a reproducer\. The output filename is \fBpath/to/output\.tar\fR where \fBpath/to/output\fR is an output filename specified by \fB\-o\fR\.
 79: .TP
 80: \fB\-\-reverse\-sections\fR
 81: Reverses the order of input sections before assigning them the offsets in the output file\.
 82: .IP
 83: This option is useful for finding bugs that depend on an initialization order of global objects\. In C++, constructors of global objects in a single source file are guaranteed to be executed in the source order, but there's no such guarantee across compilation units\. Usually, constructors are executed in the order given to the linker, but depending on it is a mistake\.
 84: .IP
 85: By reversing the order of input sections using \fB\-\-reverse\-sections\fR, you can easily test that your program works in the reversed initialization order\.
 86: .TP
 87: \fB\-\-run\fR \fIcommand\fR \fIarg\fR\|\.\|\.\|\.
 88: Run \fBcommand\fR with \fBmold\fR \fB/usr/bin/ld\fR\. Specifically, \fBmold\fR runs a given command with the \fBLD_PRELOAD\fR environment set to intercept exec(3) family functions and replaces \fBargv[0]\fR with itself if it is \fBld\fR, \fBld\.gold\fR or \fBld\.lld\fR\.
 89: .TP
 90: \fB\-\-shuffle\-sections\fR, \fB\-\-shuffle\-sections\fR=\fInumber\fR
 91: Randomizes the output by shuffling the order of input sections before assigning them the offsets in the output file\. If \fInumber\fR is given, it's used as a seed for the random number generator, so that the linker produces the same output for the same seed\. If no seed is given, a random number is used as a seed\.
 92: .IP
 93: This option is useful for benchmarking\. Modern CPUs are sensitive to program's memory layout\. A seeming benign change in program layout such as a small size increase of a function in the middle of a program can affect program's performance\. Therefore, even if you write new code and get a good benchmark result, it is hard to say whether or not the new code improves the programs performance; it is possible that the new memory layout happens to perform better\.
 94: .IP
 95: By running a benchmark multiple time with randomized memory layouts using \fB\-\-shuffle\-sections\fR, you can isolate your program's real performance number from the randomness caused by memory layout changes\.
 96: .TP
 97: \fB\-\-stats\fR
 98: Print input statistics\.
 99: .TP
100: \fB\-\-thread\-count\fR=\fIcount\fR
101: Use \fIcount\fR number of threads\.
102: .TP
103: \fB\-\-threads\fR, \fB\-\-no\-threads\fR
104: Use multiple threads\. By default, \fBmold\fR uses as many threads as the number of cores or 32, whichever is the smallest\. The reason why it is capped to 32 is because \fBmold\fR doesn't scale well beyond that point\. To use only one thread, pass \fB\-no\-threads\fR or \fB\-thread\-count=1\fR\.
105: .TP
106: \fB\-\-quick\-exit\fR, \fB\-\-no\-quick\-exit\fR
107: Use or do not use \fBquick_exit\fR to exit\.
108: .SH "GNU\-COMPATIBLE OPTIONS"
109: .TP
110: \fB\-\-help\fR
111: Report usage information to stdout and exit\.
112: .TP
113: \fB\-v\fR, \fB\-\-version\fR
114: Report version information to stdout\.
115: .TP
116: \fB\-V\fR
117: Report version and target information to stdout\.
118: .TP
119: \fB\-E\fR, \fB\-\-export\-dynamic\fR, \fB\-\-no\-export\-dynamic\fR
120: When creating an executable, using the \fB\-E\fR option causes all global symbols to be put into the dynamic symbol table, so that the symbols are visible from other ELF modules at runtime\.
121: .IP
122: By default, or if \fB\-\-no\-export\-dynamic\fR is given, only symbols that are referenced by DSOs at link\-time are exported from an executable\.
123: .TP
124: \fB\-F\fR \fIlibname\fR, \fB\-\-filter\fR=\fIlibname\fR
125: Set the \fBDT_FILTER\fR dynamic section field to \fIlibname\fR\.
126: .TP
127: \fB\-I\fR \fIfile\fR, \fB\-\-dynamic\-linker\fR=\fIfile\fR, \fB\-\-no\-dynamic\-linker\fR
128: Set the dynamic linker path to \fIfile\fR\. If no \fB\-I\fR option is given, or if \fB\-\-no\-dynamic\-linker\fR is given, no dynamic linker path is set to an output file\. This is contrary to the GNU linkers which sets a default dynamic linker path in that case\. This difference doesn't usually make any difference because the compiler driver always passes \fB\-I\fR to the linker\.
129: .TP
130: \fB\-L\fR \fIdir\fR, \fB\-\-library\-path\fR=\fIdir\fR
131: Add \fIdir\fR to the list of library search paths from which \fBmold\fR searches libraries for the \fB\-l\fR option\.
132: .IP
133: Unlike the GNU linkers, \fBmold\fR does not have the default search paths\. This difference doesn't usually make any difference because the compiler driver always passes all necessary search paths to the linker\.
134: .TP
135: \fB\-M\fR, \fB\-\-print\-map\fR
136: Write a map file to stdout\.
137: .TP
138: \fB\-N\fR, \fB\-\-omagic\fR, \fB\-\-no\-omagic\fR
139: Force \fBmold\fR to emit an output file with an old\-fashioned memory layout\. First, it makes the first data segment to not be aligned to a page boundary\. Second, text segments are marked as writable if the option is given\.
140: .TP
141: \fB\-S\fR, \fB\-\-strip\-debug\fR
142: Omit \fB\.debug_*\fR sections from the output file\.
143: .TP
144: \fB\-T\fR \fIfile\fR, \fB\-\-script\fR=\fIfile\fR
145: Read linker script from \fIfile\fR\.
146: .TP
147: \fB\-X\fR, \fB\-\-discard\-locals\fR
148: Discard temporary local symbols to reduce the sizes of the symbol table and the string table\. Temporary local symbols are local symbols starting with \fB\.L\fR\. Compilers usually generate such symbols for unnamed program elements such as string literals or floating\-point literals\.
149: .TP
150: \fB\-e\fR \fIsymbol\fR, \fB\-\-entry\fR=\fIsymbol\fR
151: Use \fIsymbol\fR as the entry point symbol instead of the default entry point symbol \fB_start\fR\.
152: .TP
153: \fB\-f\fR \fIshlib\fR, \fB\-\-auxiliary\fR=\fIshlib\fR
154: Set the \fBDT_AUXILIARY\fR dynamic section field to \fIshlib\fR\.
155: .TP
156: \fB\-h\fR \fIlibname\fR, \fB\-\-soname\fR=\fIlibname\fR
157: Set the \fBDT_SONAME\fR dynamic section field to \fIlibname\fR\. This option is used when creating a shared object file\. Typically, when you create \fBlibfoo\.so\fR, you want to pass \fB\-\-soname=foo\fR to a linker\.
158: .TP
159: \fB\-l\fR \fIlibname\fR
160: Search for \fBlib\fR\fIlibname\fR\fB\.so\fR or \fBlib\fR\fIlibname\fR\fB\.a\fR from library search paths\.
161: .TP
162: \fB\-m\fR \fItarget\fR
163: Choose a target\.
164: .TP
165: \fB\-o\fR \fIfile\fR, \fB\-\-output\fR=\fIfile\fR
166: Use \fIfile\fR as the output file name instead of the default name \fBa\.out\fR\.
167: .TP
168: \fB\-r\fR, \fB\-\-relocatable\fR
169: Instead of generating an executable or a shared object file, combine input object files to generate another object file that can be used as an input to a linker\.
170: .TP
171: \fB\-\-relocatable\-merge\-sections\fR
172: By default, \fBmold\fR doesn't merge input sections by name when merging input object files into a single output object file for \fB\-r\fR\. For example, \fB\.text\.foo\fR and \fB\.text\.bar\fR aren't merged for \fB\-r\fR even though they are merged into \fB\.text\fR according to the default section merging rules\.
173: .IP
174: This option changes the behavior so that \fBmold\fR merges input sections by name by the default section merging rules\.
175: .TP
176: \fB\-s\fR, \fB\-\-strip\-all\fR
177: Omit \fB\.symtab\fR section from the output file\.
178: .TP
179: \fB\-u\fR \fIsymbol\fR, \fB\-\-undefined\fR=\fIsymbol\fR
180: If \fIsymbol\fR remains as an undefined symbol after reading all object files, and if there is a static archive that contains an object file defining \fIsymbol\fR, pull out the object file and link it so that the output file contains a definition of \fIsymbol\fR\.
181: .TP
182: \fB\-\-Bdynamic\fR
183: Link against shared libraries\.
184: .TP
185: \fB\-\-Bstatic\fR
186: Do not link against shared libraries\.
187: .TP
188: \fB\-\-Bsymbolic\fR
189: When creating a shared library, make global symbols export\-only (i\.e\. do not import the same symbol)\. As a result, references within a shared library is always resolved locally, negating symbol override at runtime\. See "Dynamic symbol resolution" for more information about symbol imports and exports\.
190: .TP
191: \fB\-\-Bsymbolic\-functions\fR
192: This option has the same effect as \fB\-\-Bsymbolic\fR but works only for function symbols\. Data symbols remain being both imported and exported\.
193: .TP
194: \fB\-\-Bno\-symbolic\fR
195: Cancel \fB\-\-Bsymbolic\fR and \fB\-\-Bsymbolic\-functions\fR\.
196: .TP
197: \fB\-\-Map\fR=\fIfile\fR
198: Write map file to \fIfile\fR\.
199: .TP
200: \fB\-\-Tbss\fR=\fIaddress\fR
201: Alias for \fB\-\-section\-start=\.bss=\fR\fIaddress\fR\.
202: .TP
203: \fB\-\-Tdata\fR=\fIaddress\fR
204: Alias for \fB\-\-section\-start=\.data=\fR\fIaddress\fR\.
205: .TP
206: \fB\-\-Ttext\fR=\fIaddress\fR
207: Alias for \fB\-\-section\-start=\.text=\fR\fIaddress\fR\.
208: .TP
209: \fB\-\-allow\-multiple\-definition\fR
210: Normally, the linker reports an error if there are more than one definition of a symbol\. This option changes the default behavior so that it doesn't report an error for duplicate definitions and instead use the first definition\.
211: .TP
212: \fB\-\-as\-needed\fR, \fB\-\-no\-as\-needed\fR
213: By default, shared libraries given to the linker are unconditionally added to the list of required libraries in an output file\. However, shared libraries after \fB\-\-as\-needed\fR are added to the list only when at least one symbol is actually used by the output file\. In other words, shared libraries after \fB\-\-as\-needed\fR are not added to the list of needed libraries if they are not needed by a program\.
214: .IP
215: The \fB\-\-no\-as\-needed\fR option restores the default behavior for subsequent files\.
216: .TP
217: \fB\-\-build\-id\fR=[ \fBmd5\fR | \fBsha1\fR | \fBsha256\fR | \fBuuid\fR | \fB0x\fR\fIhexstring\fR | \fBnone\fR ]
218: Create a \fB\.note\.gnu\.build\-id\fR section containing a byte string to uniquely identify an output file\. \fBsha256\fR compute a 256\-bit cryptographic hash of an output file and set it to build\-id\. \fBmd5\fR and \fBsha1\fR compute the same hash but truncate it to 128 and 160 bits, respectively, before setting it to build\-id\. \fBuuid\fR sets a random 128\-bit UUID\. \fB0x\fR\fIhexstring\fR sets \fIhexstring\fR\.
219: .TP
220: \fB\-\-build\-id\fR
221: Synonym for \fB\-\-build\-id=sha256\fR\.
222: .TP
223: \fB\-\-no\-build\-id\fR
224: Synonym for \fB\-\-build\-id=none\fR\.
225: .TP
226: \fB\-\-compress\-debug\-sections\fR=[ \fBzlib\fR | \fBzlib\-gabi\fR | \fBzstd\fR | \fBnone\fR ]
227: Compress DWARF debug info (\fB\.debug_*\fR sections) using the zlib or zstd compression algorithm\. \fB\-zlib\-gabi\fR is an alias for \fB\-zlib\fR\.
228: .TP
229: \fB\-\-defsym\fR=\fIsymbol\fR=\fIvalue\fR
230: Define \fIsymbol\fR as an alias for \fIvalue\fR\.
231: .IP
232: \fIvalue\fR is either an integer (in decimal or hexadecimal with \fB0x\fR prefix) or a symbol name\. If an integer is given as a value, \fIsymbol\fR is defined as an absolute symbol with the given value\.
233: .TP
234: \fB\-\-default\-symver\fR
235: Use soname as a symbol version and append that version to all symbols\.
236: .TP
237: \fB\-\-demangle\fR, \fB\-\-no\-demangle\fR
238: Demangle C++ and Ruts symbols in log messages\.
239: .TP
240: \fB\-\-dependency\-file\fR=\fIfile\fR
241: Write a dependency file to \fIfile\fR\. The contents of the written file is readable by make(1), which defines only one rule with the linker's output file as a target and all input files as its prerequisites\. Users are expected to include the generated dependency file into a Makefile to automate the dependency management\. This option is analogous to the compiler's \fB\-MM \-MF\fR options\.
242: .TP
243: \fB\-\-dynamic\-list\fR=\fIfile\fR
244: Read a list of dynamic symbols from \fIfile\fR\. Same as \fB\-\-export\-dynamic\-symbol\-list\fR, except that it implies \fB\-\-Bsymbolic\fR\. If \fIfile\fR does not exist in the current directory, it is searched from library search paths for the sake of compatibility with GNU ld\.
245: .TP
246: \fB\-\-eh\-frame\-hdr\fR, \fB\-\-no\-eh\-frame\-hdr\fR
247: Create \fB\.eh_frame_hdr\fR section\.
248: .TP
249: \fB\-\-emit\-relocs\fR
250: The linker usually "consumes" relocation sections\. That is, the linker applies relocations to other sections, and relocation sections themselves are discarded\.
251: .IP
252: The \fB\-\-emit\-relocs\fR instructs the linker to leave relocation sections in the output file\. Some post\-link binary analysis or optimization tools such as LLVM Bolt need them\.
253: .TP
254: \fB\-\-enable\-new\-dtags\fR, \fB\-\-disable\-new\-dtags\fR
255: By default, \fBmold\fR emits \fBDT_RUNPATH\fR for \fB\-\-rpath\fR\. If you pass \fB\-\-disable\-new\-dtags\fR, mold emits \fBDT_RPATH\fR for \fB\-\-rpath\fR instead\.
256: .TP
257: \fB\-\-execute\-only\fR
258: Traditionally, most processors require both executable and readable bits to 1 to make the page executable, which allows machine code to be read as data at runtime\. This is actually what an attacker often does after gaining a limited control of a process to find pieces of machine code they can use to gain the full control of the process\. As a mitigation, some recent processors allows "execute\-only" pages\. If a page is execute\-only, you can call a function there as long as you know its address but can't read it as data\.
259: .IP
260: This option marks text segments execute\-only\. This option currently works only on some ARM64 processors\.
261: .TP
262: \fB\-\-exclude\-libs\fR=\fIlibraries\fR \|\.\|\.\|\.
263: Mark all symbols in the given \fIlibraries\fR hidden\.
264: .TP
265: \fB\-\-export\-dynamic\-symbol\fR=\fIsymbol\fR
266: Put symbols matching \fIsymbol\fR in the dynamic symbol table\. \fIsymbol\fR may be a glob pattern in the same syntax as for the \fB\-\-export\-dynamic\-\fR\fIsymbol\-list\fR or \fB\-\-version\-script\fR options\.
267: .TP
268: \fB\-\-export\-dynamic\-symbol\-list\fR=\fIfile\fR
269: Read a list of dynamic symbols from \fIfile\fR\.
270: .TP
271: \fB\-\-fatal\-warnings\fR, \fB\-\-no\-fatal\-warnings\fR
272: Treat warnings as errors\.
273: .TP
274: \fB\-\-fini\fR=\fIsymbol\fR
275: Call \fIsymbol\fR at unload\-time\.
276: .TP
277: \fB\-\-gc\-sections\fR, \fB\-\-no\-gc\-sections\fR
278: Remove unreferenced sections\.
279: .TP
280: \fB\-\-gdb\-index\fR
281: Create a \fB\.gdb_index\fR section to speed up GNU debugger\. To use this, you need to compile source files with the \fB\-ggnu\-pubnames\fR compiler flag\.
282: .TP
283: \fB\-\-hash\-style\fR=[ \fBsysv\fR | \fBgnu\fR | \fBboth\fR | \fBnone\fR ]
284: Set hash style\.
285: .TP
286: \fB\-\-icf\fR=[ \fBsafe\fR | \fBall\fR | \fBnone\fR ], \fB\-\-no\-icf\fR
287: It is not uncommon for a program to contain many identical functions that differ only in name\. For example, a C++ template \fBstd::vector\fR is very likely to be instantiated to the identical code for \fBstd::vector<int>\fR and \fBstd::vector<unsigned>\fR because the container cares only about the size of the parameter type\. Identical Code Folding (ICF) is a size optimization to identify and merge such identical functions\.
288: .IP
289: If \fB\-\-icf=all\fR is given, \fBmold\fR tries to merge all identical functions\. This reduces the size of the output most, but it is not a "safe" optimization\. It is guaranteed in C and C++ that two pointers pointing two different functions will never be equal, but \fB\-\-icf=all\fR breaks that assumption as two identical functions have the same address after merging\. So a care must be taken when you use thsi flag that your program does not depend on the function pointer uniqueness\.
290: .IP
291: \fB\-\-icf=safe\fR is a flag to merge functions only when it is safe to do so\. That is, if a program does not take an address of a function, it is safe to merge that function with other function, as you cannot compare a function pointer with something else without taking an address of a function\.
292: .IP
293: \fB\-\-icf=safe\fR needs to be used with a compiler that supports \fB\.llvm_addrsig\fR section which contains the information as to what symbols are address\-taken\. LLVM/Clang supports that section by default\. Since GCC does not support it yet, you cannot use \fB\-\-icf=safe\fR with GCC (it doesn't do any harm but can't optimize at all\.)
294: .IP
295: \fB\-\-icf=none\fR and \fB\-\-no\-icf\fR disables ICF\.
296: .TP
297: \fB\-\-ignore\-data\-address\-equality\fR
298: Make ICF to merge not only functions but also data\. This option should be used in combination with \fB\-\-icf=all\fR\.
299: .TP
300: \fB\-\-image\-base\fR=\fIaddr\fR
301: Set the base address to \fIaddr\fR\.
302: .TP
303: \fB\-\-init\fR=\fIsymbol\fR
304: Call \fIsymbol\fR at load\-time\.
305: .TP
306: \fB\-\-no\-undefined\fR
307: Report undefined symbols (even with \fB\-\-shared\fR)\.
308: .TP
309: \fB\-\-noinhibit\-exec\fR
310: Create an output file even if errors occur\.
311: .TP
312: \fB\-\-pack\-dyn\-relocs\fR=[ \fBrelr\fR | \fBnone\fR ]
313: If \fBrelr\fR is specified, all \fBR_*_RELATIVE\fR relocations are put into \fB\.relr\.dyn\fR section instead of \fB\.rel\.dyn\fR or \fB\.rela\.dyn\fR section\. Since \fB\.relr\.dyn\fR section uses a space\-efficient encoding scheme, specifying this flag can reduce the size of the output\. This is typically most effective for position\-independent executable\.
314: .IP
315: Note that a runtime loader has to support \fB\.relr\.dyn\fR to run executables or shared libraries linked with \fB\-\-pack\-dyn\-relocs=relr\fR\. As of 2022, only ChromeOS, Android and Fuchsia support it \.
316: .TP
317: \fB\-\-package\-metadata\fR=\fIstring\fR
318: Embed \fIstring\fR to a \fB\.note\.package\fR section\. This option in intended to be used by a package management command such as rpm(8) to embed metadata regarding a package to each executable file\.
319: .TP
320: \fB\-\-pie\fR, \fB\-pic\-executable\fR, \fB\-\-no\-pie\fR, \fB\-no\-pic\-executable\fR
321: Create a position\-independent executable\.
322: .TP
323: \fB\-\-print\-gc\-sections\fR, \fB\-\-no\-print\-gc\-sections\fR
324: Print removed unreferenced sections\.
325: .TP
326: \fB\-\-print\-icf\-sections\fR, \fB\-\-no\-print\-icf\-sections\fR
327: Print folded identical sections\.
328: .TP
329: \fB\-\-push\-state\fR, \fB\-\-pop\-state\fR
330: \fB\-\-push\-state\fR saves the current values of \fB\-\-as\-needed\fR, \fB\-\-whole\-archive\fR, \fB\-\-static\fR, and \fB\-\-start\-lib\fR\. The saved values can be restored by \fB\-\-pop\-state\fR\.
331: .IP
332: \fB\-\-push\-state\fR and \fB\-\-pop\-state\fR pairs can nest\.
333: .IP
334: These options are useful when you want to construct linker command line options programmatically\. For example, if you want to link \fBlibfoo\.so\fR by as\-needed basis but don't want to change the global state of \fB\-\-as\-needed\fR, you can append \fB\-\-push\-state \-\-as\-needed \-lfoo \-\-pop\-state\fR to the linker command line options\.
335: .TP
336: \fB\-\-relax\fR, \fB\-\-no\-relax\fR
337: Rewrite machine instructions with more efficient ones for some relocations\. The feature is enabled by default\.
338: .TP
339: \fB\-\-require\-defined\fR=\fIsymbol\fR
340: Like \fB\-\-undefined\fR, except the new symbol must be defined by the end of the link\.
341: .TP
342: \fB\-\-retain\-symbols\-file\fR=\fIfile\fR
343: Keep only symbols listed in \fIfile\fR\. \fIfile\fR is a text file containing a symbol name on each line\. \fBmold\fR discards all local symbols as well as global symbol that are not in \fIfile\fR\. Note that this option removes symbols only from \fI\.symtab\fR section and does not affect \fI\.dynsym\fR section, which is used for dynamic linking\.
344: .TP
345: \fB\-\-rpath\fR=\fIdir\fR
346: Add \fIdir\fR to runtime search paths\.
347: .TP
348: \fB\-\-section\-start=\fR\fIsection\fR=\fIaddress\fR
349: Set \fIaddress\fR to \fIsection\fR\. \fIaddress\fR is a hexadecimal number that may start with an optional \fB0x\fR\.
350: .TP
351: \fB\-\-shared\fR, \fB\-Bshareable\fR
352: Create a share library\.
353: .TP
354: \fB\-\-spare\-dynamic\-tags\fR=\fInumber\fR
355: Reserve the given \fInumber\fR of tags in \fI\.dynamic\fR section\.
356: .TP
357: \fB\-\-start\-lib\fR, \fB\-\-end\-lib\fR
358: Handle object files between \fB\-\-start\-lib\fR and \fB\-\-end\-lib\fR as if they were in an archive file\. That means object files between them are linked only when they are needed to resolve undefined symbols\. The options are useful if you want to link object files only when they are needed but want to avoid the overhead of running ar(3)\.
359: .TP
360: \fB\-\-static\fR
361: Do not link against shared libraries\.
362: .TP
363: \fB\-\-sysroot\fR=\fIdir\fR
364: Set target system root directory to \fIdir\fR\.
365: .TP
366: \fB\-\-trace\fR
367: Print name of each input file\.
368: .TP
369: \fB\-\-undefined\-version\fR, \fB\-\-no\-undefined\-version\fR
370: By default, \fBmold\fR warns on a symbol specified by a version script or by \fB\-\-export\-dynamic\-symbol\fR if it is not defined\. You can silence the warning by \fB\-\-undefined\-version\fR\.
371: .TP
372: \fB\-\-unique\fR=\fIpattern\fR
373: Don't merge input sections that match the given glob pattern \fIpattern\fR\.
374: .TP
375: \fB\-\-unresolved\-symbols\fR=[ \fBreport\-all\fR | \fBignore\-all\fR | \fBignore\-in\-object\-files\fR | \fBignore\-in\-shared\-libs\fR ]
376: How to handle undefined symbols\.
377: .TP
378: \fB\-\-version\-script\fR=\fIfile\fR
379: Read version script from \fIfile\fR\. If \fIfile\fR does not exist in the current directory, it is searched from library search paths for the sake of compatibility with GNU ld\.
380: .TP
381: \fB\-\-warn\-common\fR, \fB\-\-no\-warn\-common\fR
382: Warn about common symbols\.
383: .TP
384: \fB\-\-warn\-once\fR
385: Only warn once for each undefined symbol instead of warn for each relocation referring an undefined symbol\.
386: .TP
387: \fB\-\-warn\-unresolved\-symbols\fR, \fB\-\-error\-unresolved\-symbols\fR
388: Normally, the linker reports an error for unresolved symbols\. \fB\-\-warn\-unresolved\-symbols\fR option turns it into a warning\. \fB\-\-error\-unresolved\-symbols\fR option restores the default behavior\.
389: .TP
390: \fB\-\-whole\-archive\fR, \fB\-\-no\-whole\-archive\fR
391: When archive files (\fB\.a\fR files) are given to the linker, only object files that are needed to resolve undefined symbols are extracted from them and linked to an output file\. \fB\-\-whole\-archive\fR changes that behavior for subsequent archives so that the linker extracts all object files and link them to an output\. For example, if you are creating a shared object file and you want to include all archive members to the output, you should pass \fB\-\-whole\-archive\fR\. \fB\-\-no\-whole\-archive\fR restores the default behavior for subsequent archives\.
392: .TP
393: \fB\-\-wrap\fR=\fIsymbol\fR
394: Make \fIsymbol\fR to be resolved to \fB__wrap_\fR\fIsymbol\fR\. The original symbol can be resolved as \fB__real_\fR\fIsymbol\fR\. This option is typically used for wrapping an existing function\.
395: .TP
396: \fB\-z cet\-report\fR=[ \fBwarning\fR | \fBerror\fR | \fBnone\fR]
397: Intel Control\-flow Enforcement Technology (CET) is a new x86 feature available since Tiger Lake which is released in 2020\. It defines new instructions to harden security to protect programs from control hijacking attacks\. You can tell compiler to use the feature by specifying the \fB\-fcf\-protection\fR flag\.
398: .IP
399: \fB\-z cet\-report\fR flag is used to make sure that all object files were compiled with a correct \fB\-fcf\-protection\fR flag\. If \fBwarning\fR or \fBerror\fR are given, \fBmold\fR prints out a warning or an error message if an object file was not compiled with the compiler flag\.
400: .IP
401: \fBmold\fR looks for \fBGNU_PROPERTY_X86_FEATURE_1_IBT\fR bit and \fBGNU_PROPERTY_X86_FEATURE_1_SHSTK\fR bit in \fB\.note\.gnu\.property\fR section to determine whether or not an object file was compiled with \fB\-fcf\-protection\fR\.
402: .TP
403: \fB\-z now\fR, \fB\-z lazy\fR
404: By default, functions referring to other ELF modules are resolved by the dynamic linker when they are called for the first time\. \fB\-z now\fR marks an executable or a shared library file so that all dynamic symbols are resolved when a file is loaded to memory\. \fB\-z lazy\fR restores the default behavior\.
405: .TP
406: \fB\-z origin\fR
407: Mark object requiring immediate \fB$ORIGIN\fR processing at runtime\.
408: .TP
409: \fB\-z ibt\fR
410: Turn on \fBGNU_PROPERTY_X86_FEATURE_1_IBT\fR bit in \fB\.note\.gnu\.property\fR section to indicate that the output uses IBT\-enabled PLT\. This option implies \fB\-z ibtplt\fR\.
411: .TP
412: \fB\-z ibtplt\fR
413: Generate Intel Branch Tracking (IBT)\-enabled PLT which is the default on x86\-64\. This is the default\.
414: .TP
415: \fB\-z execstack\fR, \fB\-z noexecstack\fR
416: By default, the pages for the stack area (i\.e\. the pages where local variables reside) are not executable for security reasons\. \fB\-z execstack\fR makes it executable\. \fB\-z noexecstack\fR restores the default behavior\.
417: .TP
418: \fB\-z keep\-text\-section\-prefix\fR, \fB\-z nokeep\-text\-section\-prefix\fR
419: Keep \fB\.text\.hot\fR, \fB\.text\.unknown\fR, \fB\.text\.unlikely\fR, \fB\.text\.startup\fR and \fB\.text\.exit\fR as separate sections in the final binary instead of merging them as \fB\.text\fR\.
420: .TP
421: \fB\-z relro\fR, \fB\-z norelro\fR
422: Some sections such as \fB\.dynamic\fR have to be writable only during an executable or a shared library file is being loaded to memory\. Once the dynamic linker finishes its job, such sections won't be mutated by anyone\. As a security mitigation, it is preferred to make such segments read\-only during program execution\.
423: .IP
424: \fB\-z relro\fR puts such sections into a special segment called \fBrelro\fR\. The dynamic linker make a relro segment read\-only after it finishes its job\.
425: .IP
426: By default, \fBmold\fR generates a relro segment\. \fB\-z norelro\fR disables the feature\.
427: .TP
428: \fB\-z separate\-loadable\-segments\fR, \fB\-z separate\-code\fR, \fB\-z noseparate\-code\fR
429: If one memory page contains multiple segments, the page protection bits are set in such a way that needed attributes (writable or executable) are satisfied for all segments\. This usually happens at a boundary of two segments with two different attributes\.
430: .IP
431: \fBseparate\-loadable\-segments\fR adds paddings between segments with different attributes so that they do not share the same page\. This is the default\.
432: .IP
433: \fBseparate\-code\fR adds paddings only between executable and non\-executable segments\.
434: .IP
435: \fBnoseparate\-code\fR does not add any paddings between segments\.
436: .TP
437: \fB\-z defs\fR, \fB\-z nodefs\fR
438: Report undefined symbols (even with \fB\-\-shared\fR)\.
439: .TP
440: \fB\-z shstk\fR
441: Enforce shadow stack by turning \fBGNU_PROPERTY_X86_FEATURE_1_SHSTK\fR bit in \fB\.note\.gnu\.property\fR output section\. Shadow stack is part of Intel Control\-flow Enforcement Technology (CET), which is available since Tiger Lake (2020)\.
442: .TP
443: \fB\-z text\fR, \fB\-z notext\fR, \fB\-z textoff\fR
444: \fBmold\fR by default reports an error if dynamic relocations are created in read\-only sections\. If \fB\-z notext\fR or \fB\-z textoff\fR are given, \fBmold\fR creates such dynamic relocations without reporting an error\. \fB\-z text\fR restores the default behavior\.
445: .TP
446: \fB\-z max\-page\-size\fR=\fInumber\fR
447: Some CPU ISAs support multiple different memory page sizes\. This option specifies the maximum page size that an output binary can run on\. The default value is 4 KiB for i386, x86\-64 and RISC\-V, and 64 KiB for ARM64\.
448: .TP
449: \fB\-z nodefaultlib\fR
450: Make the dynamic loader to ignore default search paths\.
451: .TP
452: \fB\-z nodelete\fR
453: Mark DSO non\-deletable at runtime\.
454: .TP
455: \fB\-z nodlopen\fR
456: Mark DSO not available to dlopen(3)\. This option makes it possible for the linker to optimize thread\-local variable accesses by rewriting instructions for some targets\.
457: .TP
458: \fB\-z nodump\fR
459: Mark DSO not available to dldump(3)\.
460: .TP
461: \fB\-z nocopyreloc\fR
462: Do not create copy relocations\.
463: .TP
464: \fB\-z initfirst\fR
465: Mark DSO to be initialized first at runtime\.
466: .TP
467: \fB\-z interpose\fR
468: Mark object to interpose all DSOs but executable\.
469: .TP
470: \fB\-(\fR, \fB\-)\fR, \fB\-EL\fR, \fB\-O\fR\fInumber\fR, \fB\-\-allow\-shlib\-undefined\fR, \fB\-\-dc\fR, \fB\-\-dp\fR, \fB\-\-end\-group\fR, \fB\-\-no\-add\-needed\fR, \fB\-\-no\-allow\-shlib\-undefined\fR, \fB\-\-no\-copy\-dt\-needed\-entries\fR, \fB\-\-no\-fatal\-warnings\fR, \fB\-\-nostdlib\fR, \fB\-\-rpath\-link=Ar dir\fR, \fB\-\-sort\-common\fR, \fB\-\-sort\-section\fR, \fB\-\-start\-group\fR, \fB\-\-warn\-constructors\fR, \fB\-\-warn\-once\fR, \fB\-\-fix\-cortex\-a53\-835769\fR, \fB\-\-fix\-cortex\-a53\-843419\fR, \fB\-z combreloc\fR, \fB\-z common\-page\-size\fR, \fB\-z nocombreloc\fR
471: Ignored
472: .SH "ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES"
473: .IP "\[ci]" 4
474: \fBMOLD_DEBUG\fR: If this variable is set to a non\-empty string, \fBmold\fR embeds its command\-line options to the output file's \fB\.comment\fR section\.
475: .IP "\[ci]" 4
476: \fBMOLD_REPRO\fR Setting this variable to a non\-empty string has the same effect as passign the \fB\-\-repro\fR option\.
477: .IP "" 0
478: .SH "SEE ALSO"
479: gold(1), ld(1), elf(5), ld\.so(8)
480: .SH "AUTHOR"
481: Rui Ueyama \fIruiu@cs\.stanford\.edu\fR
482: .SH "BUGS"
483: Report bugs to \fIhttps://github\.com/rui314/mold/issues\fR\.

================
File: docs/mold.md
================
  1: mold(1) -- a modern linker
  2: ==========================
  3: 
  4: ## SYNOPSIS
  5: 
  6: `mold` [_option_...] _file_...
  7: 
  8: ## DESCRIPTION
  9: `mold` is a faster drop-in replacement for the default GNU ld(1).
 10: 
 11: ### How to use
 12: See <https://github.com/rui314/mold#how-to-use>.
 13: 
 14: ### Compatibility
 15: 
 16: **Mold** is designed to be a drop-in replacement for the GNU linkers for
 17: linking user-land programs. If your user-land program cannot be built due to
 18: missing command-line options, please file a bug at
 19: <https://github.com/rui314/mold/issues>.
 20: 
 21: Mold supports a very limited set of linker script features, which is just
 22: sufficient to read `/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so` on Linux systems (on
 23: Linux, that file is despite its name not a shared library but an ASCII linker
 24: script that loads a real `libc.so` file.)
 25: 
 26: Beyond that, we have no plan to support any linker script features. The linker
 27: script is an ad-hoc, over-designed, complex language which we believe needs to
 28: be disrupted by a simpler mechanism. We have a plan to add a replacement for
 29: the linker script to `mold` instead.
 30: 
 31: ### Archive symbol resolution
 32: 
 33: Traditionally, Unix linkers are sensitive to the order in which input files
 34: appear on command line. They process input files from the first (leftmost)
 35: file to the last (rightmost) file one-by-one. While reading input files, they
 36: maintain sets of defined and undefined symbols. When visiting an archive file
 37: (`.a` files), they pull out object files to resolve as many undefined symbols
 38: as possible and go on to the next input file. Object files that weren't pulled
 39: out will never have a chance for a second look.
 40: 
 41: Due to this semantics, you usually have to add archive files at the end of a
 42: command line, so that when a linker reaches archive files, it knows what
 43: symbols remain as undefined.
 44: 
 45: If you put archive files at the beginning of a command line, a linker doesn't
 46: have any undefined symbol, and thus no object files will be pulled out from
 47: archives. You can change the processing order by `--start-group` and
 48: `--end-group` options, though they make a linker slower.
 49: 
 50: `mold` as well as LLVM lld(1) linker take a different approach. They memorize
 51: what symbols can be resolved from archive files instead of forgetting it after
 52: processing each archive. Therefore, `mold` and lld(1) can "go back" in a
 53: command line to pull out object files from archives if they are needed to
 54: resolve remaining undefined symbols. They are not sensitive to the input file
 55: order.
 56: 
 57: `--start-group` and `--end-group` are still accepted by `mold` and lld(1)
 58: for compatibility with traditional linkers, but they are silently ignored.
 59: 
 60: ### Dynamic symbol resolution
 61: 
 62: Some Unix linker features are unable to be understood without understanding
 63: the semantics of dynamic symbol resolution. Therefore, even though that's not
 64: specific to `mold`, we'll explain it here.
 65: 
 66: We use "ELF module" or just "module" as a collective term to refer to an
 67: executable or a shared library file in the ELF format.
 68: 
 69: An ELF module may have lists of imported symbols and exported symbols, as well
 70: as a list of shared library names from which imported symbols should be
 71: imported. The point is that imported symbols are not bound to any specific
 72: shared library until runtime.
 73: 
 74: Here is how the Unix dynamic linker resolves dynamic symbols. Upon the start
 75: of an ELF program, the dynamic linker construct a list of ELF modules which as
 76: a whole consist of a complete program. The executable file is always at the
 77: beginning of the list followed by its depending shared libraries. An imported
 78: symbol is searched from the beginning of the list to the end. If two or more
 79: modules define the same symbol, the one that appears first in the list takes
 80: precedence over the others.
 81: 
 82: This Unix semantics are contrary to systems such as Windows that have the
 83: two-level namespace for dynamic symbols. On Windows, for example, dynamic
 84: symbols are represented as a tuple of (`symbol-name`, `shared-library-name`),
 85: so that each dynamic symbol is guaranteed to be resolved from some specific
 86: library.
 87: 
 88: Typically, an ELF module that exports a symbol also imports the same symbol.
 89: Such a symbol is usually resolved to itself, but that's not the case if a
 90: module that appears before in the symbol search list provides another
 91: definition of the same symbol.
 92: 
 93: Let me take `malloc` as an example. Assume that you define your version of
 94: `malloc` in your main executable file. Then, all `malloc` calls from any
 95: module are resolved to your function instead of that in libc, because the
 96: executable is always at the beginning of the dynamic symbol search list. Note
 97: that even `malloc` calls within libc are resolved to your definition since
 98: libc exports and imports `malloc`. Therefore, by defining `malloc` yourself,
 99: you can overwrite a library function, and the `malloc` in libc becomes dead
100: code.
101: 
102: These Unix semantics are tricky and sometimes considered harmful. For example,
103: assume that you accidentally define `atoi` as a global function in your
104: executable that behaves completely differently from the one in the C standard.
105: Then, all `atoi` function calls from any modules (even function calls within
106: libc) are redirected to your function instead of the one in libc which
107: will very likely to cause a problem.
108: 
109: That is a somewhat surprising consequence for an accidental name conflict. On
110: the other hand, this semantic is sometimes useful because it allows users to
111: override library functions without rebuilding modules containing them.
112: 
113: Whether good or bad, you should keep this semantics in mind to understand the
114: Unix linkers behaviors.
115: 
116: ### Build reproducibility
117: 
118: `mold`'s output is deterministic. That is, if you pass the same object files
119: and the same command-line options to the same version of `mold`, it is
120: guaranteed that `mold` produces the bit-by-bit identical same output. The
121: linker's internal randomness, such as the timing of thread scheduling or
