122: iteration orders of hash tables, doesn't affect the output.
123: 
124: `mold` does not have any host-specific default settings. This is contrary to
125: the GNU linkers to which some configurable values, such as system-dependent
126: library search paths, are hard-coded. `mold` depends only on its command-line
127: arguments.
128: 
129: 
130: ## MOLD-SPECIFIC OPTIONS
131: 
132: * `--chroot`=_dir_:
133:   Set _dir_ to root directory.
134: 
135: * `--color-diagnostics`=[ _auto_ | _always_ | _never_ ]:
136:   Show diagnostics messages in color using ANSI escape sequences. `auto`
137:   means that `mold` prints out messages in color only if the standard output
138:   is connected to a TTY. Default is `auto`.
139: 
140: * `--color-diagnostics`:
141:   Synonym for `--color-diagnostics=auto`.
142: 
143: * `--no-color-diagnostics`:
144:   Synonym for `--color-diagnostics=never`.
145: 
146: * `--fork`, `--no-fork`:
147:   Spawn a child process and let it do the actual linking. When linking a
148:   large program, the OS kernel can take a few hundred milliseconds to
149:   terminate a `mold` process. `--fork` hides that latency. By default, it
150:   does fork.
151: 
152: * `--perf`:
153:   Print performance statistics.
154: 
155: * `--print-dependencies`:
156:   Print out dependency information for input files.
157: 
158:   Each line of the output for this option shows that which file depends on
159:   which file to use which symbol. This option is useful to debug why some
160:   object file in a static archive got linked or why some shared library is
161:   kept in an output file's dependency list even with `--as-needed`.
162: 
163: * `--repro`:
164:   Archive input files as well as a text file containing command line options
165:   as a tar file so that you can run `mold` with the exact same inputs again.
166:   This is useful to report a bug with a reproducer. The output filename is
167:   `path/to/output.tar` where `path/to/output` is an output filename specified
168:   by `-o`.
169: 
170: * `--reverse-sections`:
171:   Reverses the order of input sections before assigning them the offsets in
172:   the output file.
173: 
174:   This option is useful for finding bugs that depend on an initialization
175:   order of global objects. In C++, constructors of global objects in a
176:   single source file are guaranteed to be executed in the source order, but
177:   there's no such guarantee across compilation units. Usually, constructors
178:   are executed in the order given to the linker, but depending on it is a
179:   mistake.
180: 
181:   By reversing the order of input sections using `--reverse-sections`, you
182:   can easily test that your program works in the reversed initialization
183:   order.
184: 
185: * `--run` _command_ _arg_...:
186:   Run `command` with `mold` `/usr/bin/ld`. Specifically, `mold` runs a given
187:   command with the `LD_PRELOAD` environment set to intercept exec(3) family
188:   functions and replaces `argv[0]` with itself if it is `ld`, `ld.gold` or
189:   `ld.lld`.
190: 
191: * `--shuffle-sections`, `--shuffle-sections`=_number_:
192:   Randomizes the output by shuffling the order of input sections before
193:   assigning them the offsets in the output file. If _number_ is given, it's
194:   used as a seed for the random number generator, so that the linker
195:   produces the same output for the same seed. If no seed is given, a
196:   random number is used as a seed.
197: 
198:   This option is useful for benchmarking. Modern CPUs are sensitive to
199:   program's memory layout. A seeming benign change in program layout such
200:   as a small size increase of a function in the middle of a program can
201:   affect program's performance. Therefore, even if you write new code and
202:   get a good benchmark result, it is hard to say whether or not the new code
203:   improves the programs performance; it is possible that the new memory
204:   layout happens to perform better.
205: 
206:   By running a benchmark multiple time with randomized memory layouts using
207:   `--shuffle-sections`, you can isolate your program's real performance
208:   number from the randomness caused by memory layout changes.
209: 
210: * `--stats`:
211:   Print input statistics.
212: 
213: * `--thread-count`=_count_:
214:   Use _count_ number of threads.
215: 
216: * `--threads`, `--no-threads`:
217:   Use multiple threads. By default, `mold` uses as many threads as the
218:   number of cores or 32, whichever is the smallest. The reason why it is
219:   capped to 32 is because `mold` doesn't scale well beyond that point. To
220:   use only one thread, pass `-no-threads` or `-thread-count=1`.
221: 
222: * `--quick-exit`, `--no-quick-exit`:
223:   Use or do not use `quick_exit` to exit.
224: 
225: ## GNU-COMPATIBLE OPTIONS
226: 
227: * `--help`:
228:   Report usage information to stdout and exit.
229: 
230: * `-v`, `--version`:
231:   Report version information to stdout.
232: 
233: * `-V`:
234:   Report version and target information to stdout.
235: 
236: * `-E`, `--export-dynamic`, `--no-export-dynamic`:
237:   When creating an executable, using the `-E` option causes all global
238:   symbols to be put into the dynamic symbol table, so that the symbols are
239:   visible from other ELF modules at runtime.
240: 
241:   By default, or if `--no-export-dynamic` is given, only symbols that are
242:   referenced by DSOs at link-time are exported from an executable.
243: 
244: * `-F` _libname_, `--filter`=_libname_:
245:   Set the `DT_FILTER` dynamic section field to _libname_.
246: 
247: * `-I` _file_, `--dynamic-linker`=_file_, `--no-dynamic-linker`:
248:   Set the dynamic linker path to _file_. If no `-I` option is given, or if
249:   `--no-dynamic-linker` is given, no dynamic linker path is set to an output
250:   file. This is contrary to the GNU linkers which sets a default dynamic
251:   linker path in that case. This difference doesn't usually make any
252:   difference because the compiler driver always passes `-I` to the linker.
253: 
254: * `-L` _dir_, `--library-path`=_dir_:
255:   Add _dir_ to the list of library search paths from which `mold` searches
256:   libraries for the `-l` option.
257: 
258:   Unlike the GNU linkers, `mold` does not have the default search paths.
259:   This difference doesn't usually make any difference because the compiler
260:   driver always passes all necessary search paths to the linker.
261: 
262: * `-M`, `--print-map`:
263:   Write a map file to stdout.
264: 
265: * `-N`, `--omagic`, `--no-omagic`:
266:   Force `mold` to emit an output file with an old-fashioned memory layout.
267:   First, it makes the first data segment to not be aligned to a page
268:   boundary. Second, text segments are marked as writable if the option is
269:   given.
270: 
271: * `-S`, `--strip-debug`:
272:   Omit `.debug_*` sections from the output file.
273: 
274: * `-T` _file_, `--script`=_file_:
275:   Read linker script from _file_.
276: 
277: * `-X`, `--discard-locals`:
278:   Discard temporary local symbols to reduce the sizes of the symbol table and
279:   the string table. Temporary local symbols are local symbols starting with
280:   `.L`. Compilers usually generate such symbols for unnamed program elements
281:   such as string literals or floating-point literals.
282: 
283: * `-e` _symbol_, `--entry`=_symbol_:
284:   Use _symbol_ as the entry point symbol instead of the default entry point
285:   symbol `_start`.
286: 
287: * `-f` _shlib_, `--auxiliary`=_shlib_:
288:   Set the `DT_AUXILIARY` dynamic section field to _shlib_.
289: 
290: * `-h` _libname_, `--soname`=_libname_:
291:   Set the `DT_SONAME` dynamic section field to _libname_. This option is
292:   used when creating a shared object file. Typically, when you create
293:   `libfoo.so`, you want to pass `--soname=foo` to a linker.
294: 
295: * `-l` _libname_:
296:   Search for `lib`_libname_`.so` or `lib`_libname_`.a` from library search
297:   paths.
298: 
299: * `-m` _target_:
300:   Choose a target.
301: 
302: * `-o` _file_, `--output`=_file_:
303:   Use _file_ as the output file name instead of the default name `a.out`.
304: 
305: * `-r`, `--relocatable`:
306:   Instead of generating an executable or a shared object file, combine input
307:   object files to generate another object file that can be used as an input
308:   to a linker.
309: 
310: * `--relocatable-merge-sections`:
311:   By default, `mold` doesn't merge input sections by name when merging input
312:   object files into a single output object file for `-r`. For example,
313:   `.text.foo` and `.text.bar` aren't merged for `-r` even though they are
314:   merged into `.text` according to the default section merging rules.
315: 
316:   This option changes the behavior so that `mold` merges input sections by
317:   name by the default section merging rules.
318: 
319: * `-s`, `--strip-all`:
320:   Omit `.symtab` section from the output file.
321: 
322: * `-u` _symbol_, `--undefined`=_symbol_:
323:   If _symbol_ remains as an undefined symbol after reading all object files,
324:   and if there is a static archive that contains an object file defining
325:   _symbol_, pull out the object file and link it so that the output file
326:   contains a definition of _symbol_.
327: 
328: * `--Bdynamic`:
329:   Link against shared libraries.
330: 
331: * `--Bstatic`:
332:   Do not link against shared libraries.
333: 
334: * `--Bsymbolic`:
335:   When creating a shared library, make global symbols export-only (i.e. do
336:   not import the same symbol). As a result, references within a shared
337:   library is always resolved locally, negating symbol override at runtime.
338:   See "Dynamic symbol resolution" for more information about symbol imports
339:   and exports.
340: 
341: * `--Bsymbolic-functions`:
342:   This option has the same effect as `--Bsymbolic` but works only for function
343:   symbols. Data symbols remain being both imported and exported.
344: 
345: * `--Bno-symbolic`:
346:   Cancel `--Bsymbolic` and `--Bsymbolic-functions`.
347: 
348: * `--Map`=_file_:
349:   Write map file to _file_.
350: 
351: * `--Tbss`=_address_:
352:   Alias for `--section-start=.bss=`_address_.
353: 
354: * `--Tdata`=_address_:
355:   Alias for `--section-start=.data=`_address_.
356: 
357: * `--Ttext`=_address_:
358:   Alias for `--section-start=.text=`_address_.
359: 
360: * `--allow-multiple-definition`:
361:   Normally, the linker reports an error if there are more than one
362:   definition of a symbol. This option changes the default behavior so that
363:   it doesn't report an error for duplicate definitions and instead use the
364:   first definition.
365: 
366: * `--as-needed`, `--no-as-needed`:
367:   By default, shared libraries given to the linker are unconditionally added
368:   to the list of required libraries in an output file. However, shared
369:   libraries after `--as-needed` are added to the list only when at least one
370:   symbol is actually used by the output file. In other words, shared libraries
371:   after `--as-needed` are not added to the list of needed libraries if they
372:   are not needed by a program.
373: 
374:   The `--no-as-needed` option restores the default behavior for subsequent
375:   files.
376: 
377: * `--build-id`=[ `md5` | `sha1` | `sha256` | `uuid` | `0x`_hexstring_ | `none` ]:
378:   Create a `.note.gnu.build-id` section containing a byte string to uniquely
379:   identify an output file. `sha256` compute a 256-bit cryptographic hash of
380:   an output file and set it to build-id. `md5` and `sha1` compute the same
381:   hash but truncate it to 128 and 160 bits, respectively, before setting it
382:   to build-id. `uuid` sets a random 128-bit UUID. `0x`_hexstring_ sets
383:   _hexstring_.
384: 
385: * `--build-id`:
386:   Synonym for `--build-id=sha256`.
387: 
388: * `--no-build-id`:
389:   Synonym for `--build-id=none`.
390: 
391: * `--compress-debug-sections`=[ `zlib` | `zlib-gabi` | `zstd` | `none` ]:
392:   Compress DWARF debug info (`.debug_*` sections) using the zlib or zstd
393:   compression algorithm. `-zlib-gabi` is an alias for `-zlib`.
394: 
395: * `--defsym`=_symbol_=_value_:
396:   Define _symbol_ as an alias for _value_.
397: 
398:   _value_ is either an integer (in decimal or hexadecimal with `0x` prefix)
399:   or a symbol name. If an integer is given as a value, _symbol_ is defined
400:   as an absolute symbol with the given value.
401: 
402: * `--default-symver`:
403:   Use soname as a symbol version and append that version to all symbols.
404: 
405: * `--demangle`, `--no-demangle`:
406:   Demangle C++ and Ruts symbols in log messages.
407: 
408: * `--dependency-file`=_file_:
409:   Write a dependency file to _file_. The contents of the written file is
410:   readable by make(1), which defines only one rule with the linker's output
411:   file as a target and all input files as its prerequisites. Users are
412:   expected to include the generated dependency file into a Makefile to
413:   automate the dependency management. This option is analogous to the
414:   compiler's `-MM -MF` options.
415: 
416: * `--dynamic-list`=_file_:
417:   Read a list of dynamic symbols from _file_. Same as
418:   `--export-dynamic-symbol-list`, except that it implies `--Bsymbolic`.
419:   If _file_ does not exist in the current directory, it is searched from
420:   library search paths for the sake of compatibility with GNU ld.
421: 
422: * `--eh-frame-hdr`, `--no-eh-frame-hdr`:
423:   Create `.eh_frame_hdr` section.
424: 
425: * `--emit-relocs`:
426:   The linker usually "consumes" relocation sections. That is, the linker
427:   applies relocations to other sections, and relocation sections themselves
428:   are discarded.
429: 
430:   The `--emit-relocs` instructs the linker to leave relocation sections in
431:   the output file. Some post-link binary analysis or optimization tools such
432:   as LLVM Bolt need them.
433: 
434: * `--enable-new-dtags`, `--disable-new-dtags`:
435:   By default, `mold` emits `DT_RUNPATH` for `--rpath`. If you pass
436:   `--disable-new-dtags`, mold emits `DT_RPATH` for `--rpath` instead.
437: 
438: * `--execute-only`:
439:   Traditionally, most processors require both executable and readable bits
440:   to 1 to make the page executable, which allows machine code to be read as
441:   data at runtime. This is actually what an attacker often does after gaining
442:   a limited control of a process to find pieces of machine code they can use
443:   to gain the full control of the process. As a mitigation, some recent
444:   processors allows "execute-only" pages. If a page is execute-only, you can
445:   call a function there as long as you know its address but can't read it as
446:   data.
447: 
448:   This option marks text segments execute-only. This option currently works
449:   only on some ARM64 processors.
450: 
451: * `--exclude-libs`=_libraries_ ...:
452:   Mark all symbols in the given _libraries_ hidden.
453: 
454: * `--export-dynamic-symbol`=_symbol_:
455:   Put symbols matching _symbol_ in the dynamic symbol table. _symbol_ may be a
456:   glob pattern in the same syntax as for the `--export-dynamic-`_symbol-list_
457:   or `--version-script` options.
458: 
459: * `--export-dynamic-symbol-list`=_file_:
460:   Read a list of dynamic symbols from _file_.
461: 
462: * `--fatal-warnings`, `--no-fatal-warnings`:
463:   Treat warnings as errors.
464: 
465: * `--fini`=_symbol_:
466:   Call _symbol_ at unload-time.
467: 
468: * `--gc-sections`, `--no-gc-sections`:
469:   Remove unreferenced sections.
470: 
471: * `--gdb-index`:
472:   Create a `.gdb_index` section to speed up GNU debugger. To use this, you
473:   need to compile source files with the `-ggnu-pubnames` compiler flag.
474: 
475: * `--hash-style`=[ `sysv` | `gnu` | `both` | `none` ]:
476:   Set hash style.
477: 
478: * `--icf`=[ `safe` | `all` | `none` ], `--no-icf`:
479:   It is not uncommon for a program to contain many identical functions that
480:   differ only in name. For example, a C++ template `std::vector` is very
481:   likely to be instantiated to the identical code for `std::vector<int>` and
482:   `std::vector<unsigned>` because the container cares only about the size of
483:   the parameter type. Identical Code Folding (ICF) is a size optimization to
484:   identify and merge such identical functions.
485: 
486:   If `--icf=all` is given, `mold` tries to merge all identical functions.
487:   This reduces the size of the output most, but it is not a "safe"
488:   optimization. It is guaranteed in C and C++ that two pointers pointing two
489:   different functions will never be equal, but `--icf=all` breaks that
490:   assumption as two identical functions have the same address after merging.
491:   So a care must be taken when you use thsi flag that your program does not
492:   depend on the function pointer uniqueness.
493: 
494:   `--icf=safe` is a flag to merge functions only when it is safe to do so.
495:   That is, if a program does not take an address of a function, it is safe
496:   to merge that function with other function, as you cannot compare a
497:   function pointer with something else without taking an address of a
498:   function.
499: 
500:   `--icf=safe` needs to be used with a compiler that supports
501:   `.llvm_addrsig` section which contains the information as to what symbols
502:   are address-taken. LLVM/Clang supports that section by default. Since GCC
503:   does not support it yet, you cannot use `--icf=safe` with GCC (it doesn't
504:   do any harm but can't optimize at all.)
505: 
506:   `--icf=none` and `--no-icf` disables ICF.
507: 
508: * `--ignore-data-address-equality`:
509:   Make ICF to merge not only functions but also data. This option should be
510:   used in combination with `--icf=all`.
511: 
512: * `--image-base`=_addr_:
513:   Set the base address to _addr_.
514: 
515: * `--init`=_symbol_:
516:   Call _symbol_ at load-time.
517: 
518: * `--no-undefined`:
519:   Report undefined symbols (even with `--shared`).
520: 
521: *  `--noinhibit-exec`:
522:   Create an output file even if errors occur.
523: 
524: * `--pack-dyn-relocs`=[ `relr` | `none` ]:
525:   If `relr` is specified, all `R_*_RELATIVE` relocations are put into
526:   `.relr.dyn` section instead of `.rel.dyn` or `.rela.dyn` section. Since
527:   `.relr.dyn` section uses a space-efficient encoding scheme, specifying
528:   this flag can reduce the size of the output. This is typically most
529:   effective for position-independent executable.
530: 
531:   Note that a runtime loader has to support `.relr.dyn` to run executables or
532:   shared libraries linked with `--pack-dyn-relocs=relr`. As of 2022, only
533:   ChromeOS, Android and Fuchsia support it .
534: 
535: * `--package-metadata`=_string_:
536:   Embed _string_ to a `.note.package` section. This option in intended to be
537:   used by a package management command such as rpm(8) to embed metadata
538:   regarding a package to each executable file.
539: 
540: * `--pie`, `-pic-executable`, `--no-pie`, `-no-pic-executable`:
541:   Create a position-independent executable.
542: 
543: * `--print-gc-sections`, `--no-print-gc-sections`:
544:   Print removed unreferenced sections.
545: 
546: * `--print-icf-sections`, `--no-print-icf-sections`:
547:   Print folded identical sections.
548: 
549: * `--push-state`, `--pop-state`:
550:   `--push-state` saves the current values of `--as-needed`,
551:   `--whole-archive`, `--static`, and `--start-lib`. The saved values can be
552:   restored by `--pop-state`.
553: 
554:   `--push-state` and `--pop-state` pairs can nest.
555: 
556:   These options are useful when you want to construct linker command line
557:   options programmatically. For example, if you want to link `libfoo.so` by
558:   as-needed basis but don't want to change the global state of `--as-needed`,
559:   you can append `--push-state --as-needed -lfoo --pop-state` to the linker
560:   command line options.
561: 
562: * `--relax`, `--no-relax`:
563:   Rewrite machine instructions with more efficient ones for some
564:   relocations. The feature is enabled by default.
565: 
566: * `--require-defined`=_symbol_:
567:   Like `--undefined`, except the new symbol must be defined by the end of
568:   the link.
569: 
570: * `--retain-symbols-file`=_file_:
571:   Keep only symbols listed in _file_. _file_ is a text file containing a
572:   symbol name on each line. `mold` discards all local symbols as well as
573:   global symbol that are not in _file_. Note that this option removes symbols
574:   only from _.symtab_ section and does not affect _.dynsym_ section, which is
575:   used for dynamic linking.
576: 
577: * `--rpath`=_dir_:
578:   Add _dir_ to runtime search paths.
579: 
580: * `--section-start=`_section_=_address_:
581:   Set _address_ to _section_. _address_ is a hexadecimal number that may
582:   start with an optional `0x`.
583: 
584: * `--shared`, `-Bshareable`:
585:   Create a share library.
586: 
587: * `--spare-dynamic-tags`=_number_:
588:   Reserve the given _number_ of tags in _.dynamic_ section.
589: 
590: * `--start-lib`, `--end-lib`:
591:   Handle object files between `--start-lib` and `--end-lib` as if they were
592:   in an archive file. That means object files between them are linked only
593:   when they are needed to resolve undefined symbols. The options are useful
594:   if you want to link object files only when they are needed but want to
595:   avoid the overhead of running ar(3).
596: 
597: * `--static`:
598:   Do not link against shared libraries.
599: 
600: * `--sysroot`=_dir_:
601:   Set target system root directory to _dir_.
602: 
603: * `--trace`:
604:   Print name of each input file.
605: 
606: * `--undefined-version`, `--no-undefined-version`:
607:   By default, `mold` warns on a symbol specified by a version script or by
608:   `--export-dynamic-symbol` if it is not defined. You can silence the
609:   warning by `--undefined-version`.
610: 
611: * `--unique`=_pattern_:
612:   Don't merge input sections that match the given glob pattern _pattern_.
613: 
614: * `--unresolved-symbols`=[ `report-all` | `ignore-all` | `ignore-in-object-files` | `ignore-in-shared-libs` ]:
615:   How to handle undefined symbols.
616: 
617: * `--version-script`=_file_:
618:   Read version script from _file_. If _file_ does not exist in the current
619:   directory, it is searched from library search paths for the sake of
620:   compatibility with GNU ld.
621: 
622: * `--warn-common`, `--no-warn-common`:
623:   Warn about common symbols.
624: 
625: * `--warn-once`:
626:   Only warn once for each undefined symbol instead of warn for each
627:   relocation referring an undefined symbol.
628: 
629: * `--warn-unresolved-symbols`, `--error-unresolved-symbols`:
630:   Normally, the linker reports an error for unresolved symbols.
631:   `--warn-unresolved-symbols` option turns it into a warning.
632:   `--error-unresolved-symbols` option restores the default behavior.
633: 
634: * `--whole-archive`, `--no-whole-archive`:
635:   When archive files (`.a` files) are given to the linker, only object files
636:   that are needed to resolve undefined symbols are extracted from them and
637:   linked to an output file. `--whole-archive` changes that behavior for
638:   subsequent archives so that the linker extracts all object files and link
639:   them to an output. For example, if you are creating a shared object file
640:   and you want to include all archive members to the output, you should pass
641:   `--whole-archive`. `--no-whole-archive` restores the default behavior for
642:   subsequent archives.
643: 
644: * `--wrap`=_symbol_:
645:   Make _symbol_ to be resolved to `__wrap_`_symbol_. The original symbol can
646:   be resolved as `__real_`_symbol_. This option is typically used for
647:   wrapping an existing function.
648: 
649: * `-z cet-report`=[ `warning` | `error` | `none`]:
650:   Intel Control-flow Enforcement Technology (CET) is a new x86 feature
651:   available since Tiger Lake which is released in 2020. It defines new
652:   instructions to harden security to protect programs from control hijacking
653:   attacks. You can tell compiler to use the feature by specifying the
654:   `-fcf-protection` flag.
655: 
656:   `-z cet-report` flag is used to make sure that all object files were
657:   compiled with a correct `-fcf-protection` flag. If `warning` or `error`
658:   are given, `mold` prints out a warning or an error message if an object
659:   file was not compiled with the compiler flag.
660: 
661:   `mold` looks for `GNU_PROPERTY_X86_FEATURE_1_IBT` bit and
662:   `GNU_PROPERTY_X86_FEATURE_1_SHSTK` bit in `.note.gnu.property` section to
663:   determine whether or not an object file was compiled with
664:   `-fcf-protection`.
665: 
666: * `-z now`, `-z lazy`:
667:   By default, functions referring to other ELF modules are resolved by the
668:   dynamic linker when they are called for the first time. `-z now` marks an
669:   executable or a shared library file so that all dynamic symbols are resolved
670:   when a file is loaded to memory. `-z lazy` restores the default behavior.
671: 
672: * `-z origin`:
673:   Mark object requiring immediate `$ORIGIN` processing at runtime.
674: 
675: * `-z ibt`:
676:   Turn on `GNU_PROPERTY_X86_FEATURE_1_IBT` bit in `.note.gnu.property`
677:   section to indicate that the output uses IBT-enabled PLT. This option
678:   implies `-z ibtplt`.
679: 
680: * `-z ibtplt`:
681:   Generate Intel Branch Tracking (IBT)-enabled PLT which is the default on
682:   x86-64. This is the default.
683: 
684: * `-z execstack`, `-z noexecstack`:
685:   By default, the pages for the stack area (i.e. the pages where local
686:   variables reside) are not executable for security reasons. `-z execstack`
687:   makes it executable. `-z noexecstack` restores the default behavior.
688: 
689: * `-z keep-text-section-prefix`, `-z nokeep-text-section-prefix`:
690:   Keep `.text.hot`, `.text.unknown`, `.text.unlikely`, `.text.startup` and
691:   `.text.exit` as separate sections in the final binary instead of merging
692:   them as `.text`.
693: 
694: * `-z relro`, `-z norelro`:
695:   Some sections such as `.dynamic` have to be writable only during an
696:   executable or a shared library file is being loaded to memory. Once the
697:   dynamic linker finishes its job, such sections won't be mutated by anyone.
698:   As a security mitigation, it is preferred to make such segments read-only
699:   during program execution.
700: 
701:   `-z relro` puts such sections into a special segment called `relro`. The
702:   dynamic linker make a relro segment read-only after it finishes its job.
703: 
704:   By default, `mold` generates a relro segment. `-z norelro` disables the
705:   feature.
706: 
707: * `-z separate-loadable-segments`, `-z separate-code`, `-z noseparate-code`:
708:   If one memory page contains multiple segments, the page protection bits
709:   are set in such a way that needed attributes (writable or executable) are
710:   satisfied for all segments. This usually happens at a boundary of two
711:   segments with two different attributes.
712: 
713:   `separate-loadable-segments` adds paddings between segments with different
714:   attributes so that they do not share the same page. This is the default.
715: 
716:   `separate-code` adds paddings only between executable and non-executable
717:   segments.
718: 
719:   `noseparate-code` does not add any paddings between segments.
720: 
721: * `-z defs`, `-z nodefs`:
722:   Report undefined symbols (even with `--shared`).
723: 
724: * `-z shstk`:
725:   Enforce shadow stack by turning `GNU_PROPERTY_X86_FEATURE_1_SHSTK` bit in
726:   `.note.gnu.property` output section. Shadow stack is part of Intel
727:   Control-flow Enforcement Technology (CET), which is available since Tiger
728:   Lake (2020).
729: 
730: * `-z text`, `-z notext`, `-z textoff`:
731:   `mold` by default reports an error if dynamic relocations are created in
732:   read-only sections. If `-z notext` or `-z textoff` are given, `mold`
733:   creates such dynamic relocations without reporting an error. `-z text`
734:   restores the default behavior.
735: 
736: * `-z max-page-size`=_number_:
737:   Some CPU ISAs support multiple different memory page sizes. This option
738:   specifies the maximum page size that an output binary can run on. The
739:   default value is 4 KiB for i386, x86-64 and RISC-V, and 64 KiB for ARM64.
740: 
741: * `-z nodefaultlib`:
742:   Make the dynamic loader to ignore default search paths.
743: 
744: * `-z nodelete`:
745:   Mark DSO non-deletable at runtime.
746: 
747: * `-z nodlopen`:
748:   Mark DSO not available to dlopen(3). This option makes it possible for the
749:   linker to optimize thread-local variable accesses by rewriting
750:   instructions for some targets.
751: 
752: * `-z nodump`:
753:   Mark DSO not available to dldump(3).
754: 
755: * `-z nocopyreloc`:
756:   Do not create copy relocations.
757: 
758: * `-z initfirst`:
759:   Mark DSO to be initialized first at runtime.
760: 
761: * `-z interpose`:
762:   Mark object to interpose all DSOs but executable.
763: 
764: * `-(`, `-)`, `-EL`, `-O`_number_, `--allow-shlib-undefined`, `--dc`, `--dp`, `--end-group`, `--no-add-needed`, `--no-allow-shlib-undefined`, `--no-copy-dt-needed-entries`, `--no-fatal-warnings`, `--nostdlib`, `--rpath-link=Ar dir`, `--sort-common`, `--sort-section`, `--start-group`, `--warn-constructors`, `--warn-once`, `--fix-cortex-a53-835769`, `--fix-cortex-a53-843419`, `-z combreloc`, `-z common-page-size`, `-z nocombreloc`:
765:   Ignored
766: 
767: ## ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES
768: 
769: * `MOLD_DEBUG`:
770:   If this variable is set to a non-empty string, `mold` embeds its
771:   command-line options to the output file's `.comment` section.
772: 
773: * `MOLD_REPRO`
774:   Setting this variable to a non-empty string has the same effect as
775:   passign the `--repro` option.
776: 
777: ## SEE ALSO
778: 
779: gold(1), ld(1), elf(5), ld.so(8)
780: 
781: ## AUTHOR
782: 
783: Rui Ueyama <ruiu@cs.stanford.edu>
784: 
785: ## BUGS
786: 
787: Report bugs to <https://github.com/rui314/mold/issues>.

================
File: elf/arch-alpha.cc
================
  1: // Alpha is a 64-bit RISC ISA developed by DEC (Digital Equipment
  2: // Corporation) in the early '90s. It aimed to be an ISA that would last
  3: // 25 years. DEC expected Alpha would become 1000x faster during that time
  4: // span. Since the ISA was developed from scratch for future machines,
  5: // it's 64-bit from the beginning. There's no 32-bit variant.
  6: //
  7: // DEC ported its own Unix (Tru64) to Alpha. Microsoft also ported Windows
  8: // NT to it. But it wasn't a huge commercial success.
  9: //
 10: // DEC was acquired by Compaq in 1997. In the late '90s, Intel and
 11: // Hewlett-Packard were advertising that their upcoming Itanium processor
 12: // would achieve significantly better performance than RISC processors, so
 13: // Compaq decided to discontinue the Alpha processor line to switch to
 14: // Itanium. Itanium resulted in a miserable failure, but it still suceeded
 15: // to wipe out several RISC processors just by promising overly optimistic
 16: // perf numbers. Alpha as an ISA would probably have been fine after 25
 17: // years since its introduction (which is 1992 + 25 = 2017), but the
 18: // company and its market didn't last that long.
 19: //
 20: // From the linker's point of view, there are a few peculiarities in its
 21: // psABI as shown below:
 22: //
 23: //  - Alpha lacks PC-relative memory load/store instructions, so it uses
 24: //    register-relative load/store instructions in position-independent
 25: //    code. Specifically, GP (which is an alias for $r29) is always
 26: //    maintained to refer to .got+0x8000, and global variables' addresses
 27: //    are loaded in a GP-relative manner.
 28: //
 29: //  - It looks like even function addresses are first loaded to register
 30: //    in a GP-relative manner before calling it. We can relax it to
 31: //    convert the instruction sequence with a direct branch instruction,
 32: //    but by default, object files don't use a direct branch to call a
 33: //    function. Therefore, by default, we don't need to create a PLT.
 34: //    Any function call is made by first reading its address from GOT and
 35: //    jump to the address.
 36: 
 37: #include "mold.h"
 38: 
 39: namespace mold::elf {
 40: 
 41: using E = ALPHA;
 42: 
 43: // A 32-bit immediate can be materialized in a register with a "load high"
 44: // and a "load low" instruction sequence. The first instruction sets the
 45: // upper 16 bits in a register, and the second one set the lower 16
 46: // bits. When doing so, they sign-extend an immediate.  Therefore, if the
 47: // 15th bit of an immediate happens to be 1, setting a "low half" value
 48: // negates the upper 16 bit values that has already been set in a
 49: // register. To compensate that, we need to add 0x8000 when setting the
 50: // upper 16 bits.
 51: static u32 hi(u32 val) {
 52:   return bits(val + 0x8000, 31, 16);
 53: }
 54: 
 55: template <>
 56: void write_plt_header(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf) {}
 57: 
 58: template <>
 59: void write_plt_entry(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf, Symbol<E> &sym) {}
 60: 
 61: template <>
 62: void write_pltgot_entry(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf, Symbol<E> &sym) {}
 63: 
 64: template <>
 65: void EhFrameSection<E>::apply_reloc(Context<E> &ctx, const ElfRel<E> &rel,
 66:                                     u64 offset, u64 val) {
 67:   u8 *loc = ctx.buf + this->shdr.sh_offset + offset;
 68: 
 69:   switch (rel.r_type) {
 70:   case R_NONE:
 71:     break;
 72:   case R_ALPHA_SREL32:
 73:     *(ul32 *)loc = val - this->shdr.sh_addr - offset;
 74:     break;
 75:   default:
 76:     Fatal(ctx) << "unsupported relocation in .eh_frame: " << rel;
 77:   }
 78: }
 79: 
 80: template <>
 81: void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_alloc(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *base) {
 82:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
 83: 
 84:   ElfRel<E> *dynrel = nullptr;
 85:   if (ctx.reldyn)
 86:     dynrel = (ElfRel<E> *)(ctx.buf + ctx.reldyn->shdr.sh_offset +
 87:                            file.reldyn_offset + this->reldyn_offset);
 88: 
 89:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
 90:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
 91:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE)
 92:       continue;
 93: 
 94:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
 95:     u8 *loc = base + rel.r_offset;
 96: 
 97:     u64 S = sym.get_addr(ctx);
 98:     u64 A = rel.r_addend;
 99:     u64 P = get_addr() + rel.r_offset;
100:     u64 G = sym.get_got_idx(ctx) * sizeof(Word<E>);
101:     u64 GOT = ctx.got->shdr.sh_addr;
102:     u64 GP = ctx.got->shdr.sh_addr + 0x8000;
103: 
104:     switch (rel.r_type) {
105:     case R_ALPHA_REFQUAD:
106:       apply_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel, loc, S, A, P, dynrel);
107:       break;
108:     case R_ALPHA_GPREL32:
109:       *(ul32 *)loc = S + A - GP;
110:       break;
111:     case R_ALPHA_LITERAL:
112:       if (A)
113:         *(ul16 *)loc = ctx.extra.got->get_addr(sym, A) - GP;
114:       else
115:         *(ul16 *)loc = GOT + G - GP;
116:       break;
117:     case R_ALPHA_BRSGP:
118:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A - P - 4, 22, 0);
119:       break;
120:     case R_ALPHA_GPDISP:
121:       *(ul16 *)loc = hi(GP - P);
122:       *(ul16 *)(loc + A) = GP - P;
123:       break;
124:     case R_ALPHA_SREL32:
125:       *(ul32 *)loc = S + A - P;
126:       break;
127:     case R_ALPHA_GPRELHIGH:
128:       *(ul16 *)loc = hi(S + A - GP);
129:       break;
130:     case R_ALPHA_GPRELLOW:
131:       *(ul16 *)loc = S + A - GP;
132:       break;
133:     case R_ALPHA_TLSGD:
134:       *(ul16 *)loc = sym.get_tlsgd_addr(ctx) - GP;
135:       break;
136:     case R_ALPHA_TLSLDM:
137:       *(ul16 *)loc = ctx.got->get_tlsld_addr(ctx) - GP;
138:       break;
139:     case R_ALPHA_DTPRELHI:
140:       *(ul16 *)loc = hi(S + A - ctx.dtp_addr);
141:       break;
142:     case R_ALPHA_DTPRELLO:
143:       *(ul16 *)loc = S + A - ctx.dtp_addr;
144:       break;
145:     case R_ALPHA_GOTTPREL:
146:       *(ul16 *)loc = sym.get_gottp_addr(ctx) + A - GP;
147:       break;
148:     case R_ALPHA_TPRELHI:
149:       *(ul16 *)loc = hi(S + A - ctx.tp_addr);
150:       break;
151:     case R_ALPHA_TPRELLO:
152:       *(ul16 *)loc = S + A - ctx.tp_addr;
153:       break;
154:     case R_ALPHA_LITUSE:
155:     case R_ALPHA_HINT:
156:       break;
157:     default:
158:       unreachable();
159:     }
160:   }
161: }
162: 
163: template <>
164: void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_nonalloc(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *base) {
165:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
166: 
167:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
168:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
169:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE || record_undef_error(ctx, rel))
170:       continue;
171: 
172:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
173:     u8 *loc = base + rel.r_offset;
174: 
175:     SectionFragment<E> *frag;
176:     i64 frag_addend;
177:     std::tie(frag, frag_addend) = get_fragment(ctx, rel);
178: 
179:     u64 S = frag ? frag->get_addr(ctx) : sym.get_addr(ctx);
180:     u64 A = frag ? frag_addend : (i64)rel.r_addend;
181: 
182:     switch (rel.r_type) {
183:     case R_ALPHA_REFLONG:
184:       if (std::optional<u64> val = get_tombstone(sym, frag))
185:         *(ul32 *)loc = *val;
186:       else
187:         *(ul32 *)loc = S + A;
188:       break;
189:     case R_ALPHA_REFQUAD:
190:       if (std::optional<u64> val = get_tombstone(sym, frag))
191:         *(ul64 *)loc = *val;
192:       else
193:         *(ul64 *)loc = S + A;
194:       break;
195:     default:
196:       Fatal(ctx) << *this << ": invalid relocation for non-allocated sections: "
197:                  << rel;
198:     }
199:   }
200: }
201: 
202: template <>
203: void InputSection<E>::scan_relocations(Context<E> &ctx) {
204:   assert(shdr().sh_flags & SHF_ALLOC);
205: 
206:   this->reldyn_offset = file.num_dynrel * sizeof(ElfRel<E>);
207:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
208: 
209:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
210:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
211:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE || record_undef_error(ctx, rel))
212:       continue;
213: 
214:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
215: 
216:     if (sym.is_ifunc())
217:       Error(ctx) << sym << ": GNU ifunc symbol is not supported on Alpha";
218: 
219:     switch (rel.r_type) {
220:     case R_ALPHA_REFQUAD:
221:       scan_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel);
222:       break;
223:     case R_ALPHA_LITERAL:
224:       if (rel.r_addend)
225:         ctx.extra.got->add_symbol(sym, rel.r_addend);
226:       else
227:         sym.flags |= NEEDS_GOT;
228:       break;
229:     case R_ALPHA_SREL32:
230:       scan_pcrel(ctx, sym, rel);
231:       break;
232:     case R_ALPHA_BRSGP:
233:       if (sym.is_imported)
234:         sym.flags |= NEEDS_PLT;
235:       break;
236:     case R_ALPHA_TLSGD:
237:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_TLSGD;
238:       break;
239:     case R_ALPHA_TLSLDM:
240:       ctx.needs_tlsld = true;
241:       break;
242:     case R_ALPHA_GOTTPREL:
243:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_GOTTP;
244:       break;
245:     case R_ALPHA_TPRELHI:
246:     case R_ALPHA_TPRELLO:
247:       check_tlsle(ctx, sym, rel);
248:       break;
249:     case R_ALPHA_GPREL32:
250:     case R_ALPHA_LITUSE:
251:     case R_ALPHA_GPDISP:
252:     case R_ALPHA_HINT:
253:     case R_ALPHA_GPRELHIGH:
254:     case R_ALPHA_GPRELLOW:
255:     case R_ALPHA_DTPRELHI:
256:     case R_ALPHA_DTPRELLO:
257:       break;
258:     default:
259:       Fatal(ctx) << *this << ": unknown relocation: " << rel;
260:     }
261:   }
262: }
263: 
264: // An R_ALPHA_LITERAL relocation may request the linker to create a GOT
265: // entry for an external symbol with a non-zero addend. This is an unusual
266: // request which is not found in any other targets.
267: //
268: // Referring an external symbol with a non-zero addend is a bad practice
269: // because we need to create as many dynamic relocations as the number of
270: // distinctive addends for the same symbol.
271: //
272: // We don't want to mess up the implementation of the common GOT section
273: // for Alpha. So we create another GOT-like section, .alpha_got. Any GOT
274: // entry for an R_ALPHA_LITERAL reloc with a non-zero addend is created
275: // not in .got but in .alpha_got.
276: //
277: // Since .alpha_got entries are accessed relative to GP, .alpha_got
278: // needs to be close enough to .got. It's actually placed next to .got.
279: void AlphaGotSection::add_symbol(Symbol<E> &sym, i64 addend) {
280:   assert(addend);
281:   std::scoped_lock lock(mu);
282:   entries.push_back({&sym, addend});
283: }
284: 
285: bool operator<(const AlphaGotSection::Entry &a, const AlphaGotSection::Entry &b) {
286:   return std::tuple(a.sym->file->priority, a.sym->sym_idx, a.addend) <
287:          std::tuple(b.sym->file->priority, b.sym->sym_idx, b.addend);
288: };
289: 
290: u64 AlphaGotSection::get_addr(Symbol<E> &sym, i64 addend) {
291:   auto it = std::lower_bound(entries.begin(), entries.end(), Entry{&sym, addend});
292:   assert(it != entries.end());
293:   return this->shdr.sh_addr + (it - entries.begin()) * sizeof(Word<E>);
294: }
295: 
296: i64 AlphaGotSection::get_reldyn_size(Context<E> &ctx) const {
297:   i64 n = 0;
298:   for (const Entry &e : entries)
299:     if (e.sym->is_imported || (ctx.arg.pic && !e.sym->is_absolute()))
300:       n++;
301:   return n;
302: }
303: 
304: void AlphaGotSection::finalize() {
305:   sort(entries);
306:   remove_duplicates(entries);
307:   shdr.sh_size = entries.size() * sizeof(Word<E>);
308: }
309: 
310: void AlphaGotSection::copy_buf(Context<E> &ctx) {
311:   ElfRel<E> *dynrel = (ElfRel<E> *)(ctx.buf + ctx.reldyn->shdr.sh_offset +
312:                                     reldyn_offset);
313: 
314:   for (i64 i = 0; i < entries.size(); i++) {
315:     Entry &e = entries[i];
316:     u64 P = this->shdr.sh_addr + sizeof(Word<E>) * i;
317:     ul64 *buf = (ul64 *)(ctx.buf + this->shdr.sh_offset + sizeof(Word<E>) * i);
318: 
319:     if (e.sym->is_imported) {
320:       *buf = ctx.arg.apply_dynamic_relocs ? e.addend : 0;
321:       *dynrel++ = ElfRel<E>(P, E::R_ABS, e.sym->get_dynsym_idx(ctx), e.addend);
322:     } else {
323:       *buf = e.sym->get_addr(ctx) + e.addend;
324:       if (ctx.arg.pic && !e.sym->is_absolute())
325:         *dynrel++ = ElfRel<E>(P, E::R_RELATIVE, 0, *buf);
326:     }
327:   }
328: }
329: 
330: } // namespace mold::elf

================
File: elf/arch-arm32.cc
================
  1: // ARM32 is a bit special from the linker's viewpoint because ARM
  2: // processors support two different instruction encodings: Thumb and
  3: // ARM (in a narrower sense). Thumb instructions are either 16 bits or
  4: // 32 bits, while ARM instructions are all 32 bits. Feature-wise,
  5: // thumb is a subset of ARM, so not all ARM instructions are
  6: // representable in Thumb.
  7: //
  8: // ARM processors originally supported only ARM instructions. Thumb
  9: // instructions were later added to increase code density.
 10: //
 11: // ARM processors runs in either ARM mode or Thumb mode. The mode can
 12: // be switched using BX (branch and mode exchange)-family instructions.
 13: // We need to use that instructions to, for example, call a function
 14: // encoded in Thumb from a function encoded in ARM. Sometimes, the
 15: // linker even has to emit an interworking thunk code to switch mode.
 16: //
 17: // ARM instructions are aligned to 4 byte boundaries. Thumb are to 2
 18: // byte boundaries.
 19: //
 20: // You can distinguish Thumb functions from ARM functions by looking
 21: // at the least significant bit (LSB) of its "address". If LSB is 0,
 22: // it's ARM; otherwise, Thumb.
 23: //
 24: // For example, if a symbol `foo` is of type STT_FUNC and has value
 25: // 0x2001, `foo` is a function using Thumb instructions whose address
 26: // is 0x2000 (not 0x2001, as Thumb instructions are always 2-byte
 27: // aligned). Likewise, if a function pointer has value 0x2001, it
 28: // refers a Thumb function at 0x2000.
 29: //
 30: // https://github.com/ARM-software/abi-aa/blob/main/aaelf32/aaelf32.rst
 31: 
 32: #include "mold.h"
 33: 
 34: #include <tbb/parallel_for.h>
 35: #include <tbb/parallel_for_each.h>
 36: #include <tbb/parallel_sort.h>
 37: 
 38: namespace mold::elf {
 39: 
 40: using E = ARM32;
 41: 
 42: template <>
 43: i64 get_addend(u8 *loc, const ElfRel<E> &rel) {
 44:   switch (rel.r_type) {
 45:   case R_ARM_ABS32:
 46:   case R_ARM_REL32:
 47:   case R_ARM_TARGET1:
 48:   case R_ARM_BASE_PREL:
 49:   case R_ARM_GOTOFF32:
 50:   case R_ARM_GOT_PREL:
 51:   case R_ARM_GOT_BREL:
 52:   case R_ARM_TLS_GD32:
 53:   case R_ARM_TLS_LDM32:
 54:   case R_ARM_TLS_LDO32:
 55:   case R_ARM_TLS_IE32:
 56:   case R_ARM_TLS_LE32:
 57:   case R_ARM_TLS_GOTDESC:
 58:   case R_ARM_TARGET2:
 59:     return *(il32 *)loc;
 60:   case R_ARM_THM_JUMP11:
 61:     return sign_extend(*(ul16 *)loc, 10) << 1;
 62:   case R_ARM_THM_CALL:
 63:   case R_ARM_THM_JUMP24:
 64:   case R_ARM_THM_TLS_CALL: {
 65:     u32 S = bit(*(ul16 *)loc, 10);
 66:     u32 J1 = bit(*(ul16 *)(loc + 2), 13);
 67:     u32 J2 = bit(*(ul16 *)(loc + 2), 11);
 68:     u32 I1 = !(J1 ^ S);
 69:     u32 I2 = !(J2 ^ S);
 70:     u32 imm10 = bits(*(ul16 *)loc, 9, 0);
 71:     u32 imm11 = bits(*(ul16 *)(loc + 2), 10, 0);
 72:     u32 val = (S << 24) | (I1 << 23) | (I2 << 22) | (imm10 << 12) | (imm11 << 1);
 73:     return sign_extend(val, 24);
 74:   }
 75:   case R_ARM_CALL:
 76:   case R_ARM_JUMP24:
 77:   case R_ARM_PLT32:
 78:   case R_ARM_TLS_CALL:
 79:     return sign_extend(*(ul32 *)loc, 23) << 2;
 80:   case R_ARM_MOVW_PREL_NC:
 81:   case R_ARM_MOVW_ABS_NC:
 82:   case R_ARM_MOVT_PREL:
 83:   case R_ARM_MOVT_ABS: {
 84:     u32 imm12 = bits(*(ul32 *)loc, 11, 0);
 85:     u32 imm4 = bits(*(ul32 *)loc, 19, 16);
 86:     return sign_extend((imm4 << 12) | imm12, 15);
 87:   }
 88:   case R_ARM_PREL31:
 89:     return sign_extend(*(ul32 *)loc, 30);
 90:   case R_ARM_THM_MOVW_PREL_NC:
 91:   case R_ARM_THM_MOVW_ABS_NC:
 92:   case R_ARM_THM_MOVT_PREL:
 93:   case R_ARM_THM_MOVT_ABS: {
 94:     u32 imm4 = bits(*(ul16 *)loc, 3, 0);
 95:     u32 i = bit(*(ul16 *)loc, 10);
 96:     u32 imm3 = bits(*(ul16 *)(loc + 2), 14, 12);
 97:     u32 imm8 = bits(*(ul16 *)(loc + 2), 7, 0);
 98:     u32 val = (imm4 << 12) | (i << 11) | (imm3 << 8) | imm8;
 99:     return sign_extend(val, 15);
100:   }
101:   default:
102:     return 0;
103:   }
104: }
105: 
106: static void write_mov_imm(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
107:   u32 imm12 = bits(val, 11, 0);
108:   u32 imm4 = bits(val, 15, 12);
109:   *(ul32 *)loc = (*(ul32 *)loc & 0xfff0f000) | (imm4 << 16) | imm12;
110: }
111: 
112: static void write_thm_b_imm(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
113:   // https://developer.arm.com/documentation/ddi0406/cb/Application-Level-Architecture/Instruction-Details/Alphabetical-list-of-instructions/BL--BLX--immediate-
114:   u32 sign = bit(val, 24);
115:   u32 I1 = bit(val, 23);
116:   u32 I2 = bit(val, 22);
117:   u32 J1 = !I1 ^ sign;
118:   u32 J2 = !I2 ^ sign;
119:   u32 imm10 = bits(val, 21, 12);
120:   u32 imm11 = bits(val, 11, 1);
121: 
122:   ul16 *buf = (ul16 *)loc;
123:   buf[0] = (buf[0] & 0b1111'1000'0000'0000) | (sign << 10) | imm10;
124:   buf[1] = (buf[1] & 0b1101'0000'0000'0000) | (J1 << 13) | (J2 << 11) | imm11;
125: }
126: 
127: static void write_thm_mov_imm(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
128:   // https://developer.arm.com/documentation/ddi0406/cb/Application-Level-Architecture/Instruction-Details/Alphabetical-list-of-instructions/MOVT
129:   u32 imm4 = bits(val, 15, 12);
130:   u32 i = bit(val, 11);
131:   u32 imm3 = bits(val, 10, 8);
132:   u32 imm8 = bits(val, 7, 0);
133: 
134:   ul16 *buf = (ul16 *)loc;
135:   buf[0] = (buf[0] & 0b1111'1011'1111'0000) | (i << 10) | imm4;
136:   buf[1] = (buf[1] & 0b1000'1111'0000'0000) | (imm3 << 12) | imm8;
137: }
138: 
139: template <>
140: void write_addend(u8 *loc, i64 val, const ElfRel<E> &rel) {
141:   switch (rel.r_type) {
142:   case R_ARM_NONE:
143:     break;
144:   case R_ARM_ABS32:
145:   case R_ARM_REL32:
146:   case R_ARM_TARGET1:
147:   case R_ARM_BASE_PREL:
148:   case R_ARM_GOTOFF32:
149:   case R_ARM_GOT_PREL:
150:   case R_ARM_GOT_BREL:
151:   case R_ARM_TLS_GD32:
152:   case R_ARM_TLS_LDM32:
153:   case R_ARM_TLS_LDO32:
154:   case R_ARM_TLS_IE32:
155:   case R_ARM_TLS_LE32:
156:   case R_ARM_TLS_GOTDESC:
157:   case R_ARM_TARGET2:
158:     *(ul32 *)loc = val;
159:     break;
160:   case R_ARM_THM_JUMP11:
161:     *(ul16 *)loc = (*(ul16 *)loc & 0xf800) | bits(val, 11, 1);
162:     break;
163:   case R_ARM_THM_CALL:
164:   case R_ARM_THM_JUMP24:
165:   case R_ARM_THM_TLS_CALL:
166:     write_thm_b_imm(loc, val);
167:     break;
168:   case R_ARM_CALL:
169:   case R_ARM_JUMP24:
170:   case R_ARM_PLT32:
171:     *(ul32 *)loc = (*(ul32 *)loc & 0xff00'0000) | bits(val, 25, 2);
172:     break;
173:   case R_ARM_MOVW_PREL_NC:
174:   case R_ARM_MOVW_ABS_NC:
175:   case R_ARM_MOVT_PREL:
176:   case R_ARM_MOVT_ABS:
177:     write_mov_imm(loc, val);
178:     break;
179:   case R_ARM_PREL31:
180:     *(ul32 *)loc = (*(ul32 *)loc & 0x8000'0000) | (val & 0x7fff'ffff);
181:     break;
182:   case R_ARM_THM_MOVW_PREL_NC:
183:   case R_ARM_THM_MOVW_ABS_NC:
184:   case R_ARM_THM_MOVT_PREL:
185:   case R_ARM_THM_MOVT_ABS:
186:     write_thm_mov_imm(loc, val);
187:     break;
188:   default:
189:     unreachable();
190:   }
191: }
192: 
193: template <>
194: void write_plt_header(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf) {
195:   static const ul32 insn[] = {
196:     0xe52d'e004, // push {lr}
197:     0xe59f'e004, // ldr lr, 2f
198:     0xe08f'e00e, // 1: add lr, pc, lr
199:     0xe5be'f008, // ldr pc, [lr, #8]!
200:     0x0000'0000, // 2: .word .got.plt - 1b - 8
201:     0xe320'f000, // nop
202:     0xe320'f000, // nop
203:     0xe320'f000, // nop
204:   };
205: 
206:   memcpy(buf, insn, sizeof(insn));
207:   *(ul32 *)(buf + 16) = ctx.gotplt->shdr.sh_addr - ctx.plt->shdr.sh_addr - 16;
208: }
209: 
210: static const ul32 plt_entry[] = {
211:   0xe59f'c004, // 1: ldr ip, 2f
212:   0xe08c'c00f, // add ip, ip, pc
213:   0xe59c'f000, // ldr pc, [ip]
214:   0x0000'0000, // 2: .word sym@GOT - 1b
215: };
216: 
217: template <>
218: void write_plt_entry(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf, Symbol<E> &sym) {
219:   memcpy(buf, plt_entry, sizeof(plt_entry));
220:   *(ul32 *)(buf + 12) = sym.get_gotplt_addr(ctx) - sym.get_plt_addr(ctx) - 12;
221: }
222: 
223: template <>
224: void write_pltgot_entry(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf, Symbol<E> &sym) {
225:   memcpy(buf, plt_entry, sizeof(plt_entry));
226:   *(ul32 *)(buf + 12) = sym.get_got_addr(ctx) - sym.get_plt_addr(ctx) - 12;
227: }
228: 
229: // ARM does not use .eh_frame for exception handling. Instead, it uses
230: // .ARM.exidx and .ARM.extab. So this function is empty.
231: template <>
232: void EhFrameSection<E>::apply_reloc(Context<E> &ctx, const ElfRel<E> &rel,
233:                                     u64 offset, u64 val) {}
234: 
235: // ARM and Thumb branch instructions can jump within ±16 MiB.
236: static bool is_jump_reachable(i64 val) {
237:   return sign_extend(val, 24) == val;
238: }
239: 
240: template <>
241: void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_alloc(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *base) {
242:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
243: 
244:   ElfRel<E> *dynrel = nullptr;
245:   if (ctx.reldyn)
246:     dynrel = (ElfRel<E> *)(ctx.buf + ctx.reldyn->shdr.sh_offset +
247:                            file.reldyn_offset + this->reldyn_offset);
248: 
249:   std::span<std::unique_ptr<RangeExtensionThunk<E>>> thunks =
250:     output_section->thunks;
251: 
252:   auto get_tls_trampoline_addr = [&](u64 addr) {
253:     for (;;) {
254:       assert(!thunks.empty());
255:       i64 disp = output_section->shdr.sh_addr + thunks[0]->offset - addr;
256:       if (is_jump_reachable(disp))
257:         return disp;
258:       thunks = thunks.subspan(1);
259:     }
260:   };
261: 
262:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
263:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
264:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE || rel.r_type == R_ARM_V4BX)
265:       continue;
266: 
267:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
268:     u8 *loc = base + rel.r_offset;
269: 
270:     auto check = [&](i64 val, i64 lo, i64 hi) {
271:       if (val < lo || hi <= val)
272:         Error(ctx) << *this << ": relocation " << rel << " against "
273:                    << sym << " out of range: " << val << " is not in ["
274:                    << lo << ", " << hi << ")";
275:     };
276: 
277:     u64 S = sym.get_addr(ctx);
278:     u64 A = get_addend(*this, rel);
279:     u64 P = get_addr() + rel.r_offset;
280:     u64 T = S & 1;
281:     u64 G = sym.get_got_idx(ctx) * sizeof(Word<E>);
282:     u64 GOT = ctx.got->shdr.sh_addr;
283: 
284:     auto get_thumb_thunk_addr = [&] { return get_thunk_addr(i); };
285:     auto get_arm_thunk_addr   = [&] { return get_thunk_addr(i) + 4; };
286: 
287:     switch (rel.r_type) {
288:     case R_ARM_ABS32:
289:     case R_ARM_TARGET1:
290:       apply_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel, loc, S, A, P, dynrel);
291:       break;
292:     case R_ARM_REL32:
293:       *(ul32 *)loc = S + A - P;
294:       break;
295:     case R_ARM_THM_CALL: {
296:       if (sym.is_remaining_undef_weak()) {
297:         // On ARM, calling an weak undefined symbol jumps to the
298:         // next instruction.
299:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0x8000'f3af; // NOP.W
300:         break;
301:       }
302: 
303:       // THM_CALL relocation refers either BL or BLX instruction.
304:       // They are different in only one bit. We need to use BL if
305:       // the jump target is Thumb. Otherwise, use BLX.
306:       i64 val = S + A - P;
307:       if (is_jump_reachable(val)) {
308:         if (T) {
309:           write_thm_b_imm(loc, val);
310:           *(ul16 *)(loc + 2) |= 0x1000;  // rewrite to BL
311:         } else {
312:           write_thm_b_imm(loc, align_to(val, 4));
313:           *(ul16 *)(loc + 2) &= ~0x1000; // rewrite to BLX
314:         }
315:       } else {
316:         write_thm_b_imm(loc, align_to(get_arm_thunk_addr() + A - P, 4));
317:         *(ul16 *)(loc + 2) &= ~0x1000;  // rewrite to BLX
318:       }
319:       break;
320:     }
321:     case R_ARM_BASE_PREL:
322:       *(ul32 *)loc = GOT + A - P;
323:       break;
324:     case R_ARM_GOTOFF32:
325:       *(ul32 *)loc = ((S + A) | T) - GOT;
326:       break;
327:     case R_ARM_GOT_PREL:
328:     case R_ARM_TARGET2:
329:       *(ul32 *)loc = GOT + G + A - P;
330:       break;
331:     case R_ARM_GOT_BREL:
332:       *(ul32 *)loc = G + A;
333:       break;
334:     case R_ARM_CALL: {
335:       if (sym.is_remaining_undef_weak()) {
336:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0xe320'f000; // NOP
337:         break;
338:       }
339: 
340:       // Just like THM_CALL, ARM_CALL relocation refers either BL or
341:       // BLX instruction. We may need to rewrite BL → BLX or BLX → BL.
342:       bool is_bl = ((*(ul32 *)loc & 0xff00'0000) == 0xeb00'0000);
343:       bool is_blx = ((*(ul32 *)loc & 0xfe00'0000) == 0xfa00'0000);
344:       if (!is_bl && !is_blx)
345:         Fatal(ctx) << *this << ": R_ARM_CALL refers neither BL nor BLX";
346: 
347:       u64 val = S + A - P;
348:       if (is_jump_reachable(val)) {
349:         if (T) {
350:           *(ul32 *)loc = 0xfa00'0000; // BLX
351:           *(ul32 *)loc |= (bit(val, 1) << 24) | bits(val, 25, 2);
352:         } else {
353:           *(ul32 *)loc = 0xeb00'0000; // BL
354:           *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(val, 25, 2);
355:         }
356:       } else {
357:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0xeb00'0000; // BL
358:         *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(get_arm_thunk_addr() + A - P, 25, 2);
359:       }
360:       break;
361:     }
362:     case R_ARM_JUMP24: {
363:       if (sym.is_remaining_undef_weak()) {
364:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0xe320'f000; // NOP
365:         break;
366:       }
367: 
368:       // These relocs refers a B (unconditional branch) instruction.
369:       // Unlike BL or BLX, we can't rewrite B to BX in place when the
370:       // processor mode switch is required because BX doesn't takes an
371:       // immediate; it takes only a register. So if mode switch is
372:       // required, we jump to a linker-synthesized thunk which does the
373:       // job with a longer code sequence.
374:       u64 val = S + A - P;
375:       if (!is_jump_reachable(val) || T)
376:         val = get_arm_thunk_addr() + A - P;
377:       *(ul32 *)loc = (*(ul32 *)loc & 0xff00'0000) | bits(val, 25, 2);
378:       break;
379:     }
380:     case R_ARM_PLT32:
381:       if (sym.is_remaining_undef_weak()) {
382:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0xe320'f000; // NOP
383:       } else {
384:         u64 val = (T ? get_arm_thunk_addr() : S) + A - P;
385:         *(ul32 *)loc = (*(ul32 *)loc & 0xff00'0000) | bits(val, 25, 2);
386:       }
387:       break;
388:     case R_ARM_THM_JUMP11:
389:       assert(T);
390:       check(S + A - P, -(1 << 11), 1 << 11);
391:       *(ul16 *)loc &= 0xf800;
392:       *(ul16 *)loc |= bits(S + A - P, 11, 1);
393:       break;
394:     case R_ARM_THM_JUMP19: {
395:       i64 val = S + A - P;
396:       check(val, -(1 << 19), 1 << 19);
397: 
398:       // sign:J2:J1:imm6:imm11:'0'
399:       u32 sign = bit(val, 20);
400:       u32 J2 = bit(val, 19);
401:       u32 J1 = bit(val, 18);
402:       u32 imm6 = bits(val, 17, 12);
403:       u32 imm11 = bits(val, 11, 1);
404: 
405:       *(ul16 *)loc &= 0b1111'1011'1100'0000;
406:       *(ul16 *)loc |= (sign << 10) | imm6;
407: 
408:       *(ul16 *)(loc + 2) &= 0b1101'0000'0000'0000;
409:       *(ul16 *)(loc + 2) |= (J2 << 13) | (J1 << 11) | imm11;
410:       break;
411:     }
412:     case R_ARM_THM_JUMP24: {
413:       if (sym.is_remaining_undef_weak()) {
414:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0x8000'f3af; // NOP
415:         break;
416:       }
417: 
418:       // Just like R_ARM_JUMP24, we need to jump to a thunk if we need to
419:       // switch processor mode.
420:       u64 val = S + A - P;
421:       if (!is_jump_reachable(val) || !T)
422:         val = get_thumb_thunk_addr() + A - P;
423:       write_thm_b_imm(loc, val);
424:       break;
425:     }
426:     case R_ARM_MOVW_PREL_NC:
427:       write_mov_imm(loc, ((S + A) | T) - P);
428:       break;
429:     case R_ARM_MOVW_ABS_NC:
430:       write_mov_imm(loc, (S + A) | T);
431:       break;
432:     case R_ARM_THM_MOVW_PREL_NC:
433:       write_thm_mov_imm(loc, ((S + A) | T) - P);
434:       break;
435:     case R_ARM_PREL31:
436:       check(S + A - P, -(1LL << 30), 1LL << 30);
437:       *(ul32 *)loc &= 0x8000'0000;
438:       *(ul32 *)loc |= (S + A - P) & 0x7fff'ffff;
439:       break;
440:     case R_ARM_THM_MOVW_ABS_NC:
441:       write_thm_mov_imm(loc, (S + A) | T);
442:       break;
443:     case R_ARM_MOVT_PREL:
444:       write_mov_imm(loc, (S + A - P) >> 16);
445:       break;
446:     case R_ARM_THM_MOVT_PREL:
447:       write_thm_mov_imm(loc, (S + A - P) >> 16);
448:       break;
449:     case R_ARM_MOVT_ABS:
450:       write_mov_imm(loc, (S + A) >> 16);
451:       break;
452:     case R_ARM_THM_MOVT_ABS:
453:       write_thm_mov_imm(loc, (S + A) >> 16);
454:       break;
455:     case R_ARM_TLS_GD32:
456:       *(ul32 *)loc = sym.get_tlsgd_addr(ctx) + A - P;
457:       break;
458:     case R_ARM_TLS_LDM32:
459:       *(ul32 *)loc = ctx.got->get_tlsld_addr(ctx) + A - P;
460:       break;
461:     case R_ARM_TLS_LDO32:
462:       *(ul32 *)loc = S + A - ctx.dtp_addr;
463:       break;
464:     case R_ARM_TLS_IE32:
465:       *(ul32 *)loc = sym.get_gottp_addr(ctx) + A - P;
466:       break;
467:     case R_ARM_TLS_LE32:
468:       *(ul32 *)loc = S + A - ctx.tp_addr;
469:       break;
470:     case R_ARM_TLS_GOTDESC:
471:       if (sym.has_tlsdesc(ctx)) {
472:         // A is odd if the corresponding TLS_CALL is Thumb.
473:         if (A & 1)
474:           *(ul32 *)loc = sym.get_tlsdesc_addr(ctx) - P + A - 6;
475:         else
476:           *(ul32 *)loc = sym.get_tlsdesc_addr(ctx) - P + A - 4;
477:       } else {
478:         *(ul32 *)loc = S - ctx.tp_addr;
479:       }
480:       break;
481:     case R_ARM_TLS_CALL:
482:       if (sym.has_tlsdesc(ctx)) {
483:         // BL <tls_trampoline>
484:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0xeb00'0000 | bits(get_tls_trampoline_addr(P + 8), 25, 2);
485:       } else {
486:         // BL -> NOP
487:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0xe320'f000;
488:       }
489:       break;
490:     case R_ARM_THM_TLS_CALL:
491:       if (sym.has_tlsdesc(ctx)) {
492:         u64 val = align_to(get_tls_trampoline_addr(P + 4), 4);
493:         write_thm_b_imm(loc, val);
494:         *(ul16 *)(loc + 2) &= ~0x1000; // rewrite BL with BLX
495:       } else {
496:         // BL -> NOP.W
497:         *(ul32 *)loc = 0x8000'f3af;
498:       }
499:       break;
500:     default:
501:       Error(ctx) << *this << ": unknown relocation: " << rel;
502:     }
503:   }
504: }
505: 
506: template <>
507: void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_nonalloc(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *base) {
508:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
509: 
510:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
511:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
512:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE || record_undef_error(ctx, rel))
513:       continue;
514: 
515:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
516:     u8 *loc = base + rel.r_offset;
517: 
518:     SectionFragment<E> *frag;
519:     i64 frag_addend;
520:     std::tie(frag, frag_addend) = get_fragment(ctx, rel);
521: 
522:     u64 S = frag ? frag->get_addr(ctx) : sym.get_addr(ctx);
523:     u64 A = frag ? frag_addend : get_addend(*this, rel);
524: 
525:     switch (rel.r_type) {
526:     case R_ARM_ABS32:
527:       if (std::optional<u64> val = get_tombstone(sym, frag))
528:         *(ul32 *)loc = *val;
529:       else
530:         *(ul32 *)loc = S + A;
531:       break;
532:     case R_ARM_TLS_LDO32:
533:       if (std::optional<u64> val = get_tombstone(sym, frag))
534:         *(ul32 *)loc = *val;
535:       else
536:         *(ul32 *)loc = S + A - ctx.dtp_addr;
537:       break;
538:     default:
539:       Fatal(ctx) << *this << ": invalid relocation for non-allocated sections: "
540:                  << rel;
541:       break;
542:     }
543:   }
544: }
545: 
546: template <>
547: void InputSection<E>::scan_relocations(Context<E> &ctx) {
548:   assert(shdr().sh_flags & SHF_ALLOC);
549: 
550:   this->reldyn_offset = file.num_dynrel * sizeof(ElfRel<E>);
551:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
552: 
553:   // Scan relocations
554:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
555:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
556:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE || record_undef_error(ctx, rel))
557:       continue;
558: 
559:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
560: 
561:     if (sym.is_ifunc())
562:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_GOT | NEEDS_PLT;
563: 
564:     switch (rel.r_type) {
565:     case R_ARM_ABS32:
566:     case R_ARM_MOVT_ABS:
567:     case R_ARM_THM_MOVT_ABS:
568:     case R_ARM_TARGET1:
569:       scan_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel);
570:       break;
571:     case R_ARM_THM_CALL:
572:     case R_ARM_CALL:
573:     case R_ARM_JUMP24:
574:     case R_ARM_PLT32:
575:     case R_ARM_THM_JUMP24:
576:       if (sym.is_imported)
577:         sym.flags |= NEEDS_PLT;
578:       break;
579:     case R_ARM_GOT_PREL:
580:     case R_ARM_GOT_BREL:
581:     case R_ARM_TARGET2:
582:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_GOT;
583:       break;
584:     case R_ARM_MOVT_PREL:
585:     case R_ARM_THM_MOVT_PREL:
586:     case R_ARM_PREL31:
587:       scan_pcrel(ctx, sym, rel);
588:       break;
589:     case R_ARM_TLS_GD32:
590:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_TLSGD;
591:       break;
592:     case R_ARM_TLS_LDM32:
593:       ctx.needs_tlsld = true;
594:       break;
595:     case R_ARM_TLS_IE32:
596:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_GOTTP;
597:       break;
598:     case R_ARM_TLS_GOTDESC:
599:       if (!relax_tlsdesc(ctx, sym))
600:         sym.flags |= NEEDS_TLSDESC;
601:       break;
602:     case R_ARM_TLS_LE32:
603:       check_tlsle(ctx, sym, rel);
604:       break;
605:     case R_ARM_REL32:
606:     case R_ARM_BASE_PREL:
607:     case R_ARM_GOTOFF32:
608:     case R_ARM_THM_JUMP11:
609:     case R_ARM_THM_JUMP19:
610:     case R_ARM_MOVW_PREL_NC:
611:     case R_ARM_MOVW_ABS_NC:
612:     case R_ARM_THM_MOVW_PREL_NC:
613:     case R_ARM_THM_MOVW_ABS_NC:
614:     case R_ARM_TLS_LDO32:
615:     case R_ARM_TLS_CALL:
616:     case R_ARM_THM_TLS_CALL:
617:     case R_ARM_V4BX:
618:       break;
619:     default:
620:       Error(ctx) << *this << ": unknown relocation: " << rel;
621:     }
622:   }
623: }
624: 
625: template <>
626: void RangeExtensionThunk<E>::copy_buf(Context<E> &ctx) {
627:   u8 *buf = ctx.buf + output_section.shdr.sh_offset + offset;
628: 
629:   // TLS trampoline code. ARM32's TLSDESC is designed so that this
630:   // common piece of code is factored out from object files to reduce
631:   // output size. Since no one provide, the linker has to synthesize it.
632:   static ul32 hdr[] = {
633:     0xe08e'0000, // add r0, lr, r0
634:     0xe590'1004, // ldr r1, [r0, #4]
635:     0xe12f'ff11, // bx  r1
636:   };
637: 
638:   // This is a range extension and mode switch thunk.
639:   // It has two entry points: +0 for Thumb and +4 for ARM.
640:   const u8 entry[] = {
641:     // .thumb
642:     0xfc, 0x46,             //    mov  ip, pc
643:     0x60, 0x47,             //    bx   ip  # jumps to the following `ldr` insn
644:     // .arm
645:     0x04, 0xc0, 0x9f, 0xe5, //    ldr  ip, 2f
646:     0x0f, 0xc0, 0x8c, 0xe0, // 1: add  ip, ip, pc
647:     0x1c, 0xff, 0x2f, 0xe1, //    bx   ip
648:     0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, // 2: .word sym - 1b
649:   };
650: 
651:   static_assert(E::thunk_hdr_size == sizeof(hdr));
652:   static_assert(E::thunk_size == sizeof(entry));
653: 
654:   memcpy(buf, hdr, sizeof(hdr));
655: 
656:   for (i64 i = 0; i < symbols.size(); i++) {
657:     u8 *loc = buf + sizeof(hdr) + i * sizeof(entry);
658:     memcpy(loc, entry, sizeof(entry));
659: 
660:     u64 S = symbols[i]->get_addr(ctx);
661:     u64 P = output_section.shdr.sh_addr + offset + sizeof(hdr) + i * sizeof(entry);
662:     *(ul32 *)(loc + 16) = S - P - 16;
663:   }
664: }
665: 
666: // ARM executables use an .ARM.exidx section to look up an exception
667: // handling record for the current instruction pointer. The table needs
668: // to be sorted by their addresses.
669: //
670: // Other target uses .eh_frame_hdr instead for the same purpose.
671: // I don't know why only ARM uses the different mechanism, but it's
672: // likely that it's due to some historical reason.
673: //
674: // This function sorts .ARM.exidx records.
675: void fixup_arm_exidx_section(Context<E> &ctx) {
676:   Timer t(ctx, "fixup_arm_exidx_section");
677: 
678:   OutputSection<E> *osec = find_section(ctx, SHT_ARM_EXIDX);
679:   if (!osec)
680:     return;
681: 
682:   // .ARM.exidx records consists of a signed 31-bit relative address
683:   // and a 32-bit value. The relative address indicates the start
684:   // address of a function that the record covers. The value is one of
685:   // the followings:
686:   //
687:   // 1. CANTUNWIND indicating that there's no unwinding info for the function,
688:   // 2. a compact unwinding record encoded into a 32-bit value, or
689:   // 3. a 31-bit relative address which points to a larger record in
690:   //    the .ARM.extab section.
691:   //
692:   // CANTUNWIND is value 1. The most significant bit is set in (2) but
693:   // not in (3). So we can distinguished them just by looking at a value.
694:   const u32 EXIDX_CANTUNWIND = 1;
695: 
696:   struct Entry {
697:     ul32 addr;
698:     ul32 val;
699:   };
700: 
701:   if (osec->shdr.sh_size % sizeof(Entry))
702:     Fatal(ctx) << "invalid .ARM.exidx section size";
703: 
704:   Entry *ent = (Entry *)(ctx.buf + osec->shdr.sh_offset);
705:   i64 num_entries = osec->shdr.sh_size / sizeof(Entry);
706: 
707:   // Entry's addresses are relative to themselves. In order to sort
708:   // records by addresses, we first translate them so that the addresses
709:   // are relative to the beginning of the section.
710:   auto is_relative = [](u32 val) {
711:     return val != EXIDX_CANTUNWIND && !(val & 0x8000'0000);
712:   };
713: 
714:   tbb::parallel_for((i64)0, num_entries, [&](i64 i) {
715:     i64 offset = sizeof(Entry) * i;
716:     ent[i].addr = sign_extend(ent[i].addr, 30) + offset;
717:     if (is_relative(ent[i].val))
718:       ent[i].val = 0x7fff'ffff & (ent[i].val + offset);
719:   });
720: 
721:   tbb::parallel_sort(ent, ent + num_entries, [](const Entry &a, const Entry &b) {
722:     return a.addr < b.addr;
723:   });
724: 
725:   // Make addresses relative to themselves.
726:   tbb::parallel_for((i64)0, num_entries, [&](i64 i) {
727:     i64 offset = sizeof(Entry) * i;
728:     ent[i].addr = 0x7fff'ffff & (ent[i].addr - offset);
729:     if (is_relative(ent[i].val))
730:       ent[i].val = 0x7fff'ffff & (ent[i].val - offset);
731:   });
732: 
733:   // .ARM.exidx's sh_link should be set to the .text section index.
734:   // Runtime doesn't care about it, but the binutils's strip command does.
735:   if (ctx.shdr) {
736:     if (Chunk<E> *text = find_section(ctx, ".text")) {
737:       osec->shdr.sh_link = text->shndx;
738:       ctx.shdr->copy_buf(ctx);
739:     }
740:   }
741: }
742: 
743: } // namespace mold::elf

================
File: elf/arch-arm64.cc
================
  1: // This file contains ARM64-specific code. Being new, the ARM64's ELF
  2: // psABI doesn't have anything peculiar. ARM64 is a clean RISC
  3: // instruction set that supports PC-relative load/store instructions.
  4: //
  5: // Unlike ARM32, instructions length doesn't vary. All ARM64
  6: // instructions are 4 bytes long.
  7: //
  8: // Branch instructions used for function call can jump within ±128 MiB.
  9: // We need to create range extension thunks to support binaries whose
 10: // .text is larger than that.
 11: //
 12: // Unlike most other targets, the TLSDESC access model is used by default
 13: // for -fPIC to access thread-local variables instead of the less
 14: // efficient GD model. You can still enable GD but it needs the
 15: // -mtls-dialect=trad flag. Since GD is used rarely, we don't need to
 16: // implement GD → LE relaxation.
 17: //
 18: // https://github.com/ARM-software/abi-aa/blob/main/aaelf64/aaelf64.rst
 19: 
 20: #include "mold.h"
 21: 
 22: namespace mold::elf {
 23: 
 24: using E = ARM64;
 25: 
 26: static void write_adrp(u8 *buf, u64 val) {
 27:   *(ul32 *)buf |= (bits(val, 13, 12) << 29) | (bits(val, 32, 14) << 5);
 28: }
 29: 
 30: static void write_adr(u8 *buf, u64 val) {
 31:   *(ul32 *)buf |= (bits(val, 1, 0) << 29) | (bits(val, 20, 2) << 5);
 32: }
 33: 
 34: static void write_movn_movz(u8 *buf, i64 val) {
 35:   *(ul32 *)buf &= 0b0000'0000'0110'0000'0000'0000'0001'1111;
 36: 
 37:   if (val >= 0)
 38:     *(ul32 *)buf |= 0xd280'0000 | (bits(val, 15, 0) << 5);  // rewrite to movz
 39:   else
 40:     *(ul32 *)buf |= 0x9280'0000 | (bits(~val, 15, 0) << 5); // rewrite to movn
 41: }
 42: 
 43: static u64 page(u64 val) {
 44:   return val & 0xffff'ffff'ffff'f000;
 45: }
 46: 
 47: template <>
 48: void write_plt_header(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf) {
 49:   static const ul32 insn[] = {
 50:     0xa9bf'7bf0, // stp  x16, x30, [sp,#-16]!
 51:     0x9000'0010, // adrp x16, .got.plt[2]
 52:     0xf940'0211, // ldr  x17, [x16, .got.plt[2]]
 53:     0x9100'0210, // add  x16, x16, .got.plt[2]
 54:     0xd61f'0220, // br   x17
 55:     0xd503'201f, // nop
 56:     0xd503'201f, // nop
 57:     0xd503'201f, // nop
 58:   };
 59: 
 60:   u64 gotplt = ctx.gotplt->shdr.sh_addr + 16;
 61:   u64 plt = ctx.plt->shdr.sh_addr;
 62: 
 63:   memcpy(buf, insn, sizeof(insn));
 64:   write_adrp(buf + 4, page(gotplt) - page(plt + 4));
 65:   *(ul32 *)(buf + 8) |= bits(gotplt, 11, 3) << 10;
 66:   *(ul32 *)(buf + 12) |= (gotplt & 0xfff) << 10;
 67: }
 68: 
 69: template <>
 70: void write_plt_entry(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf, Symbol<E> &sym) {
 71:   static const ul32 insn[] = {
 72:     0x9000'0010, // adrp x16, .got.plt[n]
 73:     0xf940'0211, // ldr  x17, [x16, .got.plt[n]]
 74:     0x9100'0210, // add  x16, x16, .got.plt[n]
 75:     0xd61f'0220, // br   x17
 76:   };
 77: 
 78:   u64 gotplt = sym.get_gotplt_addr(ctx);
 79:   u64 plt = sym.get_plt_addr(ctx);
 80: 
 81:   memcpy(buf, insn, sizeof(insn));
 82:   write_adrp(buf, page(gotplt) - page(plt));
 83:   *(ul32 *)(buf + 4) |= bits(gotplt, 11, 3) << 10;
 84:   *(ul32 *)(buf + 8) |= (gotplt & 0xfff) << 10;
 85: }
 86: 
 87: template <>
 88: void write_pltgot_entry(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf, Symbol<E> &sym) {
 89:   static const ul32 insn[] = {
 90:     0x9000'0010, // adrp x16, GOT[n]
 91:     0xf940'0211, // ldr  x17, [x16, GOT[n]]
 92:     0xd61f'0220, // br   x17
 93:     0xd503'201f, // nop
 94:   };
 95: 
 96:   u64 got = sym.get_got_addr(ctx);
 97:   u64 plt = sym.get_plt_addr(ctx);
 98: 
 99:   memcpy(buf, insn, sizeof(insn));
100:   write_adrp(buf, page(got) - page(plt));
101:   *(ul32 *)(buf + 4) |= bits(got, 11, 3) << 10;
102: }
103: 
104: template <>
105: void EhFrameSection<E>::apply_reloc(Context<E> &ctx, const ElfRel<E> &rel,
106:                                     u64 offset, u64 val) {
107:   u8 *loc = ctx.buf + this->shdr.sh_offset + offset;
108: 
109:   switch (rel.r_type) {
110:   case R_NONE:
111:     break;
112:   case R_AARCH64_ABS64:
113:     *(ul64 *)loc = val;
114:     break;
115:   case R_AARCH64_PREL32:
116:     *(ul32 *)loc = val - this->shdr.sh_addr - offset;
117:     break;
118:   case R_AARCH64_PREL64:
119:     *(ul64 *)loc = val - this->shdr.sh_addr - offset;
120:     break;
121:   default:
122:     Fatal(ctx) << "unsupported relocation in .eh_frame: " << rel;
123:   }
124: }
125: 
126: static bool is_adrp(u8 *loc) {
127:   // https://developer.arm.com/documentation/ddi0596/2021-12/Base-Instructions/ADRP--Form-PC-relative-address-to-4KB-page-
128:   u32 insn = *(ul32 *)loc;
129:   return (bits(insn, 31, 24) & 0b1001'1111) == 0b1001'0000;
130: }
131: 
132: static bool is_ldr(u8 *loc) {
133:   // https://developer.arm.com/documentation/ddi0596/2021-12/Base-Instructions/LDR--immediate---Load-Register--immediate--
134:   u32 insn = *(ul32 *)loc;
135:   return (bits(insn, 31, 20) & 0b1111'1111'1100) == 0b1111'1001'0100;
136: }
137: 
138: static bool is_add(u8 *loc) {
139:   // https://developer.arm.com/documentation/ddi0596/2021-12/Base-Instructions/ADD--immediate---Add--immediate--
140:   u32 insn = *(ul32 *)loc;
141:   return (bits(insn, 31, 20) & 0b1111'1111'1100) == 0b1001'0001'0000;
142: }
143: 
144: template <>
145: void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_alloc(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *base) {
146:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
147: 
148:   ElfRel<E> *dynrel = nullptr;
149:   if (ctx.reldyn)
150:     dynrel = (ElfRel<E> *)(ctx.buf + ctx.reldyn->shdr.sh_offset +
151:                            file.reldyn_offset + this->reldyn_offset);
152: 
153:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
154:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
155:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE)
156:       continue;
157: 
158:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
159:     u8 *loc = base + rel.r_offset;
160: 
161:     auto check = [&](i64 val, i64 lo, i64 hi) {
162:       if (val < lo || hi <= val)
163:         Error(ctx) << *this << ": relocation " << rel << " against "
164:                    << sym << " out of range: " << val << " is not in ["
165:                    << lo << ", " << hi << ")";
166:     };
167: 
168:     u64 S = sym.get_addr(ctx);
169:     u64 A = rel.r_addend;
170:     u64 P = get_addr() + rel.r_offset;
171:     u64 G = sym.get_got_idx(ctx) * sizeof(Word<E>);
172:     u64 GOT = ctx.got->shdr.sh_addr;
173: 
174:     switch (rel.r_type) {
175:     case R_AARCH64_ABS64:
176:       apply_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel, loc, S, A, P, dynrel);
177:       break;
178:     case R_AARCH64_LDST8_ABS_LO12_NC:
179:     case R_AARCH64_ADD_ABS_LO12_NC:
180:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 11, 0) << 10;
181:       break;
182:     case R_AARCH64_LDST16_ABS_LO12_NC:
183:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 11, 1) << 10;
184:       break;
185:     case R_AARCH64_LDST32_ABS_LO12_NC:
186:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 11, 2) << 10;
187:       break;
188:     case R_AARCH64_LDST64_ABS_LO12_NC:
189:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 11, 3) << 10;
190:       break;
191:     case R_AARCH64_LDST128_ABS_LO12_NC:
192:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 11, 4) << 10;
193:       break;
194:     case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G0:
195:       check(S + A, 0, 1 << 16);
196:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 15, 0) << 5;
197:       break;
198:     case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G0_NC:
199:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 15, 0) << 5;
200:       break;
201:     case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G1:
202:       check(S + A, 0, 1LL << 32);
203:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 31, 16) << 5;
204:       break;
205:     case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G1_NC:
206:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 31, 16) << 5;
207:       break;
208:     case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G2:
209:       check(S + A, 0, 1LL << 48);
210:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 47, 32) << 5;
211:       break;
212:     case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G2_NC:
213:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 47, 32) << 5;
214:       break;
215:     case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G3:
216:       *(ul32 *)loc |= bits(S + A, 63, 48) << 5;
217:       break;
218:     case R_AARCH64_ADR_GOT_PAGE:
219:       if (sym.has_got(ctx)) {
220:         i64 val = page(G + GOT + A) - page(P);
221:         check(val, -(1LL << 32), 1LL << 32);
222:         write_adrp(loc, val);
223:       } else {
224:         // Relax GOT-loading ADRP+LDR to an immediate ADRP+ADD
225:         i64 val = page(S + A) - page(P);
226:         check(val, -(1LL << 32), 1LL << 32);
227:         write_adrp(loc, val);
228: 
229:         u32 reg = bits(*(ul32 *)loc, 4, 0);
230:         *(ul32 *)(loc + 4) = 0x9100'0000 | (reg << 5) | reg; // ADD
231:         *(ul32 *)(loc + 4) |= bits(S + A, 11, 0) << 10;
232:         i++;
233:       }
234:       break;
235:     case R_AARCH64_ADR_PREL_PG_HI21: {
236:       // The ARM64 psABI defines that an `ADRP x0, foo` and `ADD x0, x0,
237:       // :lo12: foo` instruction pair to materialize a PC-relative address
238:       // in a register can be relaxed to `NOP` followed by `ADR x0, foo`
239:       // if foo is in PC ± 1 MiB.
240:       if (ctx.arg.relax && i + 1 < rels.size() &&
241:           sign_extend(S + A - P - 4, 20) == S + A - P - 4) {
242:         const ElfRel<E> &rel2 = rels[i + 1];
243:         if (rel2.r_type == R_AARCH64_ADD_ABS_LO12_NC &&
244:             rel2.r_sym == rel.r_sym &&
245:             rel2.r_offset == rel.r_offset + 4 &&
246:             rel2.r_addend == rel.r_addend &&
247:             is_adrp(loc) &&
248:             is_add(loc + 4)) {
249:           u32 reg1 = bits(*(ul32 *)loc, 4, 0);
250:           u32 reg2 = bits(*(ul32 *)(loc + 4), 4, 0);
251:           if (reg1 == reg2) {
252:             *(ul32 *)loc = 0xd503'201f;              // nop
253:             *(ul32 *)(loc + 4) = 0x1000'0000 | reg1; // adr
254:             write_adr(loc + 4, S + A - P - 4);
255:             i++;
256:             break;
257:           }
258:         }
259:       }
260: 
261:       i64 val = page(S + A) - page(P);
262:       check(val, -(1LL << 32), 1LL << 32);
263:       write_adrp(loc, val);
264:       break;
265:     }
266:     case R_AARCH64_ADR_PREL_LO21:
267:       check(S + A - P, -(1LL << 20), 1LL << 20);
268:       write_adr(loc, S + A - P);
269:       break;
270:     case R_AARCH64_CALL26:
271:     case R_AARCH64_JUMP26: {
272:       if (sym.is_remaining_undef_weak()) {
273:         // On ARM, calling an weak undefined symbol jumps to the
