File: CONTRIBUTING.md
================
 1: # Contributing to mold
 2: 
 3: We request all patches to mold to be released under the dual license
 4: of the GNU AGPLv3 and the MIT license. Please read the following and
 5: sign-off your patch as an indication of agreement.
 6: 
 7: > ## Developer's Certificate of Origin
 8: >
 9: > By making a contribution to this project, I certify that:
10: >
11: >  1. The contribution was created in whole or in part by me and I
12: >     have the right to submit it under the Dual License of the GNU
13: >     AGPLv3 and the MIT license; or
14: >
15: >  2. The contribution is based upon previous work that, to the best
16: >     of my knowledge, is covered under the the Dual License and I
17: >     have the right under the Dual License to submit that work with
18: >     modifications, whether created in whole or in part by me, under
19: >     the Dual License (unless I am permitted to submit under
20: >     different licenses), as indicated in the file; or
21: >
22: >  3. The contribution was provided directly to me by some other
23: >     person who certified (1), (2) or (3) and I have not modified it.
24: >
25: >  4. I understand and agree that this project and the contribution
26: >     are public and that a record of the contribution (including all
27: >     personal information I submit with it, including my sign-off) is
28: >     maintained indefinitely and may be redistributed consistent with
29: >     this project or the open source license(s) involved.
30: 
31: If you agree, you just add a line saying:
32: 
33: ```
34: Signed-off-by: Random J Developer <random@developer.example.org>
35: ```
36: 
37: using your real name (sorry, no pseudonyms or anonymous contributions.)
38: This will be done for you automatically if you use ``git commit -s``.
39: Reverts should also include "Signed-off-by". ``git revert -s`` does that
40: for you.
41: 
42: We assume that any contribution that's 10 lines or less does not meet
43: the threshold of originality and therefore copyright does not apply.
44: You don't need to add a "Signed-off-by" line to such small patches.

================
File: install-build-deps.sh
================
 1: #!/bin/sh
 2: set -e
 3: . /etc/os-release
 4: 
 5: set -x
 6: 
 7: # The first line for each distro installs a build dependency.
 8: # The second line installs extra packages for unittests.
 9: #
10: # Feel free to send me a PR if your OS is not on this list.
11: 
12: case "$ID-$VERSION_ID" in
13: ubuntu-20.* | pop-20.*)
14:   apt-get update
15:   apt-get install -y cmake libssl-dev zlib1g-dev gcc g++ g++-10
16:   apt-get install -y file
17:   ;;
18: ubuntu-* | pop-* | linuxmint-* | debian-* | raspbian-*)
19:   apt-get update
20:   apt-get install -y cmake libssl-dev zlib1g-dev gcc g++
21:   apt-get install -y file
22:   ;;
23: fedora-*)
24:   dnf install -y gcc-g++ cmake openssl-devel zlib-devel
25:   dnf install -y glibc-static file libstdc++-static diffutils util-linux
26:   ;;
27: opensuse-leap-*)
28:   zypper install -y make cmake zlib-devel libopenssl-devel gcc-c++ gcc11-c++
29:   zypper install -y glibc-devel-static tar diffutils util-linux
30:   ;;
31: opensuse-tumbleweed-*)
32:   zypper install -y make cmake zlib-devel libopenssl-devel gcc-c++
33:   zypper install -y glibc-devel-static tar diffutils util-linux
34:   ;;
35: gentoo-*)
36:   emerge-webrsync
37:   emerge dev-util/cmake sys-libs/zlib
38:   ;;
39: arch-*)
40:   pacman -Sy
41:   pacman -S --needed --noconfirm base-devel zlib openssl cmake util-linux
42:   ;;
43: void-*)
44:   xbps-install -Sy xbps
45:   xbps-install -Sy bash make cmake openssl-devel zlib-devel gcc
46:   xbps-install -Sy tar diffutils util-linux
47:   ;;
48: *)
49:   echo "Error: don't know anything about build dependencies on $ID-$VERSION_ID"
50:   exit 1
51: esac

================
File: docs/macho-x86_64-compat-plan.md
================
  1: # Mach-O x86_64 Compatibility Plan
  2: 
  3: ## Current Status
  4: 
  5: This repository already contains a substantial Mach-O linker. In sold builds,
  6: `LICENSE.md` enables the Mach-O path, CMake instantiates Mach-O targets for
  7: `X86_64`, `ARM64`, and `ARM64_32`, and test builds create a real `ld64`
  8: entrypoint in the build tree. The implementation under `macho/` already
  9: covers:
 10: 
 11: - ld64-style command-line parsing
 12: - object, archive, universal binary, dylib, framework, and `.tbd` ingestion
 13: - symbol resolution and dead stripping
 14: - relocation scanning and target-specific thunks
 15: - LTO integration
 16: - synthetic chunk construction and final output writing
 17: - bind/rebase/export metadata, fixup chains, code signatures, unwind info,
 18:   stubs, GOT, and TLS-related chunks
 19: 
 20: The practical meaning is that the shortest path to "fully functional on macOS
 21: x86_64" is not a redesign. It is a compatibility burn-down with stricter
 22: validation on real Intel hardware.
 23: 
 24: ## What Works Today
 25: 
 26: - The Mach-O path is already live behind the `ld64` entrypoint.
 27: - The Rust-first benchmark/replay harness under `benchmarks/` is usable on
 28:   macOS and currently targets x86_64 replay.
 29: - The active replay corpus in `benchmarks/corpus.toml` contains two real local
 30:   Rust executables: `forge` and `tools-mcp`.
 31: - The current local replay-only baseline in
 32:   `benchmarks/out-direct-replay/results.json` shows sold successfully replaying
 33:   both targets through a real `ld64` entrypoint and passing `file`, `lipo`,
 34:   `otool`, and target smoke checks.
 35: - The current replay-only timings are a bring-up baseline, not a stable
 36:   performance claim:
 37:   - `forge`: Apple `0.31s`, sold `0.60s`
 38:   - `tools-mcp`: Apple `0.22s`, sold `0.32s`
 39: 
 40: Recent bring-up fixes also matter for interpreting current status:
 41: 
 42: - `macho/input-files.cc` now falls back to `find_subsection(ctx, msym.value)`
 43:   for `N_SECT` symbols when `sym_to_subsec[i]` is null. This is required for
 44:   always-split sections such as `__cstring`.
 45: - Defined `private extern` symbols must remain resolvable within the current
 46:   link. They are module-local for export visibility, not fully file-local.
 47: - Recent command-line and autolink compatibility fixes now cover
 48:   `-fatal_warnings`, the `-macosx_version_min` alias, both
 49:   `-needed_framework` and `-needed-framework`, and the common
 50:   `LC_LINKER_OPTION` autolink forms without treating every unfamiliar embedded
 51:   directive as a hard fatal error.
 52: 
 53: ## Phase 1 Definition Of Done
 54: 
 55: Phase 1 should stay narrow:
 56: 
 57: - Host: a real Intel Mac running macOS
 58: - Outputs: x86_64 Mach-O main executables
 59: - Inputs: real Rust/Cargo workloads first, then focused handwritten fixtures
 60: - Success criteria: correct runtime behavior, not just successful linking
 61: 
 62: The source of truth for this phase should be differential validation against
 63: Apple `ld`, using the existing replay harness plus targeted shell tests.
 64: 
 65: ## Highest-Leverage Compatibility Work
 66: 
 67: ### 1. Command-line Fidelity
 68: 
 69: The fastest wins are in `macho/cmdline.cc`. The remaining work is not to make
 70: the linker more feature-rich in the abstract, but to match the option spellings
 71: and behaviors emitted by real Apple toolchains and drivers. Each option seen in
 72: captured invocations should be classified as one of:
 73: 
 74: - fully implemented
 75: - accepted and ignored
 76: - accepted with warning
 77: - hard error because semantics matter
 78: 
 79: Unsupported but harmless flags should not abort the link unless Apple `ld`
 80: would also abort.
 81: 
 82: ### 2. Autolinking And Embedded Link Directives
 83: 
 84: `LC_LINKER_OPTION` handling is a first-order compatibility surface. Modern
 85: Apple toolchains do emit autolink directives, so the linker must accept the
 86: common forms seen in real captures and avoid treating every unfamiliar embedded
 87: option as a fatal error. The initial goal is compatibility with captured Rust
 88: and mixed-toolchain workloads, not exhaustive support for every obscure option.
 89: 
 90: ### 3. Runtime-Correctness Corners
 91: 
 92: Unwind and metadata correctness should be treated as bring-up blockers, not as
 93: late polish:
 94: 
 95: - `__compact_unwind`
 96: - `__eh_frame`
 97: - personality references and LSDA
 98: - panic/unwind across crates
 99: - mixed Rust/C++ binaries
100: - deployment-target-sensitive behaviors such as chained fixups and
101:   `__init_offsets`
102: 
103: These are exactly the cases that tend to fail after simple binaries already
104: look healthy.
105: 
106: ## Validation Strategy
107: 
108: The current harness already provides the right backbone:
109: 
110: - capture Cargo's real linker-driver argv
111: - derive the final Apple `ld` invocation via `clang -###`
112: - replay the same link against Apple `ld` and sold
113: - validate the output with `file`, `lipo`, `otool`, and a per-target smoke
114:   command
115: 
116: For x86_64 bring-up, validation should be tightened in this order:
117: 
118: 1. Keep growing the replay corpus from real Rust workloads.
119: 2. Add focused shell fixtures for dylibs, bundles, frameworks, weak/reexported
120:    libraries, TLV, dead strip, order files, LTO, and unwind-heavy binaries.
121: 3. Require Intel-host regression gates: `ctest`, replay corpus, and
122:    differential checks against Apple `ld`.
123: 4. Treat arm64-host x86_64 test coverage as supplemental signal, not the main
124:    gate.
125: 
126: ## Priority Order
127: 
128: If choosing only a few near-term items, do them in this order:
129: 
130: 1. Fix parser and option-compatibility bugs in `macho/cmdline.cc`.
131: 2. Make `LC_LINKER_OPTION` handling accept the common autolink forms from real
132:    captures.
133: 3. Turn real Rust captures into required x86_64 regression cases.
134: 4. Add unwind-focused regression binaries.
135: 5. Only after correctness is stable, use the replay harness to rank
136:    performance hotspots.
137: 
138: ## Bottom Line
139: 
140: The current sold tree is already well past the "stub linker" stage for Mach-O.
141: The remaining work to make x86_64 dependable on macOS is primarily
142: compatibility and validation discipline: tighter ld64 option fidelity, broader
143: autolink coverage, deeper unwind/runtime tests, and real Intel-host regression
144: gates.

================
File: elf/arch-riscv.cc
================
  1: // RISC-V is a clean RISC ISA. It supports PC-relative load/store for
  2: // position-independent code. Its 32-bit and 64-bit ISAs are almost
  3: // identical. That is, you can think RV32 as a RV64 without 64-bit
  4: // operations. In this file, we support both RV64 and RV32.
  5: //
  6: // RISC-V is essentially little-endian, but the big-endian version is
  7: // available as an extension. GCC supports `-mbig-endian` to generate
  8: // big-endian code. Even in big-endian mode, machine instructions are
  9: // defined to be encoded in little-endian, though. Only the behavior of
 10: // load/store instructions are different between LE RISC-V and BE RISC-V.
 11: //
 12: // From the linker's point of view, the RISC-V's psABI is unique because
 13: // sections in input object files can be shrunk while being copied to the
 14: // output file. That is contrary to other psABIs in which sections are an
 15: // atomic unit of copying. Let me explain it in more details.
 16: //
 17: // Since RISC-V instructions are 16-bit or 32-bit long, there's no way to
 18: // embed a very large immediate into a branch instruction. In fact, JAL
 19: // (jump and link) instruction can jump to only within PC ± 1 MiB because
 20: // its immediate is only 21 bits long. If the destination is out of its
 21: // reach, we need to use two instructions instead; the first instruction
 22: // being AUIPC which sets upper 20 bits to a register and the second being
 23: // JALR with a 12-bit immediate and the register. Combined, they specify a
 24: // 32 bits displacement.
 25: //
 26: // Other RISC ISAs have the same limitation, and they solved the problem by
 27: // letting the linker create so-called "range extension thunks". It works as
 28: // follows: the compiler optimistically emits single jump instructions for
 29: // function calls. If the linker finds that a branch target is out of reach,
 30: // it emits a small piece of machine code near the branch instruction and
 31: // redirect the branch to the linker-synthesized code. The code constructs a
 32: // full 32-bit address in a register and jump to the destination. That
 33: // linker-synthesized code is called "range extension thunks" or just
 34: // "thunks".
 35: //
 36: // The RISC-V psABI is unique that it works the other way around. That is,
 37: // for RISC-V, the compiler always emits two instructions (AUIPC + JAL) for
 38: // function calls. If the linker finds the destination is reachable with a
 39: // single instruction, it replaces the two instructions with the one and
 40: // shrink the section size by one instruction length, instead of filling the
 41: // gap with a nop.
 42: //
 43: // With the presence of this relaxation, sections can no longer be
 44: // considered as an atomic unit. If we delete 4 bytes from the middle of a
 45: // section, all contents after that point needs to be shifted by 4. Symbol
 46: // values and relocation offsets have to be adjusted accordingly if they
 47: // refer to past the deleted bytes.
 48: //
 49: // In mold, we use `r_deltas` to memorize how many bytes have be adjusted
 50: // for relocations. For symbols, we directly mutate their `value` member.
 51: //
 52: // RISC-V object files tend to have way more relocations than those for
 53: // other targets. This is because all branches, including ones that jump
 54: // within the same section, are explicitly expressed with relocations.
 55: // Here is why we need them: all control-flow statements such as `if` or
 56: // `for` are implemented using branch instructions. For other targets, the
 57: // compiler doesn't emit relocations for such branches because they know
 58: // at compile-time exactly how many bytes has to be skipped. That's not
 59: // true to RISC-V because the linker may delete bytes between a branch and
 60: // its destination. Therefore, all branches including in-section ones have
 61: // to be explicitly expressed with relocations.
 62: //
 63: // Note that this mechanism only shrink sections and never enlarge, as
 64: // the compiler always emits the longest instruction sequence. This
 65: // makes the linker implementation a bit simpler because we don't need
 66: // to worry about oscillation.
 67: //
 68: // https://github.com/riscv-non-isa/riscv-elf-psabi-doc/blob/master/riscv-elf.adoc
 69: 
 70: #include "mold.h"
 71: 
 72: #include <tbb/parallel_for.h>
 73: #include <tbb/parallel_for_each.h>
 74: 
 75: namespace mold::elf {
 76: 
 77: static u32 itype(u32 val) {
 78:   return val << 20;
 79: }
 80: 
 81: static u32 stype(u32 val) {
 82:   return bits(val, 11, 5) << 25 | bits(val, 4, 0) << 7;
 83: }
 84: 
 85: static u32 btype(u32 val) {
 86:   return bit(val, 12) << 31 | bits(val, 10, 5) << 25 |
 87:          bits(val, 4, 1) << 8 | bit(val, 11) << 7;
 88: }
 89: 
 90: static u32 utype(u32 val) {
 91:   // U-type instructions are used in combination with I-type
 92:   // instructions. U-type insn sets an immediate to the upper 20-bits
 93:   // of a register. I-type insn sign-extends a 12-bits immediate and
 94:   // adds it to a register value to construct a complete value. 0x800
 95:   // is added here to compensate for the sign-extension.
 96:   return (val + 0x800) & 0xffff'f000;
 97: }
 98: 
 99: static u32 jtype(u32 val) {
100:   return bit(val, 20) << 31 | bits(val, 10, 1)  << 21 |
101:          bit(val, 11) << 20 | bits(val, 19, 12) << 12;
102: }
103: 
104: static u32 cbtype(u32 val) {
105:   return bit(val, 8) << 12 | bit(val, 4) << 11 | bit(val, 3) << 10 |
106:          bit(val, 7) << 6  | bit(val, 6) << 5  | bit(val, 2) << 4  |
107:          bit(val, 1) << 3  | bit(val, 5) << 2;
108: }
109: 
110: static u32 cjtype(u32 val) {
111:   return bit(val, 11) << 12 | bit(val, 4)  << 11 | bit(val, 9) << 10 |
112:          bit(val, 8)  << 9  | bit(val, 10) << 8  | bit(val, 6) << 7  |
113:          bit(val, 7)  << 6  | bit(val, 3)  << 5  | bit(val, 2) << 4  |
114:          bit(val, 1)  << 3  | bit(val, 5)  << 2;
115: }
116: 
117: static void write_itype(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
118:   *(ul32 *)loc &= 0b000000'00000'11111'111'11111'1111111;
119:   *(ul32 *)loc |= itype(val);
120: }
121: 
122: static void write_stype(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
123:   *(ul32 *)loc &= 0b000000'11111'11111'111'00000'1111111;
124:   *(ul32 *)loc |= stype(val);
125: }
126: 
127: static void write_btype(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
128:   *(ul32 *)loc &= 0b000000'11111'11111'111'00000'1111111;
129:   *(ul32 *)loc |= btype(val);
130: }
131: 
132: static void write_utype(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
133:   *(ul32 *)loc &= 0b000000'00000'00000'000'11111'1111111;
134:   *(ul32 *)loc |= utype(val);
135: }
136: 
137: static void write_jtype(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
138:   *(ul32 *)loc &= 0b000000'00000'00000'000'11111'1111111;
139:   *(ul32 *)loc |= jtype(val);
140: }
141: 
142: static void write_cbtype(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
143:   *(ul16 *)loc &= 0b111'000'111'00000'11;
144:   *(ul16 *)loc |= cbtype(val);
145: }
146: 
147: static void write_cjtype(u8 *loc, u32 val) {
148:   *(ul16 *)loc &= 0b111'00000000000'11;
149:   *(ul16 *)loc |= cjtype(val);
150: }
151: 
152: // Returns the rd register of an R/I/U/J-type instruction.
153: static u32 get_rd(u32 val) {
154:   return bits(val, 11, 7);
155: }
156: 
157: static void set_rs1(u8 *loc, u32 rs1) {
158:   assert(rs1 < 32);
159:   *(ul32 *)loc &= 0b111111'11111'00000'111'11111'1111111;
160:   *(ul32 *)loc |= rs1 << 15;
161: }
162: 
163: template <typename E>
164: void write_plt_header(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf) {
165:   static const ul32 insn_64[] = {
166:     0x0000'0397, // auipc  t2, %pcrel_hi(.got.plt)
167:     0x41c3'0333, // sub    t1, t1, t3               # .plt entry + hdr + 12
168:     0x0003'be03, // ld     t3, %pcrel_lo(1b)(t2)    # _dl_runtime_resolve
169:     0xfd43'0313, // addi   t1, t1, -44              # .plt entry
170:     0x0003'8293, // addi   t0, t2, %pcrel_lo(1b)    # &.got.plt
171:     0x0013'5313, // srli   t1, t1, 1                # .plt entry offset
172:     0x0082'b283, // ld     t0, 8(t0)                # link map
173:     0x000e'0067, // jr     t3
174:   };
175: 
176:   static const ul32 insn_32[] = {
177:     0x0000'0397, // auipc  t2, %pcrel_hi(.got.plt)
178:     0x41c3'0333, // sub    t1, t1, t3               # .plt entry + hdr + 12
179:     0x0003'ae03, // lw     t3, %pcrel_lo(1b)(t2)    # _dl_runtime_resolve
180:     0xfd43'0313, // addi   t1, t1, -44              # .plt entry
181:     0x0003'8293, // addi   t0, t2, %pcrel_lo(1b)    # &.got.plt
182:     0x0023'5313, // srli   t1, t1, 2                # .plt entry offset
183:     0x0042'a283, // lw     t0, 4(t0)                # link map
184:     0x000e'0067, // jr     t3
185:   };
186: 
187:   if constexpr (E::is_64)
188:     memcpy(buf, insn_64, sizeof(insn_64));
189:   else
190:     memcpy(buf, insn_32, sizeof(insn_32));
191: 
192:   u64 gotplt = ctx.gotplt->shdr.sh_addr;
193:   u64 plt = ctx.plt->shdr.sh_addr;
194:   write_utype(buf, gotplt - plt);
195:   write_itype(buf + 8, gotplt - plt);
196:   write_itype(buf + 16, gotplt - plt);
197: }
198: 
199: static const ul32 plt_entry_64[] = {
200:   0x0000'0e17, // auipc   t3, %pcrel_hi(function@.got.plt)
201:   0x000e'3e03, // ld      t3, %pcrel_lo(1b)(t3)
202:   0x000e'0367, // jalr    t1, t3
203:   0x0000'0013, // nop
204: };
205: 
206: static const ul32 plt_entry_32[] = {
207:   0x0000'0e17, // auipc   t3, %pcrel_hi(function@.got.plt)
208:   0x000e'2e03, // lw      t3, %pcrel_lo(1b)(t3)
209:   0x000e'0367, // jalr    t1, t3
210:   0x0000'0013, // nop
211: };
212: 
213: template <typename E>
214: void write_plt_entry(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf, Symbol<E> &sym) {
215:   if constexpr (E::is_64)
216:     memcpy(buf, plt_entry_64, sizeof(plt_entry_64));
217:   else
218:     memcpy(buf, plt_entry_32, sizeof(plt_entry_32));
219: 
220:   u64 gotplt = sym.get_gotplt_addr(ctx);
221:   u64 plt = sym.get_plt_addr(ctx);
222:   write_utype(buf, gotplt - plt);
223:   write_itype(buf + 4, gotplt - plt);
224: }
225: 
226: template <typename E>
227: void write_pltgot_entry(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf, Symbol<E> &sym) {
228:   if constexpr (E::is_64)
229:     memcpy(buf, plt_entry_64, sizeof(plt_entry_64));
230:   else
231:     memcpy(buf, plt_entry_32, sizeof(plt_entry_32));
232: 
233:   u64 got = sym.get_got_addr(ctx);
234:   u64 plt = sym.get_plt_addr(ctx);
235:   write_utype(buf, got - plt);
236:   write_itype(buf + 4, got - plt);
237: }
238: 
239: template <typename E>
240: void EhFrameSection<E>::apply_reloc(Context<E> &ctx, const ElfRel<E> &rel,
241:                                     u64 offset, u64 val) {
242:   u8 *loc = ctx.buf + this->shdr.sh_offset + offset;
243: 
244:   switch (rel.r_type) {
245:   case R_NONE:
246:     break;
247:   case R_RISCV_ADD32:
248:     *(U32<E> *)loc += val;
249:     break;
250:   case R_RISCV_SUB8:
251:     *loc -= val;
252:     break;
253:   case R_RISCV_SUB16:
254:     *(U16<E> *)loc -= val;
255:     break;
256:   case R_RISCV_SUB32:
257:     *(U32<E> *)loc -= val;
258:     break;
259:   case R_RISCV_SUB6:
260:     *loc = (*loc & 0b1100'0000) | ((*loc - val) & 0b0011'1111);
261:     break;
262:   case R_RISCV_SET6:
263:     *loc = (*loc & 0b1100'0000) | (val & 0b0011'1111);
264:     break;
265:   case R_RISCV_SET8:
266:     *loc = val;
267:     break;
268:   case R_RISCV_SET16:
269:     *(U16<E> *)loc = val;
270:     break;
271:   case R_RISCV_SET32:
272:     *(U32<E> *)loc = val;
273:     break;
274:   case R_RISCV_32_PCREL:
275:     *(U32<E> *)loc = val - this->shdr.sh_addr - offset;
276:     break;
277:   default:
278:     Fatal(ctx) << "unsupported relocation in .eh_frame: " << rel;
279:   }
280: }
281: 
282: template <typename E>
283: void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_alloc(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *base) {
284:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
285: 
286:   ElfRel<E> *dynrel = nullptr;
287:   if (ctx.reldyn)
288:     dynrel = (ElfRel<E> *)(ctx.buf + ctx.reldyn->shdr.sh_offset +
289:                            file.reldyn_offset + this->reldyn_offset);
290: 
291:   auto get_r_delta = [&](i64 idx) {
292:     return extra.r_deltas.empty() ? 0 : extra.r_deltas[idx];
293:   };
294: 
295:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
296:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
297:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE || rel.r_type == R_RISCV_RELAX)
298:       continue;
299: 
300:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
301:     i64 r_offset = rel.r_offset - get_r_delta(i);
302:     i64 removed_bytes = get_r_delta(i + 1) - get_r_delta(i);
303:     u8 *loc = base + r_offset;
304: 
305:     auto check = [&](i64 val, i64 lo, i64 hi) {
306:       if (val < lo || hi <= val)
307:         Error(ctx) << *this << ": relocation " << rel << " against "
308:                    << sym << " out of range: " << val << " is not in ["
309:                    << lo << ", " << hi << ")";
310:     };
311: 
312:     u64 S = sym.get_addr(ctx);
313:     u64 A = rel.r_addend;
314:     u64 P = get_addr() + r_offset;
315:     u64 G = sym.get_got_idx(ctx) * sizeof(Word<E>);
316:     u64 GOT = ctx.got->shdr.sh_addr;
317: 
318:     switch (rel.r_type) {
319:     case R_RISCV_32:
320:       if constexpr (E::is_64)
321:         *(U32<E> *)loc = S + A;
322:       else
323:         apply_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel, loc, S, A, P, dynrel);
324:       break;
325:     case R_RISCV_64:
326:       assert(E::is_64);
327:       apply_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel, loc, S, A, P, dynrel);
328:       break;
329:     case R_RISCV_BRANCH:
330:       check(S + A - P, -(1 << 12), 1 << 12);
331:       write_btype(loc, S + A - P);
332:       break;
333:     case R_RISCV_JAL:
334:       check(S + A - P, -(1 << 20), 1 << 20);
335:       write_jtype(loc, S + A - P);
336:       break;
337:     case R_RISCV_CALL:
338:     case R_RISCV_CALL_PLT: {
339:       u32 rd = get_rd(*(ul32 *)(contents.data() + rel.r_offset + 4));
340: 
341:       if (removed_bytes == 4) {
342:         // auipc + jalr -> jal
343:         *(ul32 *)loc = (rd << 7) | 0b1101111;
344:         write_jtype(loc, S + A - P);
345:       } else if (removed_bytes == 6 && rd == 0) {
346:         // auipc + jalr -> c.j
347:         *(ul16 *)loc = 0b101'00000000000'01;
348:         write_cjtype(loc, S + A - P);
349:       } else if (removed_bytes == 6 && rd == 1) {
350:         // auipc + jalr -> c.jal
351:         assert(!E::is_64);
352:         *(ul16 *)loc = 0b001'00000000000'01;
353:         write_cjtype(loc, S + A - P);
354:       } else {
355:         assert(removed_bytes == 0);
356:         // Calling an undefined weak symbol does not make sense.
357:         // We make such call into an infinite loop. This should
358:         // help debugging of a faulty program.
359:         u64 val = sym.esym().is_undef_weak() ? 0 : S + A - P;
360:         check(val, -(1LL << 31), 1LL << 31);
361:         write_utype(loc, val);
362:         write_itype(loc + 4, val);
363:       }
364:       break;
365:     }
366:     case R_RISCV_GOT_HI20:
367:       *(ul32 *)loc = G + GOT + A - P;
368:       break;
369:     case R_RISCV_TLS_GOT_HI20:
370:       *(ul32 *)loc = sym.get_gottp_addr(ctx) + A - P;
371:       break;
372:     case R_RISCV_TLS_GD_HI20:
373:       *(ul32 *)loc = sym.get_tlsgd_addr(ctx) + A - P;
374:       break;
375:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_HI20:
376:       *(ul32 *)loc = S + A - P;
377:       break;
378:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_LO12_I:
379:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_LO12_S:
380:       // These relocations are handled in the next loop.
381:       break;
382:     case R_RISCV_HI20:
383:       assert(removed_bytes == 0 || removed_bytes == 4);
384:       if (removed_bytes == 0) {
385:         check(S + A, -(1LL << 31), 1LL << 31);
386:         write_utype(loc, S + A);
387:       }
388:       break;
389:     case R_RISCV_LO12_I:
390:     case R_RISCV_LO12_S:
391:       if (rel.r_type == R_RISCV_LO12_I)
392:         write_itype(loc, S + A);
393:       else
394:         write_stype(loc, S + A);
395: 
396:       // Rewrite `lw t1, 0(t0)` with `lw t1, 0(x0)` if the address is
397:       // accessible relative to the zero register. If the upper 20 bits
398:       // are all zero, the corresponding LUI might have been removed.
399:       if (bits(S + A, 31, 12) == 0)
400:         set_rs1(loc, 0);
401:       break;
402:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_HI20:
403:       assert(removed_bytes == 0 || removed_bytes == 4);
404:       if (removed_bytes == 0)
405:         write_utype(loc, S + A - ctx.tp_addr);
406:       break;
407:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_ADD:
408:       // This relocation just annotates an ADD instruction that can be
409:       // removed when a TPREL is relaxed. No value is needed to be
410:       // written.
411:       assert(removed_bytes == 0 || removed_bytes == 4);
412:       break;
413:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_LO12_I:
414:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_LO12_S: {
415:       i64 val = S + A - ctx.tp_addr;
416:       if (rel.r_type == R_RISCV_TPREL_LO12_I)
417:         write_itype(loc, val);
418:       else
419:         write_stype(loc, val);
420: 
421:       // Rewrite `lw t1, 0(t0)` with `lw t1, 0(tp)` if the address is
422:       // directly accessible using tp. tp is x4.
423:       if (sign_extend(val, 11) == val)
424:         set_rs1(loc, 4);
425:       break;
426:     }
427:     case R_RISCV_ADD8:
428:       loc += S + A;
429:       break;
430:     case R_RISCV_ADD16:
431:       *(U16<E> *)loc += S + A;
432:       break;
433:     case R_RISCV_ADD32:
434:       *(U32<E> *)loc += S + A;
435:       break;
436:     case R_RISCV_ADD64:
437:       *(U64<E> *)loc += S + A;
438:       break;
439:     case R_RISCV_SUB8:
440:       loc -= S + A;
441:       break;
442:     case R_RISCV_SUB16:
443:       *(U16<E> *)loc -= S + A;
444:       break;
445:     case R_RISCV_SUB32:
446:       *(U32<E> *)loc -= S + A;
447:       break;
448:     case R_RISCV_SUB64:
449:       *(U64<E> *)loc -= S + A;
450:       break;
451:     case R_RISCV_ALIGN: {
452:       // A R_RISCV_ALIGN is followed by a NOP sequence. We need to remove
453:       // zero or more bytes so that the instruction after R_RISCV_ALIGN is
454:       // aligned to a given alignment boundary.
455:       //
456:       // We need to guarantee that the NOP sequence is valid after byte
457:       // removal (e.g. we can't remove the first 2 bytes of a 4-byte NOP).
458:       // For the sake of simplicity, we always rewrite the entire NOP sequence.
459:       i64 padding_bytes = rel.r_addend - removed_bytes;
460:       assert((padding_bytes & 1) == 0);
461: 
462:       i64 i = 0;
463:       for (; i <= padding_bytes - 4; i += 4)
464:         *(ul32 *)(loc + i) = 0x0000'0013; // nop
465:       if (i < padding_bytes)
466:         *(ul16 *)(loc + i) = 0x0001;      // c.nop
467:       break;
468:     }
469:     case R_RISCV_RVC_BRANCH:
470:       check(S + A - P, -(1 << 8), 1 << 8);
471:       write_cbtype(loc, S + A - P);
472:       break;
473:     case R_RISCV_RVC_JUMP:
474:       check(S + A - P, -(1 << 11), 1 << 11);
475:       write_cjtype(loc, S + A - P);
476:       break;
477:     case R_RISCV_SUB6:
478:       *loc = (*loc & 0b1100'0000) | ((*loc - (S + A)) & 0b0011'1111);
479:       break;
480:     case R_RISCV_SET6:
481:       *loc = (*loc & 0b1100'0000) | ((S + A) & 0b0011'1111);
482:       break;
483:     case R_RISCV_SET8:
484:       *loc = S + A;
485:       break;
486:     case R_RISCV_SET16:
487:       *(U16<E> *)loc = S + A;
488:       break;
489:     case R_RISCV_SET32:
490:       *(U32<E> *)loc = S + A;
491:       break;
492:     case R_RISCV_PLT32:
493:     case R_RISCV_32_PCREL:
494:       *(U32<E> *)loc = S + A - P;
495:       break;
496:     default:
497:       unreachable();
498:     }
499:   }
500: 
501:   // Handle PC-relative LO12 relocations. In the above loop, pcrel HI20
502:   // relocations overwrote instructions with full 32-bit values to allow
503:   // their corresponding pcrel LO12 relocations to read their values.
504:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
505:     switch (rels[i].r_type) {
506:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_LO12_I:
507:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_LO12_S: {
508:       Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rels[i].r_sym];
509:       assert(sym.get_input_section() == this);
510: 
511:       u8 *loc = base + rels[i].r_offset - get_r_delta(i);
512:       u32 val = *(ul32 *)(base + sym.value);
513: 
514:       if (rels[i].r_type == R_RISCV_PCREL_LO12_I)
515:         write_itype(loc, val);
516:       else
517:         write_stype(loc, val);
518:     }
519:     }
520:   }
521: 
522:   // Restore the original instructions pcrel HI20 relocations overwrote.
523:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
524:     switch (rels[i].r_type) {
525:     case R_RISCV_GOT_HI20:
526:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_HI20:
527:     case R_RISCV_TLS_GOT_HI20:
528:     case R_RISCV_TLS_GD_HI20: {
529:       u8 *loc = base + rels[i].r_offset - get_r_delta(i);
530:       u32 val = *(ul32 *)loc;
531:       memcpy(loc, contents.data() + rels[i].r_offset, 4);
532:       write_utype(loc, val);
533:     }
534:     }
535:   }
536: }
537: 
538: template <typename E>
539: void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_nonalloc(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *base) {
540:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
541: 
542:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
543:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
544:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE || record_undef_error(ctx, rel))
545:       continue;
546: 
547:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
548:     u8 *loc = base + rel.r_offset;
549: 
550:     SectionFragment<E> *frag;
551:     i64 frag_addend;
552:     std::tie(frag, frag_addend) = get_fragment(ctx, rel);
553: 
554:     u64 S = frag ? frag->get_addr(ctx) : sym.get_addr(ctx);
555:     u64 A = frag ? frag_addend : (i64)rel.r_addend;
556: 
557:     switch (rel.r_type) {
558:     case R_RISCV_32:
559:       *(U32<E> *)loc = S + A;
560:       break;
561:     case R_RISCV_64:
562:       if (std::optional<u64> val = get_tombstone(sym, frag))
563:         *(U64<E> *)loc = *val;
564:       else
565:         *(U64<E> *)loc = S + A;
566:       break;
567:     case R_RISCV_ADD8:
568:       *loc += S + A;
569:       break;
570:     case R_RISCV_ADD16:
571:       *(U16<E> *)loc += S + A;
572:       break;
573:     case R_RISCV_ADD32:
574:       *(U32<E> *)loc += S + A;
575:       break;
576:     case R_RISCV_ADD64:
577:       *(U64<E> *)loc += S + A;
578:       break;
579:     case R_RISCV_SUB8:
580:       *loc -= S + A;
581:       break;
582:     case R_RISCV_SUB16:
583:       *(U16<E> *)loc -= S + A;
584:       break;
585:     case R_RISCV_SUB32:
586:       *(U32<E> *)loc -= S + A;
587:       break;
588:     case R_RISCV_SUB64:
589:       *(U64<E> *)loc -= S + A;
590:       break;
591:     case R_RISCV_SUB6:
592:       *loc = (*loc & 0b1100'0000) | ((*loc - (S + A)) & 0b0011'1111);
593:       break;
594:     case R_RISCV_SET6:
595:       *loc = (*loc & 0b1100'0000) | ((S + A) & 0b0011'1111);
596:       break;
597:     case R_RISCV_SET8:
598:       *loc = S + A;
599:       break;
600:     case R_RISCV_SET16:
601:       *(U16<E> *)loc = S + A;
602:       break;
603:     case R_RISCV_SET32:
604:       *(U32<E> *)loc = S + A;
605:       break;
606:     default:
607:       Fatal(ctx) << *this << ": invalid relocation for non-allocated sections: "
608:                  << rel;
609:       break;
610:     }
611:   }
612: }
613: 
614: template <typename E>
615: void InputSection<E>::copy_contents_riscv(Context<E> &ctx, u8 *buf) {
616:   // If a section is not relaxed, we can copy it as a one big chunk.
617:   if (extra.r_deltas.empty()) {
618:     uncompress_to(ctx, buf);
619:     return;
620:   }
621: 
622:   // A relaxed section is copied piece-wise.
623:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
624:   i64 pos = 0;
625: 
626:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
627:     i64 delta = extra.r_deltas[i + 1] - extra.r_deltas[i];
628:     if (delta == 0)
629:       continue;
630:     assert(delta > 0);
631: 
632:     const ElfRel<E> &r = rels[i];
633:     memcpy(buf, contents.data() + pos, r.r_offset - pos);
634:     buf += r.r_offset - pos;
635:     pos = r.r_offset + delta;
636:   }
637: 
638:   memcpy(buf, contents.data() + pos, contents.size() - pos);
639: }
640: 
641: template <typename E>
642: void InputSection<E>::scan_relocations(Context<E> &ctx) {
643:   assert(shdr().sh_flags & SHF_ALLOC);
644: 
645:   this->reldyn_offset = file.num_dynrel * sizeof(ElfRel<E>);
646:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = get_rels(ctx);
647: 
648:   // Scan relocations
649:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
650:     const ElfRel<E> &rel = rels[i];
651:     if (rel.r_type == R_NONE || record_undef_error(ctx, rel))
652:       continue;
653: 
654:     Symbol<E> &sym = *file.symbols[rel.r_sym];
655: 
656:     if (sym.is_ifunc())
657:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_GOT | NEEDS_PLT;
658: 
659:     switch (rel.r_type) {
660:     case R_RISCV_32:
661:       if constexpr (E::is_64)
662:         scan_absrel(ctx, sym, rel);
663:       else
664:         scan_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel);
665:       break;
666:     case R_RISCV_HI20:
667:       scan_absrel(ctx, sym, rel);
668:       break;
669:     case R_RISCV_64:
670:       if constexpr (!E::is_64)
671:         Fatal(ctx) << *this << ": R_RISCV_64 cannot be used on RV32";
672:       scan_dyn_absrel(ctx, sym, rel);
673:       break;
674:     case R_RISCV_CALL:
675:     case R_RISCV_CALL_PLT:
676:     case R_RISCV_PLT32:
677:       if (sym.is_imported)
678:         sym.flags |= NEEDS_PLT;
679:       break;
680:     case R_RISCV_GOT_HI20:
681:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_GOT;
682:       break;
683:     case R_RISCV_TLS_GOT_HI20:
684:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_GOTTP;
685:       break;
686:     case R_RISCV_TLS_GD_HI20:
687:       sym.flags |= NEEDS_TLSGD;
688:       break;
689:     case R_RISCV_32_PCREL:
690:       scan_pcrel(ctx, sym, rel);
691:       break;
692:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_HI20:
693:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_LO12_I:
694:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_LO12_S:
695:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_ADD:
696:       check_tlsle(ctx, sym, rel);
697:       break;
698:     case R_RISCV_BRANCH:
699:     case R_RISCV_JAL:
700:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_HI20:
701:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_LO12_I:
702:     case R_RISCV_PCREL_LO12_S:
703:     case R_RISCV_LO12_I:
704:     case R_RISCV_LO12_S:
705:     case R_RISCV_ADD8:
706:     case R_RISCV_ADD16:
707:     case R_RISCV_ADD32:
708:     case R_RISCV_ADD64:
709:     case R_RISCV_SUB8:
710:     case R_RISCV_SUB16:
711:     case R_RISCV_SUB32:
712:     case R_RISCV_SUB64:
713:     case R_RISCV_ALIGN:
714:     case R_RISCV_RVC_BRANCH:
715:     case R_RISCV_RVC_JUMP:
716:     case R_RISCV_RELAX:
717:     case R_RISCV_SUB6:
718:     case R_RISCV_SET6:
719:     case R_RISCV_SET8:
720:     case R_RISCV_SET16:
721:     case R_RISCV_SET32:
722:       break;
723:     default:
724:       Error(ctx) << *this << ": unknown relocation: " << rel;
725:     }
726:   }
727: }
728: 
729: template <typename E>
730: static bool is_resizable(Context<E> &ctx, InputSection<E> *isec) {
731:   return isec && isec->is_alive && (isec->shdr().sh_flags & SHF_ALLOC) &&
732:          (isec->shdr().sh_flags & SHF_EXECINSTR);
733: }
734: 
735: // Returns the distance between a relocated place and a symbol.
736: template <typename E>
737: static i64 compute_distance(Context<E> &ctx, Symbol<E> &sym,
738:                             InputSection<E> &isec, const ElfRel<E> &rel) {
739:   // We handle absolute symbols as if they were infinitely far away
740:   // because `shrink_section` may increase a distance between a branch
741:   // instruction and an absolute symbol. Branching to an absolute
742:   // location is extremely rare in real code, though.
743:   if (sym.is_absolute())
744:     return INT32_MAX;
745: 
746:   // Likewise, relocations against weak undefined symbols won't be relaxed.
747:   if (sym.esym().is_undef_weak())
748:     return INT32_MAX;
749: 
750:   // Compute a distance between the relocated place and the symbol.
751:   i64 S = sym.get_addr(ctx);
752:   i64 A = rel.r_addend;
753:   i64 P = isec.get_addr() + rel.r_offset;
754:   return S + A - P;
755: }
756: 
757: // Scan relocations to shrink sections.
758: template <typename E>
759: static void shrink_section(Context<E> &ctx, InputSection<E> &isec, bool use_rvc) {
760:   std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = isec.get_rels(ctx);
761:   isec.extra.r_deltas.resize(rels.size() + 1);
762: 
763:   i64 delta = 0;
764: 
765:   for (i64 i = 0; i < rels.size(); i++) {
766:     const ElfRel<E> &r = rels[i];
767:     Symbol<E> &sym = *isec.file.symbols[r.r_sym];
768:     isec.extra.r_deltas[i] = delta;
769: 
770:     // Handling R_RISCV_ALIGN is mandatory.
771:     //
772:     // R_RISCV_ALIGN refers to NOP instructions. We need to eliminate some
773:     // or all of the instructions so that the instruction that immediately
774:     // follows the NOPs is aligned to a specified alignment boundary.
775:     if (r.r_type == R_RISCV_ALIGN) {
776:       // The total bytes of NOPs is stored to r_addend, so the next
777:       // instruction is r_addend away.
778:       u64 loc = isec.get_addr() + r.r_offset - delta;
779:       u64 next_loc = loc + r.r_addend;
780:       u64 alignment = bit_ceil(r.r_addend + 1);
781:       assert(alignment <= (1 << isec.p2align));
782:       delta += next_loc - align_to(loc, alignment);
783:       continue;
784:     }
785: 
786:     // Handling other relocations is optional.
787:     if (!ctx.arg.relax || i == rels.size() - 1 ||
788:         rels[i + 1].r_type != R_RISCV_RELAX)
789:       continue;
790: 
791:     // Linker-synthesized symbols haven't been assigned their final
792:     // values when we are shrinking sections because actual values can
793:     // be computed only after we fix the file layout. Therefore, we
794:     // assume that relocations against such symbols are always
795:     // non-relaxable.
796:     if (sym.file == ctx.internal_obj)
797:       continue;
798: 
799:     switch (r.r_type) {
800:     case R_RISCV_CALL:
801:     case R_RISCV_CALL_PLT: {
802:       // These relocations refer to an AUIPC + JALR instruction pair to
803:       // allow to jump to anywhere in PC ± 2 GiB. If the jump target is
804:       // close enough to PC, we can use C.J, C.JAL or JAL instead.
805:       i64 dist = compute_distance(ctx, sym, isec, r);
806:       if (dist & 1)
807:         break;
808: 
809:       i64 rd = get_rd(*(ul32 *)(isec.contents.data() + r.r_offset + 4));
810: 
811:       if (rd == 0 && sign_extend(dist, 11) == dist && use_rvc) {
812:         // If rd is x0 and the jump target is within ±2 KiB, we can use
813:         // C.J, saving 6 bytes.
814:         delta += 6;
815:       } else if (rd == 1 && sign_extend(dist, 11) == dist && use_rvc && !E::is_64) {
816:         // If rd is x1 and the jump target is within ±2 KiB, we can use
817:         // C.JAL. This is RV32 only because C.JAL is RV32-only instruction.
818:         delta += 6;
819:       } else if (sign_extend(dist, 20) == dist) {
820:         // If the jump target is within ±1 MiB, we can use JAL.
821:         delta += 4;
822:       }
823:       break;
824:     }
825:     case R_RISCV_HI20:
826:       // If the upper 20 bits are all zero, we can remove LUI.
827:       // The corresponding instructions referred to by LO12_I/LO12_S
828:       // relocations will use the zero register instead.
829:       if (bits(sym.get_addr(ctx), 31, 12) == 0)
830:         delta += 4;
831:       break;
832:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_HI20:
833:     case R_RISCV_TPREL_ADD:
834:       // These relocations are used to add a high 20-bit value to the
835:       // thread pointer. The following two instructions materializes
836:       // TP + HI20(foo) in %r5, for example.
837:       //
838:       //  lui  a5,%tprel_hi(foo)         # R_RISCV_TPREL_HI20 (symbol)
839:       //  add  a5,a5,tp,%tprel_add(foo)  # R_RISCV_TPREL_ADD (symbol)
840:       //
841:       // Then thread-local variable `foo` is accessed with a low 12-bit
842:       // offset like this:
843:       //
844:       //  sw   t0,%tprel_lo(foo)(a5)     # R_RISCV_TPREL_LO12_S (symbol)
845:       //
846:       // However, if the variable is at TP ±2 KiB, TP + HI20(foo) is the
847:       // same as TP, so we can instead access the thread-local variable
848:       // directly using TP like this:
849:       //
850:       //  sw   t0,%tprel_lo(foo)(tp)
851:       //
852:       // Here, we remove `lui` and `add` if the offset is within ±2 KiB.
853:       if (i64 val = sym.get_addr(ctx) + r.r_addend - ctx.tp_addr;
854:           sign_extend(val, 11) == val)
855:         delta += 4;
856:       break;
857:     }
858:   }
859: 
860:   isec.extra.r_deltas[rels.size()] = delta;
861:   isec.sh_size -= delta;
862: }
863: 
864: // Shrink sections by interpreting relocations.
865: //
866: // This operation seems to be optional, because by default longest
867: // instructions are being used. However, calling this function is actually
868: // mandatory because of R_RISCV_ALIGN. R_RISCV_ALIGN is a directive to the
869: // linker to align the location referred to by the relocation to a
870: // specified byte boundary. We at least have to interpret them to satisfy
871: // the alignment constraints.
872: template <typename E>
873: i64 riscv_resize_sections(Context<E> &ctx) {
874:   Timer t(ctx, "riscv_resize_sections");
875: 
876:   // True if we can use the 2-byte instructions. This is usually true on
877:   // Unix because RV64GC is generally considered the baseline hardware.
878:   bool use_rvc = get_eflags(ctx) & EF_RISCV_RVC;
879: 
880:   // Find all the relocations that can be relaxed.
881:   // This step should only shrink sections.
882:   tbb::parallel_for_each(ctx.objs, [&](ObjectFile<E> *file) {
883:     for (std::unique_ptr<InputSection<E>> &isec : file->sections)
884:       if (is_resizable(ctx, isec.get()))
885:         shrink_section(ctx, *isec, use_rvc);
886:   });
887: 
888:   // Fix symbol values.
889:   tbb::parallel_for_each(ctx.objs, [&](ObjectFile<E> *file) {
890:     for (Symbol<E> *sym : file->symbols) {
891:       if (sym->file != file)
892:         continue;
893: 
894:       InputSection<E> *isec = sym->get_input_section();
895:       if (!isec || isec->extra.r_deltas.empty())
896:         continue;
897: 
898:       std::span<const ElfRel<E>> rels = isec->get_rels(ctx);
899:       auto it = std::lower_bound(rels.begin(), rels.end(), sym->value,
900:                                  [&](const ElfRel<E> &r, u64 val) {
901:         return r.r_offset < val;
902:       });
903: 
904:       sym->value -= isec->extra.r_deltas[it - rels.begin()];
905:     }
906:   });
907: 
908:   // Re-compute section offset again to finalize them.
909:   compute_section_sizes(ctx);
910:   return set_osec_offsets(ctx);
911: }
912: 
913: #define INSTANTIATE(E)                                                       \
914:   template void write_plt_header(Context<E> &, u8 *);                        \
915:   template void write_plt_entry(Context<E> &, u8 *, Symbol<E> &);            \
916:   template void write_pltgot_entry(Context<E> &, u8 *, Symbol<E> &);         \
917:   template void                                                              \
918:   EhFrameSection<E>::apply_reloc(Context<E> &, const ElfRel<E> &, u64, u64); \
919:   template void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_alloc(Context<E> &, u8 *);      \
920:   template void InputSection<E>::apply_reloc_nonalloc(Context<E> &, u8 *);   \
921:   template void InputSection<E>::copy_contents_riscv(Context<E> &, u8 *);    \
922:   template void InputSection<E>::scan_relocations(Context<E> &);             \
923:   template i64 riscv_resize_sections(Context<E> &);
924: 
925: INSTANTIATE(RV64LE);
926: INSTANTIATE(RV64BE);
927: INSTANTIATE(RV32LE);
928: INSTANTIATE(RV32BE);
929: 
930: } // namespace mold::elf

101: template <>
102: std::string rel_to_string<ARM64>(u32 r_type) {
103:   switch (r_type) {
104:   case R_AARCH64_NONE: return "R_AARCH64_NONE";
105:   case R_AARCH64_ABS64: return "R_AARCH64_ABS64";
106:   case R_AARCH64_ABS32: return "R_AARCH64_ABS32";
107:   case R_AARCH64_ABS16: return "R_AARCH64_ABS16";
108:   case R_AARCH64_PREL64: return "R_AARCH64_PREL64";
109:   case R_AARCH64_PREL32: return "R_AARCH64_PREL32";
110:   case R_AARCH64_PREL16: return "R_AARCH64_PREL16";
111:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G0: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G0";
112:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G0_NC: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G0_NC";
113:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G1: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G1";
114:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G1_NC: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G1_NC";
115:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G2: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G2";
116:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G2_NC: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G2_NC";
117:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G3: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_UABS_G3";
118:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_SABS_G0: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_SABS_G0";
119:   case R_AARCH64_MOVW_SABS_G1: return "R_AARCH64_MOVW_SABS_G1";
